title: The Mortal Who Refused to Be Expelled
content:
The Celestial Sword Sect's dismissal hall smelled of sandalwood and failure.

Ren Wei stood in the center of the polished jade floor, his forehead pressed against the cold stone in the deepest kowtow the sect allowed. The gesture was ceremonial now—he'd done it seventeen times in the last hour. Each time, the Elders on their elevated platform had watched with the same expression: mild inconvenience, like swatting a persistent fly.

"Disciple Ren Wei," Elder Huo announced, his voice carrying the weight of three centuries of cultivation, "your meridians have been assessed by the Grand Examination Array. The results are final."

Ren didn't look up. He'd memorized the grain pattern in the jade tiles. It looked like a river. A river that would never flow through his spiritual channels.

"Your cultivation base: Qi Gathering, third stage. Your age: nineteen. Your potential: limited."

*Limited.* Such a polite word for *worthless*.

"The Celestial Sword Sect cannot invest further resources in a disciple who will never reach Foundation Establishment. By the authority vested in this council, you are hereby—"

"I'll pay double."

The words escaped before Ren could stop them. He raised his head, meeting Elder Huo's startled gaze. The old man's beard twitched.

"Excuse me?"

"Double the contribution points. Triple, even." Ren's voice cracked, but he pressed on. "I know I'm not talented, but I work hard. I've memorized every manual in the Outer Library. I can identify seventeen types of spirit herbs by smell alone. I—"

"You can't cultivate," Elder Huo interrupted, his tone softening to something worse than anger: pity. "The Array has spoken. Your meridians are like cracked pots. No amount of qi will fill them. This is not a punishment, Disciple Ren. It is reality."

*Reality.* As if reality couldn't be changed. As if the world wasn't built by people stubborn enough to refuse their limitations.

Ren rose slowly, brushing the dust from his gray outer robes. The dismissal hall stretched around him, vast enough to hold five hundred disciples. Today, it held only him, seven Elders, and his shattered future.

"I understand," he said, his voice flat.

He didn't understand at all.

The sect gate closed behind him with a sound like a tombstone sealing.

Ren stood on the mountain path, his belongings tied in a cloth bundle: three changes of robes, a chipped jade pendant that held no spiritual power, and a scroll on basic formations he'd borrowed from the library six months ago. Technically, it was theft now. He didn't care.

The Celestial Sword Sect rose behind him, its pagodas piercing clouds that glowed with the ambient qi of a thousand cultivators meditating in harmony. Below, the mortal world stretched in muddy roads and small villages. A world where he'd be just another peasant with calloused hands and empty dantian.

"That's it?" he muttered to himself. "Nineteen years of training, and I'm back to farming turnips?"

The wind carried the scent of wild ginger from the forest below. Somewhere, a bird sang a mocking melody.

Ren's fist tightened around his bundle. His spiritual sense was weak, barely enough to sense qi at all, but he could still feel the sect's protective formation humming behind him. A formation designed to keep threats out—and failures like him in their proper place.

*Fine.*

He turned his back on the Celestial Sword Sect.

*If the mountain won't have me, I'll build my own mountain.*

---

Three days later, Ren realized that building a mountain was significantly harder than the inspirational metaphor suggested.

He'd walked forty-three miles south, following a road that grew progressively less maintained until it became a deer trail, then a suggestion, then nothing at all. His feet bled through his straw sandals. His last rice cake had dissolved into crumbs in his pocket. And the only thing he'd found was a temple so decrepit it looked like it was actively trying to collapse.

"Perfect," he said, his voice hoarse with thirst.

The temple sat in a clearing that had once been a courtyard, now overgrown with weeds that reached his waist. The main hall's roof had caved in on one side, exposing faded murals of celestial dragons to the elements. The wooden pillars were pitted with rot and insect holes. A stone plaque above the entrance bore characters so worn he could barely read them: *Temple of the—* something. The third character was completely gone.

Ren pushed open the door. It fell off its hinges.

Inside, the temple was worse. Rain had pooled on the dirt floor, creating a small ecosystem of moss and mushrooms. A broken statue of some forgotten immortal lay in pieces near the altar, its serene face cracked in half. The air smelled of wet stone, dead leaves, and despair.

*Perfect,* Ren thought again, and this time he almost believed it.

The Celestial Sword Sect had pristine training grounds, libraries filled with ancient texts, and meditation chambers lined with spirit stones. They had everything a cultivator could want.

This temple had nothing.

Which meant Ren had nothing to lose.

He dropped his bundle in the driest corner, which was still damp enough to soak through his robes in an hour. Then he sat cross-legged on the floor, closed his eyes, and attempted to cultivate.

The qi in this area was thin. Pathetic, really. Like trying to drink soup through a clogged straw. Ren's damaged meridians struggled to draw even a wisp of spiritual energy, and what little he captured leaked out almost immediately.

*Cracked pots,* Elder Huo had said.

Ren opened his eyes. "Right. First problem: location. This place has terrible qi density."

He stood, ignoring the protest from his blistered feet, and walked outside. The temple grounds stretched about two acres, bordered by a crumbling wall on three sides and a cliff on the fourth. Beyond the cliff, a valley spread out like a green carpet, dotted with the roofs of farming villages.

No spirit veins. No natural formations. No spiritual herbs growing wild.

"Second problem: no resources." Ren counted on his fingers. "Third problem: no disciples. Fourth problem: no reputation. Fifth problem: no money."

He paused.

"Actually, that's everything. I have nothing."

For a moment, the weight of his situation pressed down on him like a mountain. He was a failed cultivator in an abandoned temple in the middle of nowhere, with nothing but the clothes on his back and a stolen formations scroll.

Then he laughed.

It wasn't a sane laugh. It was the laugh of a man who had hit rock bottom and discovered that rock bottom came with an unexpected benefit: you couldn't fall any further.

"Fine," Ren said to the empty temple, to the uncaring sky, to the entire cultivation world that had written him off. "If I have nothing, then I'll build from nothing. That's a better story anyway."

He pulled out his formations scroll and unrolled it on a relatively clean patch of ground. The paper was yellowed, the characters written in careful brushstrokes that spoke of a master's hand.

*Basic Qi Gathering Formation,* the title read. *For use in areas of low spiritual density. Requires: formation ink, spirit stones (4), brush of medium quality, stable foundation.*

Ren looked at the list. He had none of those things.

An hour later, he'd gathered materials that were *almost* what he needed. Charcoal from the temple's old cooking fire served as ink. A sharpened stick replaced the brush. He'd found four smooth river stones that had no spiritual power whatsoever but looked nice. And the temple floor was dirt, which was technically a foundation, if not a stable one.

"Improvisation," he said, drawing the formation's outer circle with charcoal-stick precision. "That's what startups do."

The formation took three hours to complete. His back ached, his fingers were black with soot, and the design looked nothing like the elegant diagrams in the scroll. But when he placed the final river stone at the formation's center and pushed a thread of qi into the activation node—

*Something happened.*

The air stirred. Dust motes danced in the dim light filtering through the collapsed roof. And for just a moment, Ren felt the qi in the temple *move*, drawn toward his crude formation like water seeking the lowest point.

*It works,* he realized, his heart pounding. *My garbage, pathetic, barely-functional formation actually works.*

The feeling lasted three seconds before the river stone cracked, the charcoal lines smeared, and the formation collapsed in a puff of impotent energy.

But for those three seconds, Ren had felt it: possibility.

He was still grinning when the drunk stumbled through the doorway.

---

The man was a disaster in human form.

His robes were the color of old tea stains, patched in a dozen places with mismatched cloth. His hair hung in greasy tangles around a face that might have been handsome once, before a lifetime of alcohol had blurred its edges. A gourd of wine hung from his belt, and he clutched a sword so rusted it looked like it might crumble if he swung it too hard.

He took one look at Ren, one look at the failed formation, and said: "That's the worst formation I've ever seen."

"Thank you," Ren replied, because he wasn't sure how else to respond.

The drunk stumbled further into the temple, his movements uncoordinated in a way that seemed almost theatrical. He tripped over a loose stone, spun in an elaborate circle that somehow kept him upright, and collapsed onto a pile of debris near the altar.

"Nice place," he said, taking a long drink from his gourd. "Very... abandoned. Is it yours?"

"Just got it."

"Ah. New management." The drunk belched. "I'm Li Feng. Used to be the Sword Saint of the Azure Peaks. Now I'm just... Li Feng." He held out the gourd. "Want some?"

Ren stared at the offered wine. His mother had taught him never to accept drinks from strangers, especially strangers who claimed to be legendary sword saints while looking like they'd slept in a ditch.

"I'm Ren Wei," he said, ignoring the gourd. "Founder of the New Dao Sect."

Li Feng's bloodshot eyes widened. "New Dao Sect? Never heard of it."

"That's because I founded it approximately four hours ago."

For a long moment, the drunk just stared at him. Then he started laughing—a wheezing, hacking sound that turned into a coughing fit that nearly knocked him off his debris throne.

"You," Li Feng gasped, pointing a shaking finger at Ren, "are either the bravest man I've ever met or the stupidest."

"Both," Ren admitted. "I'm told it's a common trait among founders."

Li Feng's laughter died down into chuckles. He took another drink, studying Ren with eyes that suddenly seemed sharper than they had any right to be. "A sect, huh? What's your cultivation level?"

"Qi Gathering, third stage."

"Ha! That's barely above mortal."

"I know."

"And you want to start a sect? Compete with the Celestial Sword Sect, the Crimson Phoenix Palace, the—"

"I don't want to compete," Ren interrupted. "I want to build something better."

Li Feng tilted his head. "Better how?"

Ren thought about it. The Celestial Sword Sect had rejected him for his lack of talent. The cultivation world was built on hierarchies of natural ability, where those born with wide meridians and strong spiritual roots rose while those like him were cast aside.

"What if," Ren said slowly, "a sect didn't care about talent? What if it accepted anyone—the failures, the misfits, the people the old sects threw away—and taught them to succeed

title: The Business Plan in Blood
content:
The morning sun did nothing to warm the crumbling temple.

Ren stood in what had once been the main hall, staring at a roof that let in more light than it kept out. Three pillars remained standing. The fourth had collapsed sometime during the night, taking a chunk of the eastern wall with it. Dust motes danced in the golden shafts of sunlight, and somewhere in the rafters, something that sounded suspiciously like a nest of spirit rats squeaked in protest.

"Perfect," Ren muttered, pulling out a roll of parchment. "Absolutely perfect."

He'd spent the entire night awake, fueled by indignation and the last of his qi reserves. The Celestial Sword Sect had thrown him out like spoiled meat. Fine. Let them. While they were busy polishing their precious swords and bowing to elders who hadn't had an original thought in three centuries, Ren would build something that would make them choke on their own superiority.

The problem was that his new sect currently consisted of:

One collapsing temple.
Zero disciples.
A business plan written in blood. 
(His own blood. That part was important.)

He unrolled the parchment, revealing a mess of characters that looked like they'd been written by a drunk spider. The ink had smeared in places, and there was definitely a tea stain near the section labeled "REVENUE STREAMS."

"Step one: Recruitment," Ren read aloud, his voice echoing in the empty hall. "Step two: Secure funding. Step three: Establish sect reputation. Step four: Profit."

He stared at the plan.

It was terrible.

No, that was generous. It was catastrophic. He had no money, no connections, and the only thing his former sect had given him was a severance package consisting of exactly three low-grade spirit stones and a scroll that said "Better luck next life."

But here was the thing about being at rock bottom—there was nowhere to go but up.

"New Dao Sect," Ren said, testing the name on his tongue. It sounded ridiculous. A startup with no product, no team, and no capital. The celestial business world would laugh at him.

Let them laugh.

He was about to start sketching out a more detailed plan when the temple's front door—which was really just a wooden slab leaning against the entrance—crashed open.

Ren's hand went to his sword.

A figure stumbled through, coughing and waving away dust. It was a woman, maybe thirty years old, wearing robes that had once been fine silk but were now stained with what looked like wine, mud, and something that might have been blood. She carried a gourd on her hip and a sword on her back, and she was grinning like she'd just won a bet.

"Nice place," she said, her voice rough. "Very... rustic."

Ren didn't lower his hand. "Who are you?"

"Name's Li Mei." She bowed, slightly unsteady on her feet. "Former Sword Saint of the Azure Cloud Sect. Well, 'former' is doing a lot of work there. I was more of a Sword Apprentice, really. But Saint sounds better, don't you think?"

"You're drunk."

"Perceptive." Li Mei pulled the gourd from her hip and took a long swig. "Want some? It's not good wine, but it's wine."

"No."

"Suit yourself." She wiped her mouth with her sleeve and looked around the temple with an expression that was equal parts amusement and pity. "I heard there was a new sect starting up in these parts. Thought I'd check it out. See if there were any opportunities."

Ren's eyes narrowed. "How did you hear about it?"

"Word travels fast when a Celestial Sword Sect reject starts shouting about building his own empire." She shrugged. "Also, I was in the tavern next door when you got kicked out. Good show, by the way. Very dramatic."

Great. His reputation was already spreading.

"Why are you here?" Ren asked, keeping his voice level. "If you're looking for a cultivator sect, there are a dozen better ones within a day's travel."

"Better ones, sure." Li Mei took another drink. "But they all have rules. Discipline. Expectations." She made a face. "I don't do well with expectations."

Ren studied her. A drunk sword master who'd been kicked out of her own sect. On paper, she was exactly the kind of person he should avoid. Unreliable. Probably unstable. Definitely a liability.

But she was also a sword cultivator. And she was standing in his temple.

"What's your cultivation level?" he asked.

"Middle stage of the Core Formation realm. Was peak, but I had a bad year." She didn't elaborate, and Ren didn't ask.

It was still impressive. Core Formation was nothing to sneeze at. The Celestial Sword Sect's junior disciples were mostly at the Qi Condensation level. Having someone with real power, even diminished, could give his sect some legitimacy.

"What do you want?" Ren asked.

"A place to sleep. Some wine occasionally. And—" She paused, her grin fading slightly. "—a chance to prove that I'm not just a washed-up drunk."

There it was. The desperation hiding behind the bravado.

Ren unrolled his parchment again. "I can't pay you."

"I figured."

"I can't offer you resources or training."

"Also figured."

"I can offer you a share in the sect's future profits, a voice in major decisions, and the satisfaction of watching the old powers choke on their own arrogance."

Li Mei's eyebrows rose. "That's... surprisingly ambitious for someone whose temple is falling apart."

"Ambitious is the only thing I have left."

She was quiet for a long moment. Then she laughed—a genuine laugh, not the hollow one she'd been using before. "Fine. I'm in. What's the plan, sect leader?"

Ren felt something lighten in his chest. He had a disciple. One disciple. A drunk, disgraced sword cultivator who might stab him in the back for a bottle of decent rice wine.

But it was a start.

"Step one," Ren said, pointing to his parchment. "We need more people. I have a list of potential recruits—outcasts, rejects, people who've been failed by the system."

"Sounds like a collection of misfits."

"Exactly." Ren smiled, and it wasn't a pleasant smile. "The old sects only take the best. The talented. The connected. We'll take the ones they threw away and turn them into something they never expected."

Li Mei whistled. "That's either brilliant or suicidal."

"Probably both."

"Where do we start?"

Ren looked at his list. The first name was circled in red ink. "A formation expert. Name's Jiang Feng. Heard he got banned from the Heavenly Array Sect for gambling debts. Rumor has it he's been living in the Ghost Bamboo Forest, avoiding debt collectors."

"Ghost Bamboo Forest?" Li Mei shuddered. "That place is cursed."

"Perfect. He'll fit right in."

---

The Ghost Bamboo Forest lived up to its name.

The bamboo stalks were bone-white, swaying even when there was no wind. A faint mist clung to the ground, and every so often, Ren could swear he saw shapes moving in the corner of his vision—shadows that vanished when he turned to look.

"This is bad luck," Li Mei muttered, her hand on her sword. "Very bad luck. I can feel the resentful qi in the air."

"Keep moving."

They'd been walking for two hours, following a trail of discarded spirit stone fragments and empty wine bottles. Whoever Jiang Feng was, he had a drinking problem that rivaled Li Mei's.

Finally, they found him.

The formation expert was sitting in a small clearing, surrounded by what looked like the remains of a dozen failed arrays. Glowing lines of energy flickered and died around him as he hunched over a piece of parchment, muttering to himself.

"Three directional fluxes," he said, scratching his head. "No, that's wrong. Four. Or maybe it's five. Why does the universe hate me?"

He looked up as Ren and Li Mei approached. He was a thin man, maybe forty, with hollow cheeks and eyes that darted nervously. His robes were patched in a dozen places, and his fingers were stained with ink and something that smelled faintly of sulfur.

"Who are you?" Jiang Feng demanded. "If you're here to collect debts, I don't have any money. If you're here to kill me, please get in line."

"Neither," Ren said. "I'm here to offer you a job."

Jiang Feng blinked. "A job?"

"I'm starting a sect. I need a formation expert."

"You need—" Jiang Feng laughed, a hysterical edge to it. "Do you know who I am? I'm banned from every respectable sect in three provinces. I owe more spirit stones than I can count. The Heavenly Array Sect put a bounty on my head—fifty spirit stones, alive or dead. Fifty! I'm worth less than a decent sword!"

"Seventy," Ren said.

"What?"

"I'll pay you seventy spirit stones per month. Plus a share of sect profits."

Jiang Feng's mouth opened and closed. "But... but I'm a failure. I'm washed up. I'm—"

"You're a formation expert who reached the Array Master level before you were thirty," Ren interrupted. "I read your file. You designed an array that could power an entire city district. The Heavenly Array Sect didn't ban you because you were bad. They banned you because you were better than them, and they couldn't control you."

The words hung in the air.

Jiang Feng's expression shifted from confusion to something raw and vulnerable. "You... you know about that?"

"I know everything."

It was a lie. Ren had read exactly one report, and half of it was illegible. But it was a useful lie.

"Why would you want me?" Jiang Feng asked. "I'm a liability. I'm—"

"I'm a Celestial Sword Sect reject. My sword master here is a drunk who got kicked out of the Azure Cloud Sect." Ren gestured at Li Mei, who waved cheerfully. "We're all liabilities. That's the point. The old sects only want perfect pieces. We're building something from the broken ones."

Jiang Feng stared at him for a long moment. Then, slowly, a smile spread across his face.

"Seventy spirit stones?"

"Seventy."

"And I get to design whatever arrays I want?"

"Within reason."

"Deal." Jiang Feng scrambled to his feet and grabbed a bag that was absolutely overflowing with formation discs. "Where do I sign?"

---

They found the chef by accident.

They were walking back to the temple, discussing plans for recruitment, when they smelled it. Roasted meat. Spices. Something that made Ren's stomach growl so loudly that Li Mei burst out laughing.

"Someone's cooking," she said. "And they're good at it."

The source was a small camp near the road. A man in his fifties, with a round face and flour-dusted robes, was stirring a pot over a fire. He had the look of someone who'd been cooking for decades—efficient, practiced, completely at peace.

"Hello there!" the man called out, spotting them. "Hungry? I've got plenty."

Ren hesitated. Taking food from a stranger in the cultivation world was a good way to get poisoned.

But the smell was irresistible.

"Who are you?" Ren asked.

"Name's Chen Wei. Former head chef of the Golden Lotus Sect's kitchens." He smiled, but there was a sadness in his eyes. "Well, 'former' is the key word. They kicked me out last week."

"Why?"

Chen Wei's smile faltered. "I accidentally infused my cooking with qi. The elders said it

title: The Investor Who Wanted Everything
content:
The morning sun painted the crumbling temple walls in shades of gold and betrayal.

Ren stood in what he now called the "main hall" — a polite term for a room with half a roof and a suspicious number of bird nests — and stared at the letter in his hands. The paper was crisp, expensive, the kind that minor sect leaders used to pretend they were major sect leaders. The ink smelled of jasmine and desperation.

"Who delivered this?" he asked.

Li Chen, his first disciple and the only one who'd shown up before noon, pointed a thumb toward the entrance. "Some kid in green robes. Ran off before I could ask questions. Said it was from the 'Azure Pond Trading Consortium.'"

Ren's blood ran cold.

The Azure Pond. One of the three great trading consortiums of the cultivation world. They didn't just move spirit stones — they moved entire economies. If a sect wanted pills, weapons, or political influence, they went through Azure Pond. If Azure Pond wanted you gone, you disappeared so thoroughly that even your ancestors forgot you existed.

And they were writing to him.

He broke the seal and read.

*Ren Wei, Founder of New Dao Sect,*

*We have reviewed your preliminary business proposal with great interest. The concept of a "startup sect" operating outside traditional cultivation hierarchies presents... unique opportunities.*

*We would like to invite you to a preliminary investment meeting at the Azure Pavilion, Jade District, three days hence. Bring your financials, your projections, and your most persuasive arguments.*

*Success may change everything for you.*

*Failure will not be forgotten.*

*— Consortium Master Huo, Strategic Investments Division*

Ren read the letter three times. Then a fourth, just to make sure the words hadn't rearranged themselves into a threat.

"Boss?" Li Chen leaned over his shoulder, squinting. "Is that good or bad?"

"Both," Ren said slowly. "It's the kind of meeting that either launches a sect or buries it."

"So... good?"

"Li Chen, do you know what 'failure will not be forgotten' means in consortium-speak?"

"No."

"It means if I waste their time, they'll make sure I never cultivate again. They'll blacklist me from every pill seller, every weapon forger, every formation master within a thousand li. I'll be a mortal with a qi deficiency and a very impressive collection of debt."

Li Chen nodded thoughtfully. "So we should probably prepare."

"Prepare?" Ren laughed, the sound hollow in the empty hall. "Li Chen, we have three disciples, a roof that leaks, and exactly seventeen low-grade spirit stones to our name. Our 'financials' are written on a napkin stained with cooking oil. Our 'projections' are optimistic guesses based on the fact that I once read a book about economics."

"Seventeen spirit stones?" Li Chen's eyes widened. "That's... that's almost enough for a decent sword."

"It's almost enough for a decent meal at a roadside inn. It is not enough to impress a consortium that moves more wealth in a day than most sects see in a century."

Ren crumpled the letter, then smoothed it out again. He couldn't afford to be dramatic — the paper was too expensive to waste.

"We need help," he said finally. "Real help. The kind that comes with actual experience and maybe a few connections that don't involve drunk sword masters and gambling addicts."

"Should I fetch Senior Sister Mei?"

"Mei?" Ren blinked. "She's in the kitchen. Probably trying to figure out how to cultivate through soup again."

"No, I mean—" Li Chen pointed toward the back of the hall, where a rickety staircase led to the upper floor. "She's been up there for two hours. Said she was 'meditating on the nature of profit.'"

Ren climbed the stairs, each step creaking like a dying spirit beast. The upper floor was even worse than the main hall — three rooms, two of which had floors, and one that Mei had claimed as her "strategic planning chamber."

She was sitting cross-legged in the center of the room, surrounded by papers. Actual papers. Neatly stacked, organized by color, each one covered in precise handwriting.

"Mei?" Ren stepped inside. "What is all this?"

She looked up, and for a moment, Ren saw something he'd never seen in his first disciple's eyes before: focus. Pure, crystalline focus.

"I've been thinking," she said. "About our business model."

"Our business model is 'don't die' and 'maybe find a way to make money.'"

"No." Mei stood, brushing dust off her robes. "Our business model is *disruption*."

She grabbed a paper covered in diagrams and numbers. Ren recognized her handwriting — messy, chaotic, but somehow always arriving at the right answer.

"The old sects operate on a subscription model," she explained. "Pay for admission, pay for techniques, pay for protection, pay for advancement. Everything costs spirit stones. The bigger the sect, the more they charge, and the more they gatekeep."

"That's... how cultivation works."

"Yes. But it's inefficient." Mei's eyes gleamed. "Think about it. Most cultivators spend decades farming spirit stones just to afford the next breakthrough. They waste time on menial tasks when they could be cultivating. They're stuck in a system that extracts value instead of creating it."

Ren felt a chill run down his spine. "What are you proposing?"

"A marketplace. A platform. Instead of hoarding techniques and resources behind sect walls, we make them accessible. We charge a small fee for access, a percentage for transactions. We become the middleman — the *efficient* middleman."

"That's..."

"Insane? Yes." Mei grinned. "Also potentially revolutionary. If we can create a network where cultivators trade techniques, pills, and services freely — with New Dao Sect taking a small cut — we bypass the old power structures entirely. We become indispensable before they even realize what we're doing."

Ren stared at her. Then at the papers. Then back at her.

"Where did you learn this?"

Mei's grin faltered. "My family. Before... before I left."

She didn't elaborate, and Ren didn't push. Everyone in New Dao Sect had a past they didn't talk about. That was practically a hiring requirement.

"This is brilliant," he said slowly. "But it's also *dangerous*. The old sects won't just ignore us — they'll crush us. We're talking about the Celestial Sword Sect, the Crimson Lotus Palace, the entire established order."

"The established order is fragile," Mei said. "It's built on scarcity and control. If we offer abundance and freedom..."

"We become a target."

"Or we become the future."

Ren looked at the papers again. The numbers made sense. The logic was sound. The risk was astronomical.

"We need that investor meeting," he said finally. "But we don't go in empty-handed. We need something to show them. A proof of concept."

"What kind of proof?"

Ren thought fast. "Our first product. Something small, something we can actually deliver. A technique, a pill formula, a formation — something that proves New Dao Sect can create value."

"We have the chef's recipes," Li Chen said from the doorway. He'd followed them up without Ren noticing. "Senior Sister Fang's cooking. People already say it helps with qi circulation."

"Fang's cooking?" Ren blinked. "She's a chef. An accident cultivator. Her food is good, but it's not a technique."

"It's a *service*," Mei corrected. "And services have value. If we can prove that our methods produce consistent, measurable results — even from unconventional sources — that's proof of concept."

Ren paced the small room. Three disciples, seventeen spirit stones, and a business plan that could get them all killed. It was insane. It was impossible.

It felt right.

"Alright," he said. "Here's the plan. Mei, you're in charge of the proof of concept. I want a presentation ready in three days — something that shows Azure Pond exactly what we're building. Li Chen, you're on logistics. Find out everything you can about the Azure Pavilion, the Jade District, and Consortium Master Huo. I want to know his weaknesses, his preferences, and his deepest fears."

"And you, boss?" Li Chen asked.

Ren smiled, and it wasn't a kind smile. "I'm going to write a business plan so beautiful it makes the Celestial Sword Sect's treasury look like a beggar's bowl."

---

Three days passed like a blade being sharpened.

Ren barely slept. He wrote, rewrote, and burned more paper than he could afford. Mei worked on her presentation, muttering about "disruptive innovation" and "network effects" until even the drunk sword master started avoiding her. Li Chen gathered intelligence the only way he knew how — by drinking with information brokers and gambling with city guards.

By the morning of the meeting, Ren had a plan. A terrible, beautiful, probably-fatal plan.

They left the temple at dawn, the three of them walking through the misty streets of the mortal city below. The Azure Pavilion was in the Jade District, the richest part of the city, where spirit stones flowed like water and cultivators walked with the arrogance of gods.

The pavilion itself was a masterpiece of architectural cultivation. Jade walls that shimmered with embedded formations. A roof of golden tiles that redirected spiritual energy downward. Guards in matching armor who looked like they could kill a Qi Condensation cultivator with their pinky fingers.

Ren straightened his robes — his best set, which meant "the one with the fewest patches" — and walked through the entrance.

The interior was even more opulent. Silk carpets, crystal chandeliers, and a reception desk made from the wood of a thousand-year-old spirit tree. A woman in elegant blue robes sat behind it, her smile professional and empty.

"Ren Wei, New Dao Sect," he said, his voice steady despite his hammering heart. "I have a meeting with Consortium Master Huo."

The woman checked a jade tablet, nodded, and gestured toward a door on the left. "The consortium master will see you now. Your... associates may wait here."

Mei squeezed his arm. "Don't mess this up."

"Thanks for the vote of confidence."

"I mean it. If you mess this up, I'm going to use your corpse as fertilizer for my next business plan."

Ren walked through the door.

The room beyond was a study, lined with bookshelves that held actual tomes — not just cultivation manuals, but histories, philosophies, economics. A man sat behind a desk of polished obsidian, his face unreadable, his robes simple but clearly expensive.

Consortium Master Huo was not what Ren expected. He looked like a retired scholar, not a financial predator. Soft hands, gentle eyes, a faint smile that could mean anything.

"Ren Wei," Huo said, his voice smooth as silk. "Please, sit."

Ren sat. The chair was comfortable. That worried him.

"I've read your proposal," Huo continued. "Fascinating. A 'startup sect,' you call it. Operating outside traditional hierarchies. Using 'agile methodology' and 'minimum viable products.' Tell me, where did you learn these terms?"

"Books," Ren said honestly. "And experience. I worked for the Celestial Sword Sect for five years. I saw how they operate — the waste, the inefficiency, the way they crush innovation because it threatens their power. I thought there had to be a better way."

"And you believe you've found it?"

Ren met his eyes. "I believe I've found *a* way. Whether it's better depends on execution. That's why I'm here. New Dao Sect needs resources to scale. Azure Pond needs new investment opportunities. I think we can help each other."

title: Chapter 4
content:
CHAPTER 4: The Price of Ambition

Huo's smile didn't waver, but something shifted in his eyes. A predator's interest, carefully concealed behind scholarly gentleness.

"Bold words," Huo said, leaning back in his chair. "Most sect leaders who come to me spend the first hour praising my reputation and begging for scraps. You speak as an equal. I appreciate that, even if it's foolish."

"Foolish because I'm weak, or foolish because I'm honest?"

"Both." Huo picked up a jade brush and tapped it against the obsidian desk. The sound was sharp, precise. "Let me be equally honest with you, Ren Wei. I've investigated your 'New Dao Sect.' A crumbling temple in the mortal district. Three disciples — a drunk, a gambler, and a cook who accidentally fell into cultivation. You have no backing, no reputation, and no results worth mentioning."

Ren kept his face neutral. Inside, his stomach had turned to ice.

"And yet," Huo continued, "you've survived three months. You've retained those misfits. You've even completed a few minor commissions from the mortal city — pest extermination, spirit beast intimidation, the kind of work that makes true cultivators laugh." He set down the brush. "That's not nothing. Most failed sects collapse in the first month."

"You've done your research."

"I'm an investor. Research is what I do." Huo opened a drawer and pulled out a scroll, unfurling it across the desk. It was a contract, dense with tiny characters that seemed to shift and writhe as Ren looked at them. Formation-assisted legal text — the kind that could trap you in obligations you didn't even know you'd agreed to.

"My offer," Huo said. "Fifty thousand spirit stones, upfront. Access to Azure Pond's trading networks. A formation master to upgrade your temple's defenses. And a protective guarantee — no major sect will move against you for the first year."

Ren's heart stopped.

Fifty thousand spirit stones. That was enough to feed his sect for a decade. Enough to buy proper cultivation resources for his disciples. Enough to upgrade the temple from "condemned" to "respectable."

"And in exchange?" Ren asked, his voice carefully level.

Huo smiled. It was the smile of a man who held all the cards. "Seventy percent equity in New Dao Sect. Full voting rights on all major decisions for the first five years. And a right of first refusal on any future funding rounds."

Ren's brief hope crashed and burned.

Seventy percent. Huo wouldn't just own the sect — he *would be* the sect. Ren would be a figurehead, a manager, a puppet dancing on jade strings. Every decision would go through Huo. Every innovation would require his approval. New Dao Sect would become just another subsidiary of Azure Pond, its name a decoration, its purpose a tax write-off.

"That's not an investment," Ren said. "That's a takeover."

"It's a *valuation*." Huo's tone sharpened. "Your sect has no assets, no revenue, no track record. The only thing of value you possess is your idea — and ideas are cheap. Every beggar on the street has an idea. What I'm offering is the means to execute yours. In exchange, I take the majority of the risk, so I take the majority of the reward. That's how investment works."

"There's a difference between majority and total control."

"Is there? In my experience, control is control. The degree only matters to those who lose it."

Ren sat in silence, his mind racing. The contract was poison, but the alternative was worse. Without funding, New Dao Sect would stagnate. The temple would crumble. His disciples would leave. He'd be back where he started — a failed cultivator with nothing but dreams.

But seventy percent?

He thought of Mei, who had poured her entire savings into this venture. Of Chen, who had given up a comfortable position in a minor sect to follow Ren's crazy vision. Of old Zhang, who had nowhere else to go and believed in Ren despite all evidence to the contrary.

They hadn't invested in Azure Pond. They'd invested in *him*.

"No," Ren said.

Huo's eyebrows rose. "No?"

"No." Ren stood up. "Seventy percent is unacceptable. I came here to find a partner, not a master. New Dao Sect will succeed or fail on its own terms."

"Sit down, Ren Wei." Huo's voice was still calm, but there was steel underneath. "You're being emotional. That's the worst thing a founder can be."

"I'm being principled. There's a difference."

"Is there?" Huo leaned forward, his eyes narrowing. "Let me tell you what happens if you walk out that door. You'll return to your crumbling temple. Your disciples will see the hope die in your eyes. Your commissions will dry up as word spreads that Azure Pond rejected you. Within six months, your 'New Dao Sect' will be a footnote in someone else's failure story."

"Maybe."

"Not maybe. Certainly." Huo's voice dropped to a whisper. "I've been doing this for thirty years, Ren Wei. I've seen a hundred idealistic founders walk through that door. Ninety-nine of them took my offer. The one who didn't? I bought his sect from his creditors six months later, for ten percent of what I'd offered him originally."

Ren felt the weight of those words like a physical pressure. Huo wasn't bluffing. He was stating facts, the way a master cultivator stated that fire burned and water flowed.

"I'll take my chances," Ren said.

"You're making a mistake."

"Probably." Ren walked toward the door. "But it's *my* mistake to make."

He reached for the handle.

"One more thing," Huo said.

Ren paused.

"The Celestial Sword Sect has been asking questions about you. They seem... interested in your activities. I wonder how they'd react if they knew you were trying to build a rival organization."

Ren's blood ran cold. He turned back to face Huo. "That's a threat."

"It's a *fact*." Huo's smile was back, but it was sharper now, more like a blade than a greeting. "I'm simply pointing out that you have more enemies than friends, and I'm offering to be the most powerful friend you could have. The price is high, but so is the value."

"Friends don't demand seventy percent."

"Friends don't let friends die poor and forgotten." Huo spread his hands. "The offer stands for three days. After that, I'll assume you've made your choice. Good luck, Ren Wei. I genuinely hope I'm wrong about you."

Ren left without another word.

The walk back through the pavilion felt longer than the walk in. The opulence that had impressed him earlier now felt suffocating, a gilded cage designed to make poverty feel like a personal failure. He passed his disciples waiting in the reception area — Mei's sharp eyes, Chen's concerned frown — and shook his head once, a silent signal that carried everything they needed to know.

They didn't speak until they were outside, standing in the shadow of the Azure Pavilion's jade walls.

"He said no?" Mei asked, her voice flat.

"I said no."

"To what?"

"Seventy percent. Full voting control. Right of first refusal on everything."

Mei's face cycled through several emotions — anger, panic, grudging respect — before settling on a tight-lipped nod. "That's robbery."

"Robbery with a smile," Chen agreed. "What now?"

Ren looked up at the pavilion, at the golden tiles and shimmering formations, at the guards who pretended not to see them. He thought about Huo's threat — the Celestial Sword Sect, asking questions. He thought about his temple, his disciples, his impossible dream.

"Now," he said, "we get creative."

"Creative how?" Mei asked. "We have no money, no backing, and apparently a major sect that wants to crush us. Creativity isn't going to solve that."

"Creativity is the *only* thing that's going to solve that." Ren started walking, his disciples falling into step beside him. "Huo thinks he has us cornered. He thinks his offer is the only game in town. But he's wrong."

"Wrong how?"

"Because he's thinking like an investor. He's looking at what we *have*, not what we *can do*." Ren's mind was racing now, pieces clicking together like a formation activating. "We don't need his money. We need his network. And there's more than one way to access a network."

Mei grabbed his arm, stopping him. "Ren. What are you planning?"

He turned to face her, and for the first time since leaving the pavilion, he smiled. It was not a nice smile.

"We're going to start a bidding war."

"A bidding war?" Chen's eyes widened. "For what? We have nothing to sell."

"We have *potential*. And in the cultivation world, potential is the most dangerous currency there is." Ren looked up at the sky, where the sun was beginning to set, painting the clouds in shades of blood and gold. "Huo isn't the only investor in this city. He's just the biggest. But there are smaller players — merchants, minor clan heads, retired cultivators with wealth and no heirs. They don't have Huo's resources, but they also don't have his hunger for control."

"You want to pitch to *everyone*?" Mei asked.

"I want to make sure that by the time Huo's three-day deadline expires, he hears that every minor investor in the city is interested in New Dao Sect." Ren's smile widened. "Let's see how confident he is when he thinks he might lose us to a competitor."

"That's insane," Mei said.

"Probably."

"It might work," Chen said slowly. "If we can generate enough buzz, Huo might sweeten his offer. Or we might find a smaller investor who's willing to accept better terms."

"Or we might get killed by the Celestial Sword Sect before we can close any deal," Mei pointed out.

"That too." Ren shrugged. "But we were going to die poor and forgotten anyway. Might as well die interesting."

Mei stared at him for a long moment, then let out a laugh that was half frustration, half genuine amusement. "You're going to get us all killed."

"Probably," Ren said again. "But at least we'll have fun doing it."

They walked into the gathering dusk, three figures in patched robes, carrying nothing but ambition and desperation. Behind them, the Azure Pavilion gleamed like a jewel, oblivious to the storm that was about to break.

And in the shadows of a nearby alley, a figure in Celestial Sword Sect robes watched them go, a communication talisman already glowing in his hand.

The game had changed.

No one had told Ren that the pieces were already moving against him.

title: Chapter 5
content:
CHAPTER 5: The Fine Print

Huo leaned back in his chair, steepling his fingers. The gesture was calculated, Ren knew. Everything about this man was calculated.

"Help each other," Huo repeated, tasting the words. "An interesting framing. Most petitioners come to me begging. You're offering a partnership."

"I am."

"Then let me be direct." Huo pulled a scroll from his sleeve and unfurled it on the obsidian desk. Ren recognized his own handwriting—the proposal he'd spent three days perfecting, written in blood from a cut on his palm that Mei had insisted was "brand authenticity."

"The numbers are interesting," Huo continued. "Three disciples. A crumbling temple. Zero income. Yet you project profitability within two years based on 'discipleship subscriptions' and 'merchant partnerships.' Tell me, how do you plan to acquire your first paying disciples when you have no reputation, no backing, and no cultivation resources to offer?"

Ren had prepared for this question. "The traditional sects offer power. We offer opportunity. There are thousands of mortals in this city alone who will never qualify for a sect—too old, too weak, too poor. They'll spend their lives as servants or merchants, never touching cultivation. New Dao Sect gives them a path. A slow path, yes. But a real one."

"Interesting." Huo's eyes glittered. "And what do these mortals pay you with?"

"A percentage of their future earnings. For life."

Silence.

Huo's smile didn't waver, but something shifted in his expression. Respect, maybe. Or hunger. "You're not building a sect. You're building a debt network."

"I'm building a system where everyone wins. They get cultivation they couldn't otherwise access. We get sustainable revenue. The sect grows, their power grows, the city grows."

"And if they default?"

Ren met his gaze. "They won't. Because if they succeed, they pay us a fraction of their success. If they fail, we don't collect. It's aligned incentives."

Huo laughed. Not a polite laugh—a genuine one, sharp and surprised. "You've actually thought about this. Most young cultivators come in here with dreams of glory and a list of spirit stones they want me to hand over. You've built an *economic model*."

"Thank you."

"It's a terrible model, by the way. Your assumptions about retention rates are optimistic, your cost projections ignore cultivation resource inflation, and you haven't accounted for the inevitable sabotage from established sects who will see you as a threat."

Ren's heart sank, but he kept his face neutral. "Then why are you smiling?"

"Because terrible models can be fixed. Greedy, stupid, or lazy models cannot. Yours is ambitious and flawed. That I can work with."

Huo pulled out a second scroll, this one blank, and dipped a brush in ink. "I'll invest. But not on your terms. On mine."

"Name them."

"First: I want fifty-one percent equity in New Dao Sect."

Ren's blood went cold. "That gives you control."

"Yes. It does."

"I came here for a partnership, not a takeover."

"You came here because you're desperate." Huo's voice remained pleasant, but the edge was there now, sharp as any blade. "Your temple is crumbling. Your disciples are misfits. The Celestial Sword Sect has already marked you for destruction—I have informants, I know they've sent scouts to track your movements. You have no allies, no resources, and no time. I'm offering you a lifeline. The price is control."

Ren's mind raced. Mei's warning echoed in his ears. *He'll try to own you.*

"Fifty-one percent is too much," Ren said slowly. "But I'm willing to negotiate on board seats. Two for me, two for you, one independent arbiter."

"Three for me, two for you."

"No independent arbiter."

Huo tapped the brush against the desk. "Forty-nine percent. But I get veto power over any decision above ten thousand spirit stones."

"Twenty thousand."

"Fifteen."

"Done."

Huo wrote quickly, the brush flowing across the scroll. "Second condition: I want right of first refusal on any future disciples you recruit who show exceptional talent."

"Define exceptional."

"Anyone who reaches Qi Condensation within two years of joining."

Ren hesitated. That was a huge concession—it meant Huo could poach his best people before they ever contributed to the sect. But if he said no, the deal was dead.

"One year," Ren countered. "After they reach Qi Condensation, they stay with the sect for one year before you can make an offer. And the offer must match what we're offering them."

"Acceptable." Huo wrote more. "Third condition: I want regular reports. Monthly financial statements, quarterly progress reviews, and immediate notification of any major incidents."

"Standard investor terms. Agreed."

"Fourth condition." Huo paused, his brush hovering. "I want a seat on your founding board. Not just my representative—me personally."

Ren frowned. "That's unusual. Most consortium masters don't get involved at the operational level."

"Most consortium masters don't invest in suicidal startup sects run by ex-disciples with three misfits and a death wish." Huo's smile returned. "I want to watch this experiment up close. Besides, my expertise might be useful."

*Or you want to steer the ship into rocks if it suits you,* Ren thought. But he nodded. "Agreed."

Huo finished writing, then slid the scroll across the desk. "Read it. Carefully. Then sign."

Ren picked up the scroll and read. The terms were exactly as they'd discussed—equity split, veto rights, right of first refusal, reporting requirements. Standard investor protections. Nothing obviously hidden.

But something felt wrong.

He read it again. And again.

And then he saw it.

Buried in the definitions section, under "Force Majeure and Default Provisions":

*In the event that the New Dao Sect fails to achieve profitability within thirty-six months of this agreement, all assets, including but not limited to the sect temple, its spiritual formation, cultivation techniques, and intellectual property, shall be transferred to Azure Pond Consortium as compensation for investment. Furthermore, the founder (Ren Wei) shall be personally liable for any remaining debt, enforceable through the Heavenly Contract Court.*

Ren's hand trembled. "This says I lose everything if we fail in three years."

"Correct."

"That's not what we discussed."

"It's in the standard template." Huo's voice was calm. "I assumed you read the full document before coming."

"I did. Your assistant sent me a summary, not the full contract."

"Ah." Huo's smile widened. "An unfortunate misunderstanding. But you've already agreed to the terms verbally. And the contract is written. All that's left is your signature."

Ren stared at the scroll. The trap had been there all along, hidden in the fine print. Huo had played him perfectly—let him negotiate on the big numbers while burying the real poison in the definitions.

He could walk away. Refuse to sign. Leave with nothing but his pride.

But he'd die. The Celestial Sword Sect would hunt him down, the temple would collapse, his disciples would scatter. Mei would never forgive him. Li Chen would go back to his drinking. And the chef—what was her name?—would probably be fine, she could always find work, but the others...

"Can I negotiate this clause?"

Huo shook his head. "Non-negotiable. It's the only protection I have against total loss. You're asking me to invest in a venture with a ninety percent failure rate. I need insurance."

"Thirty-six months isn't enough. We're starting from nothing."

"You have three months of runway from my investment. Three years is generous."

Ren's jaw tightened. "I need a lawyer. Time to review."

"You can have both. But the offer expires at midnight tonight. If you don't sign by then, I withdraw entirely."

Ren looked at the scroll. At the elegant brushstrokes that promised salvation and damnation in equal measure. At the signature line, waiting for his mark.

"I need to consult my partners."

"Of course." Huo gestured toward the door. "Take all the time you need. But remember—midnight."

Ren stood, his legs unsteady. He walked out of the study, through the opulent reception area, past the guards who looked at him like he was already dead.

Mei was waiting by the entrance, pretending to examine a jade sculpture. She fell into step beside him as he walked out.

"How bad?"

"Worse than we thought."

They found Li Chen at a tavern three blocks away, nursing a cup of wine and glaring at a group of Celestial Sword Sect disciples who were laughing too loudly at the next table. Ren pulled him outside.

"What happened?" Li Chen asked, his hand drifting toward his sword.

"The contract has a death clause." Ren explained quickly. "Three years to profitability or we lose everything. Including me."

Li Chen's face darkened. "That's slavery."

"It's leverage. Huo wants us desperate, working twice as hard, taking risks we wouldn't normally take. If we succeed, he gets rich. If we fail, he gets our assets and a lifetime servant."

Mei paced in a tight circle, her mind clearly racing. "We could refuse. Find another investor."

"There isn't another. I checked. Every major consortium in the city is either owned by a sect or too conservative to touch us. Huo is the only one willing to take the risk."

"Then we don't take his money. We figure something else out."

"Like what?" Ren's voice cracked. "We have no income, no reputation, and the Celestial Sword Sect is already hunting us. Without capital, we're dead in months. With capital, we're dead in three years. The only difference is the timing."

Li Chen grabbed Ren's shoulder. "Then we fight. We take the deal, we make it work, and when we're strong enough, we break the contract."

"You can't break a Heavenly Contract. It's enforced by the laws of cultivation itself. Breaking it means losing your cultivation."

"Then we don't break it. We fulfill it." Mei stopped pacing. "Three years. It's not impossible. We just need to grow fast enough to hit profitability."

"Fast enough means risky. Dangerous. The kind of growth that attracts enemies."

"We already have enemies." Mei's eyes were hard. "We might as well have money too."

Ren looked at his two disciples. His friends. The only people in the world who believed in him.

"Midnight," he said. "I have until midnight to decide."

The three of them stood in the darkening street, the sounds of the city washing over them. Above, the moon was rising—a sliver of silver in a sky full of clouds.

Somewhere behind them, a Celestial Sword Sect scout was watching. Somewhere ahead, a consortium master was waiting. And somewhere in between, a decision that would shape the rest of their lives.

Ren took a deep breath.

"I need to think."

He walked away, leaving Mei and Li Chen standing in silence, the weight of the future pressing down on all of them.

title: Chapter 6
content:
CHAPTER 6: The Terms of Betrayal

Consortium Master Huo's smile didn't waver. It was the kind of smile that had been practiced in mirrors, refined over decades until it communicated exactly nothing. He folded his hands on the obsidian desk and leaned forward slightly.

"Your proposal is ambitious," Huo said, "but flawed in three critical ways."

Ren kept his face neutral. "I'd like to hear them."

"First, your valuation. You're asking for ten thousand spirit stones for fifteen percent of New Dao Sect. That values your sect at nearly seventy thousand spirit stones. For comparison, I could purchase an established third-rate sect for half that amount — disciples, buildings, cultivation techniques, and all."

Ren had expected this. "Those sects come with inherited problems. Bad blood with neighbors. Disciples trained in outdated methods. Elders who resist change. You're not paying for what we have now. You're paying for what we'll become."

"Eloquent," Huo said, "but unconvincing. The second flaw is your revenue model. You project profitability within eighteen months, yet your only current income source is a single client — a merchant who hired you to clear a spirit beast nest. One client does not make a sustainable business."

"We've secured two additional contracts since then," Ren said. "A minor noble family needs their estate's spiritual veins recalibrated, and a trading caravan wants formation protection for their next journey. Both agreed to pay in installments."

"Pre-payments?"

"Half upfront."

Huo's eyebrow twitched. The first crack in his composure. "Acceptable terms. But you still haven't addressed the third flaw — and the most important one."

Ren waited.

"You have no leverage," Huo said softly. "No backing from established powers. No reputation that precedes you. If I invest in your sect and you fail, I lose money. If I invest and you succeed, I gain influence — but I could also simply... take what I want. Your disciples. Your methods. Your location. What's to stop me?"

The room grew cold. Not metaphorically — the temperature actually dropped, frost forming on the edges of the windowpanes. Huo's spiritual pressure was subtle but unmistakable, a reminder that behind the scholar's facade was a cultivator of considerable power.

Ren had prepared for this question. He reached into his robe and pulled out a single sheet of paper, placing it on the desk between them.

Huo picked it up. His eyes moved across the characters, and slowly, the frost retreated.

"This is a binding oath," he said.

"Witnessed by the Heavenly Dao itself," Ren confirmed. "If you invest in New Dao Sect, you agree to the terms outlined in the contract. Any attempt to seize our assets through force or coercion will trigger the oath's backlash. Spiritual roots destroyed. Cultivation crippled. Death within three years."

"You would bind me to such terms?"

"I would bind *anyone* to such terms. Including myself. Look at the bottom."

Huo's eyes widened. There, in dried blood, was Ren's own signature, already sealed with a cultivation oath. The terms were identical — if Ren violated the contract's spirit, he would suffer the same consequences.

"You're insane," Huo said, but there was respect in his voice.

"Desperate," Ren corrected. "There's a difference. I've put everything into this sect. My savings. My reputation. My future. I can't afford to lose it to someone who sees me as prey. This oath ensures we're partners — equals — or we're nothing."

Huo set the paper down. He was quiet for a long moment, his fingers drumming against the obsidian desk.

"Ten thousand spirit stones," he finally said. "Fifteen percent equity. And a seat on your 'board of advisors,' with veto power over major decisions."

"Twenty percent of major decisions require unanimous consent from all board members," Ren said. "Veto only applies to existential threats — dissolution, sale, merger."

"Agreed."

"Then we have a deal."

Huo stood, extending his hand. Ren took it. The moment their palms touched, he felt the oath seal activate — a warmth that spread from his chest, connecting him to Huo, binding their fates together in the eyes of heaven.

"Pleasure doing business, Sect Leader Ren."

"The pleasure's mine, Consortium Master Huo."

Ren left the study feeling like he'd just walked through fire. His hands were shaking, but he forced them still as he rejoined Mei and Li Chen in the reception area.

Mei saw his face and understood immediately. "You did it."

"We did it," Ren said. "Ten thousand spirit stones. We're funded."

Li Chen raised an eyebrow. "What did you have to give up?"

"Fifteen percent and a board seat."

"That's not bad," Mei said, already calculating. "With that valuation, we can—"

"Later," Ren interrupted. "Let's get out of here first. I don't trust this place."

They walked through the jade halls of the Azure Pavilion, past guards who seemed to watch them a little too closely, past servants who bowed a little too deeply. The opulence felt oppressive now, like a trap waiting to spring.

Outside, the morning sun had burned through the mist, revealing the Jade District in all its glory. Spirit stone merchants hawked their wares from street stalls. Cultivators in expensive robes walked with purpose. Carriages pulled by spirit beasts clattered over cobblestone streets.

"Where to first?" Li Chen asked.

"We celebrate," Ren said. "Properly. Then we get back to work."

They found a modest teahouse — not the cheapest, but not the fanciest either. Ren ordered the best leaves they had and three plates of steamed buns. For a few hours, they were just three people who had somehow pulled off the impossible.

"To New Dao Sect," Mei said, raising her cup.

"To New Dao Sect," Ren and Li Chen echoed.

The tea was good. The buns were better. And for the first time in months, Ren felt like he could breathe.

That evening, they returned to the temple. The crumbling walls, the broken windows, the garden choked with weeds — it all looked different now. Not as a failure, but as a canvas.

"We need to prioritize repairs," Ren said, already planning. "The main hall first, then the dormitory wing. We can't take on more disciples until we have proper facilities."

"The formation array needs upgrading too," Mei added. "The current one barely keeps out wild animals, let alone rival cultivators."

Li Chen was quiet. He stood at the edge of the courtyard, staring into the darkness beyond the temple walls.

"Li Chen?" Ren called. "Something wrong?"

"I don't know," Li Chen said slowly. "Maybe nothing. But I've been feeling something all day. Like we're being watched."

Ren's blood went cold. "The Azure Pavilion?"

"Maybe. Or someone else. The information brokers were asking questions about us yesterday. More than usual."

"What kind of questions?"

"About our backgrounds. Our connections. Whether we had backing from any major sects." Li Chen turned to face them, his expression grim. "Someone's been digging. And they're not done yet."

Mei's hand went to her storage ring, where she kept her defensive talismans. "Should we leave?"

"No," Ren said firmly. "This is our home now. We stay, and we prepare."

They spent the next hour reinforcing the temple's defenses. Mei reactivated the formation array, channeling spirit stones into its core until it hummed with renewed energy. Li Chen patrolled the perimeter, his sword never leaving his hand.

Ren sat in the main hall, the contract from Huo spread before him. He read it again, looking for loopholes, hidden clauses, anything that could be used against them.

The terms were clean. Fair, even. But something niggled at the back of his mind.

*Why did he agree so easily?*

Ten thousand spirit stones was a lot, even for a consortium master. Huo had seemed genuinely interested in the pitch, but his concession had come too quickly. Too smoothly. As if he already knew what Ren would offer.

As if he'd planned this from the beginning.

Ren's eyes scanned the contract one more time. And then he saw it.

Buried in the fine print, almost invisible among the standard legal language:

*"In the event of default, all assets of New Dao Sect shall be transferred to Azure Pond Consortium for the purpose of debt recovery."*

Default. What counted as default?

He kept reading.

*"Default is defined as failure to meet any single term of this agreement, including but not limited to: payment schedules, performance milestones, or conduct standards as determined by the Board of Advisors."*

Determined by the Board of Advisors. Which Huo controlled.

Ren's hands trembled. He had been so focused on the big picture — the valuation, the equity, the veto power — that he had missed the trap hidden in the details.

If Huo wanted to destroy them, all he had to do was call a board meeting, declare them in default, and seize everything.

The binding oath prevented him from using force. But it didn't prevent him from using *law*.

"You bastard," Ren whispered. "You absolute bastard."

He looked up. Through the broken window of the main hall, he saw the moon rising over the mountains. Somewhere in the distance, a wolf howled.

And in the shadows beyond the temple walls, something moved.

Ren didn't sleep that night. He sat in the main hall, the contract in his hands, watching the darkness. Waiting for the trap to spring.

At dawn, three things happened simultaneously.

First, a messenger arrived from the Azure Pavilion. The first installment of the investment — three thousand spirit stones — had been delivered to a bank in the mortal city. Ren could collect it at his convenience.

Second, a scout from a nearby village reported strange activity in the mountains. Cultivators in black robes, moving with purpose. Too many to be random.

Third, the temple's formation array detected an intruder.

Not outside the walls.

Inside.

Ren drew his sword and ran toward the source of the alert — the meditation chamber at the heart of the temple. The door was ajar, a sliver of light escaping from within.

He kicked it open.

A woman stood in the center of the room. She wore plain robes, her face hidden beneath a hood. In her hand, she held a jade slip — one of the temple's oldest artifacts, a record of the founding techniques of the original sect.

"Who are you?" Ren demanded.

The woman lowered her hood.

Ren's blood turned to ice.

It was the receptionist from the Azure Pavilion. The one who had smiled at him with professional emptiness.

"Hello, Sect Leader Ren," she said, her voice completely different now — cold, sharp, dangerous. "I have a message from Consortium Master Huo."

"What message?"

"The first test has begun." She tossed the jade slip to him. He caught it reflexively. "Pass, and the investment continues. Fail..."

She didn't finish the sentence. She didn't need to.

The woman vanished, leaving only a faint trace of spiritual energy and the sound of her laughter echoing through the empty temple.

Ren stared at the jade slip in his hands.

The trap had already sprung.

And he had walked right into it.

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: The Price of Ambition

The negotiation lasted six hours.

Ren walked out with a contract that made his hands shake and a debt that would take a hundred years to repay. Consortium Master Huo had been thorough, then ruthless, then—inexplicably—generous.

"Your idea has merit," Huo had said, signing the final document with a brush that dripped liquid spirit jade. "Most cultivators come to me wanting power. You want efficiency. That's... refreshing."

The terms were simple: Azure Pond Consortium would provide New Dao Sect with a line of credit—ten thousand medium-grade spirit stones, disbursed in monthly installments of one thousand. Ren would retain full operational control. In exchange, Azure Pond received forty percent equity and a seat on something Ren called the "Board of Strategic Oversight."

Huo had laughed at that. "You're treating a sect like a merchant company."

"Because that's what it needs to be," Ren had replied. "Sects that act like temples starve. Sects that act like businesses thrive."

Now, standing outside the Azure Pavilion with the contract tucked inside his robes, Ren felt the weight of what he'd just done. Ten thousand spirit stones. Enough to upgrade the temple's formation, buy proper cultivation resources, and maybe—maybe—attract actual disciples.

Mei appeared at his side, her eyes calculating. "You look like you just sold your soul."

"I sold forty percent of it. The soul stays with me."

"Forty percent is better than I expected. I was betting you'd give up sixty."

"Thanks for the faith."

"I'm not finished. Who's the board seat?"

"Consortium Master Huo's personal representative. Someone named Feng Yin."

Mei's face went pale. "Feng Yin? The Ghost Auditor?"

"You know her?"

"Everyone knows her. She audited the Eternal Flame Sect three years ago. Found 'irregularities' in their tribute records. The sect leader was executed within the week. She doesn't just watch numbers, Ren. She watches *people*."

Ren felt the contract grow heavier. "Good. That means she'll be too busy watching to interfere."

"You're insane."

"Probably." He started walking. "But insane is better than poor."

---

The first month was chaos.

The second month was organized chaos.

By the third month, New Dao Sect had transformed from a crumbling temple with three misfits into something that almost looked like a real sect. The formation was stable, powered by spirit stones instead of desperation. The kitchen had actual ingredients—spirit grains, medicinal herbs, even a slab of demon beast meat that Li Chen had won in a card game.

"Don't ask how," Li had said, depositing the meat on the counter. "Just cook it."

Old Chen had looked at the meat like it was a holy relic. "This is from a Silverback Ape. Peak Qi Condensation beast. The spiritual energy in this... I could refine pills from the marrow alone."

"Then refine pills," Ren said. "And make soup. We're going to start inviting people."

"Inviting who?"

"Anyone who'll come."

The first Open Day was a disaster.

Three people showed up. Two were mortal merchants looking for cheap talismans. The third was a Qi Condensation cultivator who laughed at their facilities and left after ten minutes.

"We need a different approach," Mei said, reviewing the day's losses. "We spent two hundred spirit stones on food and formation demonstrations. We made back twelve."

"We made connections."

"We made enemies. That cultivator who left? He's from the Celestial Sword Sect. He's already spreading rumors that we're a front for some demonic cult."

Ren rubbed his temples. "Great. Exactly what we need—the sect that fired me thinking I'm running a rival operation."

"You're not?"

"Not yet. But give me time."

The breakthrough came from an unexpected source.

Old Chen had been experimenting with the Silverback Ape meat, trying to create a pill that could enhance physical strength without the usual side effects. The process was delicate, requiring precise temperature control and timing. He'd failed seven times.

On the eighth attempt, he created something unexpected.

Not a pill. A broth.

A simple, golden broth that shimmered with trapped spiritual energy. When consumed, it didn't just enhance strength—it temporarily boosted cultivation speed by fifteen percent for a full day.

"A casual cultivator's dream," Ren breathed, tasting the broth. "No side effects?"

"None that I can detect," Old Chen said, looking at the pot like it had personally offended him. "But it shouldn't work. The energy density is too low. The reaction shouldn't be stable."

"Yet it is."

"Yet it is."

They called it New Dao Broth. The name was terrible. The product was revolutionary.

Mei designed the marketing campaign in an afternoon. "We target outer disciples of major sects. The ones who are stuck, who can't advance, who are desperate for any edge. We offer samples. We let the results speak for themselves."

"And when the major sects find out we're poaching their members?"

"Then we'll have enough momentum that they can't crush us without looking desperate."

It worked.

Slowly at first—a few outer disciples from minor sects, then a trickle from the major ones. The Celestial Sword Sect sent spies, but Ren welcomed them openly, giving them free samples and tours of the facilities.

"Tell your sect leader that we're not a threat," Ren said to one spy, a nervous young man in outer disciple robes. "We're a supplement. A resource. Your sect provides structure and protection. We provide innovation and opportunity. There's room for both."

The spy had nodded, taken his free broth, and left.

Three days later, he returned—not as a spy, but as a defector.

"I want to join," he said, his voice shaking. "I've been stuck at the third layer of Qi Condensation for four years. I drank your broth once and felt a breakthrough coming. The Celestial Sword Sect doesn't care about disciples like me. You do."

Ren looked at the young man—barely nineteen, desperate, hopeful. He saw himself, five years ago, begging for a chance.

"Welcome to New Dao Sect," he said. "We have a lot of work to do."

---

By the sixth month, New Dao Sect had seventeen disciples.

Seventeen. From zero.

The temple was still crumbling, the formation still patchwork, and the kitchen still smelled like a battlefield. But the energy was different. Hope had replaced desperation. Purpose had replaced survival.

Feng Yin came for her first board meeting in the seventh month.

She was not what Ren expected. Short, plain-faced, dressed in gray robes that looked like they'd been through a war. No jewelry, no cultivation aura, no visible weapons. She looked like a clerk who'd wandered into the wrong building.

"I've reviewed your financials," she said, sitting across from Ren in the temple's main hall. A pile of jade tablets sat before her, each one covered in precise, tiny script. "Your burn rate is unsustainable."

"We're investing in growth—"

"Your burn rate is unsustainable," she repeated, her voice flat. "You're spending eight hundred spirit stones a month. Your revenue is four hundred. At this rate, you'll exhaust your credit line in eighteen months."

"We're about to launch a new product—"

"I've seen the projections. They're optimistic by a factor of three." She tapped a tablet. "Your broth sales are plateauing. The initial novelty has worn off. Major sects are starting to restrict their disciples from purchasing from you. You need a new strategy."

Ren leaned back, frustration bubbling. "Do you have suggestions, or are you just here to tell me I'm failing?"

Feng Yin's expression didn't change. "Both. Your product is good. Your execution is terrible. You're trying to sell to individuals when you should be selling to institutions."

"I've tried approaching sects directly. They won't even meet with me."

"Then approach them differently." She pulled a scroll from her sleeve and unrolled it. "The Celestial Sword Sect is hosting their annual tournament in three months. Winners receive 'sponsorship' from various merchant houses. What if New Dao Sect was one of those sponsors?"

Ren stared at the scroll. It was an invitation. Official, sealed with the Celestial Sword Sect's emblem.

"How did you get this?"

"I have my methods." Feng Yin's eyes flickered with something that might have been amusement. "Consortium Master Huo wants to see if his investment was wise. I want to see if you have the spine for this business. The tournament is your opportunity."

"What's the catch?"

"There's always a catch." She stood, gathering her tablets. "The Celestial Sword Sect has not forgotten you, Ren Wei. They consider your success an embarrassment. If you show up at their tournament, they will try to humiliate you. Crush you. Make an example of you."

"And if I don't show?"

"Then they'll know you're afraid. And fear is worse than failure in this world."

She left without another word.

Ren sat in the empty hall, the tournament invitation in his hands, the weight of his choices pressing down on him. Seventeen disciples. A debt that could crush him. Enemies on every side.

But also a product that worked. A team that believed. A plan that might, just might, change the cultivation world forever.

He'd go to the tournament.

And he'd show the Celestial Sword Sect exactly what they'd thrown away.

---

The night before they left, disaster struck.

Ren woke to the sound of explosions.

The temple's main hall was on fire. The formation was flickering, destabilizing. Disciples were running, screaming, trying to save what they could.

And in the center of the chaos, standing untouched by the flames, was a figure in black robes.

The figure turned as Ren approached, and he saw a face he recognized.

The Celestial Sword Sect's outer disciple from the Open Day. The one who'd laughed and left.

"Surprise," the figure said, smiling. "The sect leader sends his regards."

The flames rose higher.

And behind the figure, in the shadows, Ren saw more shapes. More black robes. More enemies.

The Celestial Sword Sect wasn't waiting for the tournament.

They were coming for him tonight.

title: The Contract Had Fine Print
content:
Consortium Master Huo's smile didn't waver. It was the kind of smile that had been practiced in mirrors, calibrated to put people at ease while revealing nothing.

"Help each other," he repeated, savoring the words like fine wine. "I like that framing. Most petitioners come here begging. You came here proposing a partnership. It's refreshing."

Ren kept his posture relaxed, his hands visible on the armrests. Everything he knew about negotiation came from a single scroll titled "The Art of Getting What You Want (Without Getting Killed)" that he'd bought from a street vendor for three copper coins. The scroll's advice had been simple: *Never show desperation. Never be the first to name a price. And if they offer you tea, don't drink it—it's probably drugged.*

A servant appeared with a tray. Two cups of jade-green tea, steam curling upward in perfect spirals.

Huo gestured. "Please. It's a special blend. Cloud Mist Peak, first harvest. Improves spiritual perception for three hours."

Ren picked up the cup. Brought it to his lips. Didn't drink.

"Your proposal mentions a 'disruptive innovation strategy,'" Huo said, leaning back. "I found that section particularly interesting. You're planning to offer cultivation guidance at prices so low that established sects can't compete, then upsell advanced techniques once disciples are committed."

"That's the general idea."

"Bold. Reckless. Potentially brilliant." Huo's eyes sharpened. "But there's a problem."

"Only one?"

Huo laughed—a genuine sound, not the polite chuckle of a man humoring an inferior. "I like you, Ren Wei. You have the desperation of a man with nothing to lose and the confidence of a man who's already figured out he's going to die anyway. That's a rare combination."

"I prefer to think of it as 'enlightened risk assessment.'"

"Call it what you want. The problem is this: your model works only if you can deliver results faster than the established sects. To do that, you need high-quality cultivation techniques. To get high-quality techniques, you need either a patron sect, a lucky inheritance, or decades of research." Huo spread his hands. "You have none of those."

Ren had expected this. It was the question every investor asked, the flaw in his plan that he'd been trying to patch since day one.

"We have something better," Ren said. "We have a formation expert who can optimize techniques for individual disciples. Instead of forcing everyone through the same rigid path, we analyze their spiritual affinity, their cultivation base, even their personality, and tailor a growth plan that maximizes efficiency."

Huo's eyebrow rose. "That's... ambitious. Formation-based personalized cultivation isn't theoretical—it's been attempted before. The cost of the arrays alone would bankrupt a mid-tier sect."

"Our formation expert works cheap."

"Cheap how?"

Ren hesitated. This was the part he'd hoped to avoid. "She has a gambling problem. She owes debts to nine different loan sharks in three cities. She's willing to work for room and board plus a percentage of any profits."

Huo stared at him for a long moment. Then he laughed again, louder this time. "You're telling me your entire competitive advantage rests on a gambling addict?"

"She's very talented."

"I don't doubt it. Desperation breeds talent." Huo pulled a jade slip from his sleeve and placed it on the desk. "I've prepared a preliminary agreement. Standard terms for a startup sect investment.
