I'll provide one thousand low-grade spirit stones upfront, access to Azure Pond's mortal-world trade network, and protection from sect predation for the first year."

Ren's heart nearly stopped. One thousand spirit stones. That was more than he'd hoped for. Enough to repair the temple. Enough to pay off the worst of the temple's debts. Enough to—

"In exchange," Huo continued, "I want thirty percent equity in New Dao Sect, a seat on your governing council, and first refusal rights on any future funding rounds."

Thirty percent. Ren had expected twenty, maybe twenty-five. Thirty was steep, but not unreasonable for a first investment. The governing council seat was standard—investors always wanted oversight. And first refusal rights were common.

"Anything else?" Ren asked, keeping his voice neutral.

"One more thing." Huo's smile widened. "I want to meet your disciples. All of them. Tomorrow. I want to evaluate their potential personally."

The request was unusual but not alarming. Investors often wanted to see the team before committing.

"That can be arranged."

"Excellent." Huo pushed the jade slip across the desk. "Then we have a deal. Sign here."

Ren picked up the slip. It was warm to the touch, pulsing with faint spiritual energy. He pressed his thumb to the seal area, felt a brief sting as the contract extracted a drop of blood, and watched as his name flared into existence on the document.

Done.

Huo stood and extended his hand. Ren shook it. The man's grip was firm, his palm dry, his eyes holding something that might have been satisfaction or might have been hunger.

"I'll have my representatives deliver the spirit stones to your temple by noon tomorrow," Huo said. "Please have your disciples ready for my visit by the afternoon."

"We'll be there."

Ren walked out of the study, his legs barely supporting him. The moment the door closed behind him, Mei was at his side, her eyes wide.

"Well?"

"We got it. One thousand spirit stones."

Mei's face split into a grin so fierce it was almost scary. "You beautiful, beautiful idiot. You actually did it."

"Don't jinx it. The real work starts now."

Li Chen appeared from somewhere—Ren had no idea where he'd been, probably exploring the pavilion's wine cellar—and clapped him on the shoulder. "Congratulations, sect leader. You've officially made us all richer or dead. Possibly both."

"Optimistic as always."

"Realism is just pessimism with better evidence."

They left the Azure Pavilion in high spirits, walking through the Jade District as the morning sun climbed higher. The streets were filling with merchants and cultivators, the air thick with the smell of exotic spices and spiritual herbs. For the first time since founding New Dao Sect, Ren allowed himself to feel hopeful.

That hope lasted approximately four hours.

---

They returned to the temple to find a man waiting at the gate.

He was tall, gaunt, dressed in black robes that had seen better centuries. His face was a mask of weathered leather, his eyes the color of dead coals. He leaned on a staff made of what looked like human bone.

"Ren Wei," he said, his voice like grinding stones.

"That's me. Can I help you?"

The man reached into his robe and produced a folded document. "I am Wei Zhong, representative of the Almsgiver's Union. I'm here to inform you that your temple's debt has been purchased by a new creditor."

Ren took the document. Read it. Read it again.

The numbers didn't change.

"The principal debt of your temple—including accumulated interest, late fees, and administrative penalties—now totals forty-seven thousand spirit stones."

Forty-seven thousand.

The temple had owed maybe eight thousand when Ren took over. How had it grown to forty-seven thousand?

"That's impossible," Ren said. "I've been making payments. I've been—"

"Your payments have been applied to interest only," Wei Zhong said, his tone flat. "The principal has continued to accrue. Per the original agreement, which you ratified when you accepted the temple deed."

"I never saw any original agreement."

"It was included in the fine print of the deed transfer. Section fourteen, subsection three." Wei Zhong produced another document, this one yellowed with age. "You signed it."

Ren stared at the signature. It was his. He remembered signing the deed transfer. He remembered the pile of papers, the hurried explanations, the clerk who'd told him "these are all standard forms, just sign here."

He hadn't read the fine print.

No one read the fine print.

"The debt is due in full within thirty days," Wei Zhong continued. "Failure to pay will result in forfeiture of all temple assets, including the building, the land, and any cultivation resources found on the premises."

Thirty days.

Forty-seven thousand spirit stones.

"You're joking," Mei said, her voice tight. "This is a joke, right? Some kind of test?"

Wei Zhong's eyes didn't waver. "The Almsgiver's Union does not joke."

Li Chen stepped forward, his hand moving toward his sword. "And if we decide not to pay?"

"The union will send collectors. They will not be as polite as I am."

A chill ran down Ren's spine. He'd heard stories about the Almsgiver's Union collectors. They didn't just take assets. They took cultivation bases. They took memories. They took everything a person was and left behind a hollow shell that breathed but didn't live.

"We'll pay," Ren said, his voice steady despite the ice in his veins. "We'll find a way."

Wei Zhong studied him for a moment, then nodded. "See that you do." He turned and walked away, his staff clicking against the stone path, disappearing into the gathering dusk.

The silence that followed was absolute.

Mei was the first to break it. "We just got one thousand spirit stones. We need forty-seven thousand. In thirty days."

"I know."

"That's impossible."

"I know."

"We're going to lose everything."

Ren looked at the crumbling temple, the leaky roof, the cracked walls. He looked at his disciples—a drunk, a gambler, and a chef who couldn't cultivate properly. He thought about the contract he'd just signed with Huo, the promises he'd made, the dreams he'd dared to have.

"No," he said quietly. "We're not."

Li Chen raised an eyebrow. "Got a plan?"

Ren turned to face them, his eyes burning with a fire that had nothing to do with cultivation.

"We're going to make forty-seven thousand spirit stones in thirty days. We're going to use Huo's investment, his trade network, and his protection. We're going to turn this sect into something the cultivation world has never seen."

"And if we fail?"

Ren smiled. It was not a pleasant smile.

"Then we die trying. But at least we'll die interesting."

Mei groaned. "I hate that plan."

"Good. Hate keeps you alive."

As night fell over the temple, Ren began to write. A new plan. A desperate plan. A plan so audacious that even he wasn't sure it could work.

But that was fine.

The best plans always seemed impossible right before they succeeded.

Or right before they killed everyone involved.

Either way, it was going to be interesting.

title: Chapter 9
content:
CHAPTER 9: The Due Diligence From Hell

The negotiation lasted six hours.

Ren emerged from the study feeling like he'd been through a formation collapse. His voice was hoarse. His hands trembled slightly. But in his storage ring sat a jade slip containing the preliminary terms of Azure Pond Consortium's investment—ten thousand spirit stones upfront, access to their merchant network, and a grace period of six months before any profit-sharing kicked in.

In exchange, Consortium Master Huo wanted forty percent equity in New Dao Sect.

It was predatory. It was also the best deal Ren was going to get anywhere.

"The blood contract ceremony is in three days," Huo had said, shaking his hand with a grip that betrayed his scholar's appearance. "I'll send my due diligence team tomorrow. Standard procedure—they'll need to inspect your facilities, interview your disciples, verify your assets. I trust you have nothing to hide."

Ren had smiled. "Of course not."

He'd lied through his teeth.

---

The due diligence team arrived at dawn.

There were seven of them. Seven cultivators in matching blue-and-silver robes, each carrying a jade tablet for recording observations, each with eyes that missed nothing. They moved through the crumbling temple like a swarm of locusts, poking at foundation stones, examining the pathetic spirit vein that barely qualified as a trickle, taking notes on everything.

The leader was a woman named Inspector Zhao. Sharp cheekbones, sharper eyes, a cultivation base at the seventh level of Qi Condensation that she wore like a weapon. She smiled at Ren the way a cat smiles at a mouse that's particularly entertaining.

"Master Ren," she said, her voice carrying the practiced warmth of someone who'd ruined countless dreams, "your facilities are... charming. Truly. The rustic aesthetic is very much in vogue."

"The previous sect here fell on hard times," Ren said, keeping his voice light. "We're renovating."

"Renovating. Of course." Zhao made a note on her tablet. "And your spirit vein? I'm reading a density of approximately three motes of spiritual energy per cubic meter of air. That's about what I'd expect from a mortal village's communal latrine."

Mei, standing beside Ren, made a sound that could have been a laugh or a growl. "We're working on that. Master Wei has a formation that should improve circulation by—"

"A formation?" Zhao's eyebrow arched. "May I see it?"

Ren's stomach dropped.

The formation was in the basement. The basement that also contained their experimental talisman forge, the remains of three failed pill batches, and—most critically—the storage room where Li Chen kept his gambling records, his collection of "borrowed" spirit stones from various city guards, and the sword that definitely belonged to a Celestial Sword Sect elder who'd reported it stolen six months ago.

"I'll take you there myself," Ren said, his smile never wavering. "Right after you meet our disciples."

---

The interview with Xiao Bao went surprisingly well.

The chef-turned-cultivator sat across from Inspector Zhao in the temple's main hall, his hands folded neatly in his lap, his expression the perfect mixture of humble and competent. He'd spent the morning cleaning the kitchen until it gleamed. He'd prepared a demonstration dish—spirit-infused noodles that actually tasted decent. He'd even memorized a few cultivation theory basics from the manual Ren had shoved at him the night before.

"How long have you been cultivating?" Zhao asked.

"Four months," Xiao Bao said. "Before that, I was a cook at the Crimson Lotus Inn. Master Ren saw potential in me that I didn't know I had."

"And what cultivation method do you practice?"

"The Path of the Inner Flame." A lie, but a convenient one. Xiao Bao's actual cultivation was accidental—a byproduct of cooking with spirit ingredients for too long. The Path of the Inner Flame was a generic technique that could explain almost any foundation-level cultivation.

Zhao made notes. "Show me your qi circulation."

Xiao Bao closed his eyes. A faint warmth emanated from his body. Not impressive, but stable. Controlled. It spoke of discipline.

"Acceptable," Zhao said, and Ren felt a fraction of the tension leave his shoulders.

Then she turned to Mei.

---

Mei was a problem.

Not because she was incompetent—Mei was terrifyingly competent. She'd built a half-dozen businesses from nothing, negotiated with merchants who'd have sold their own mothers for profit, and once convinced a minor sect to pay her for the privilege of being her supplier.

The problem was that Mei had opinions. And she expressed them. Loudly.

"So," Zhao said, settling into her chair like a predator preparing to pounce, "you're the sect's business manager. Tell me about your experience."

"Five years in the mortal trade routes," Mei said, her voice clipped. "Three years as a purchasing agent for the Golden Lotus Sect. I've managed supply chains worth over a hundred thousand spirit stones."

"And why did you leave the Golden Lotus Sect?"

"I disagreed with their management philosophy."

"Disagreed how?"

Mei's jaw tightened. "They wanted me to pay bribes. I refused. They said it was 'standard practice.' I said it was 'standard stupidity.' They asked me to leave."

Ren winced. That was the edited version. The real story involved a screaming match in a sect leader's office, a thrown inkstone that had shattered a priceless vase, and Mei walking out with half the sect's supply ledger in her storage ring as "severance."

Zhao's smile sharpened. "I see. And your current responsibilities here?"

"Everything. Ren handles the vision, I handle the reality. Budgets, supplies, negotiations, logistics. He gets the credit, I get the headaches."

"That sounds... unbalanced."

"It's efficient." Mei leaned forward. "Look, Inspector. I know what you're doing. You're looking for cracks. Weaknesses. Reasons to justify a lower valuation or more favorable terms for your master. But here's what you're going to find: a sect that's growing, a team that's committed, and a leader who's willing to do whatever it takes to succeed. If that's not enough for Azure Pond, then your master wasted both our time."

The silence stretched.

Then Zhao laughed. A genuine laugh, surprised and appreciative.

"I like you," she said. "You're honest. Brutal, but honest. That's rare in this world."

"Thank you."

"I didn't say it was a compliment."

---

Li Chen was the final interview.

The drunk sword master had cleaned himself up. Mostly. His robes were relatively stain-free. His eyes were relatively focused. He'd even tied his hair back, though several strands had already escaped.

"Master Li," Zhao said, "your reputation precedes you. The Drunken Sword of the Southern Marches. Three years as a wandering cultivator. Fifteen confirmed duels, all victories."

"Seventeen," Li Chen corrected. "Two of them were against the same guy, but he didn't learn the first time."

"And now you're teaching at a startup sect. Why?"

Li Chen took a long drink from his gourd. "Ren asked."

"That's it? He asked?"

"He didn't treat me like a failure." Li Chen's voice lost its playful edge. "Every other sect I approached wanted to 'reform' me. 'Li Chen, you drink too much.' 'Li Chen, you're unreliable.' 'Li Chen, you're wasting your potential.' Ren just said, 'You're good with a sword. You like drinking. I can work with that.'"

Zhao studied him. "And do you think this sect can succeed?"

"I think..." Li Chen paused, considering his words carefully. "I think the traditional sects are dying. They're too rigid. Too afraid of change. Ren's trying something new. Maybe it'll work. Maybe it won't. But at least he's trying."

"Eloquent." Zhao made more notes. "One last question. Where did you get that sword?"

Li Chen's hand moved to the hilt. A casual gesture, but Ren saw the tension in his shoulders.

"It was a gift."

"From whom?"

"An admirer."

Zhao's eyes narrowed. "That looks like a Starfall Blade. Forged by the Celestial Sword Sect's master smith. They only make seven per generation. I find it hard to believe someone would give one away as a 'gift.'"

"Believe what you want." Li Chen's voice was flat. "It's my sword now."

The silence was brittle. Ren could feel the interview crumbling.

"Ahem," he interrupted. "Inspector Zhao, perhaps we should continue the facility tour? I believe you wanted to see our formation array."

Zhao held Li Chen's gaze for a moment longer, then turned. "Yes. The facility tour. Lead the way, Master Ren."

---

They were halfway to the basement when the messenger pigeon arrived.

A streak of white light shot through the temple's broken window, resolving into a paper talisman that hovered in front of Ren's face. He caught it, read the message, and felt his blood turn to ice.

*Ren Wei,*

*This is an official notification from the Celestial Sword Sect. It has come to our attention that you are operating an unauthorized cultivation sect within territory historically claimed by our jurisdiction. Furthermore, we have received credible reports that your sect may be in possession of stolen property belonging to Elder Feng Xun.*

*Effective immediately, you are ordered to cease all operations and present yourself to the Celestial Sword Sect's Hall of Discipline for questioning. Failure to comply will result in the dispatch of an enforcement team.*

*The investigation begins in seven days.*

*—Sect Master Yun, Celestial Sword Sect*

Ren read it three times.

Seven days. He had seven days to either prove his legitimacy, return the sword, or prepare for war.

Zhao was reading over his shoulder. Her expression was unreadable.

"Master Ren," she said slowly, "is there something you'd like to tell me?"

Ren crumpled the talisman in his fist.

"The tour," he said, his voice steady despite the chaos in his mind, "will have to wait. I have a crisis to manage."

"Consortium Master Huo will want to know about this."

"Tell him." Ren met her eyes. "Tell him everything. And then ask him if he still wants to invest in a sect that's about to go to war with the Celestial Sword Sect."

He turned and walked away, leaving Zhao standing in the ruined hallway, her jade tablet clutched to her chest, her smile finally gone.

Behind him, he heard Mei's voice: "Ren? Ren, what did that say? What are we going to do?"

He didn't answer.

He was already calculating. Seven days. An enforcement team. A stolen sword. A blood contract that wasn't signed yet.

And somewhere, in the shadows of the cultivation world, the information brokers were sharpening their knives, waiting to see which way the wind would blow.

The startup sect's first real crisis had arrived.

And Ren had no idea if they'd survive it.

title: The Investor Who Wanted Everything
content:
Huo leaned back in his chair, steepling his fingers. The motion was deliberate, practiced—the kind of gesture that made lesser negotiators sweat.

"You're direct," Huo said. "I appreciate that. Most cultivators who walk through my door spend twenty minutes talking about the weather before they mention spirit stones. You got to the point in thirty seconds."

"I don't have time for weather talk, Consortium Master. My sect is growing, and growing fast. But growth requires capital."

"Indeed it does." Huo pulled a scroll from his desk, unrolled it. Ren recognized his own handwriting—the proposal he'd spent three sleepless nights perfecting. "I have questions."

"Ask anything."

"First: you're asking for ten thousand spirit stones. That's a significant investment for a sect that's been operational for... what, three months?"

"Four months and twelve days."

"Four months. And in that time, your accomplishments include: defeating a rogue cultivator who was terrorizing a village, establishing a trade route between two mortal cities, and—" Huo's eyebrow rose slightly, "—making the Celestial Sword Sect look foolish in public."

"We didn't make them look foolish. They did that themselves. We just happened to be watching."

Huo chuckled. It was a genuine sound, warm and human. "I heard about that. The Celestial Sword Sect sent three Inner Disciples to 'investigate' your sect, and they ended up getting lost in a formation you set up in a cave. Took them six hours to find their way out."

"Seven hours, actually. But who's counting?"

"The point is," Huo said, rolling the scroll back up, "you've accomplished more in four months than most sects accomplish in four years. That's impressive. But it's also suspicious."

"Suspicious how?"

Huo's smile faded. "The Celestial Sword Sect isn't known for forgiving humiliation. They have long memories and sharp swords. If I invest in your sect, I'm not just investing in a business—I'm investing in a target."

Ren had expected this. "The Celestial Sword Sect won't move against us directly. They can't. If they attack a smaller sect without provocation, the other major sects will see it as aggression. The balance of power in the region is fragile. They won't risk it."

"They'll find other ways."

"Let them. I've built New Dao Sect to be adaptable. We're not a fortress—we're a network. You can't destroy a network by cutting off one head."

Huo studied him for a long moment. Then he reached into his robe and pulled out a small jade token, placing it on the desk between them.

"This is a token of intent," Huo said. "If I give this to you, it means I'm committing to the investment. But I have conditions."

*Here it comes.* "Name them."

"First: I want a seat on your sect's advisory board. Not control—you still make the final decisions. But I want to know what's happening, when it's happening."

"Acceptable."

"Second: I want an exclusivity clause. For the next three years, New Dao Sect doesn't take investment from any other consortium."

Ren's jaw tightened. "That's a significant restriction."

"It's protection for my investment. If you take money from multiple sources, your loyalties split. I need to know that when push comes to shove, you're with Azure Pond."

"And the third condition?"

Huo's eyes glinted. "I want access to your cultivation technique."

The room went cold.

Ren's hand moved instinctively toward his sword, stopping just before it touched the hilt. "That's not negotiable."

"Everything is negotiable."

"No." Ren's voice was flat, hard. "The New Dao Sect technique isn't mine to sell. It belongs to the sect, to my disciples. I won't trade their future for spirit stones."

"A smaller share, then. Fifty percent of the technique. I don't need the foundation, just the unique elements that make it work."

"The answer is still no."

Huo leaned forward, his expression shifting from friendly businessman to something more predatory. "Let me explain something to you, Ren Wei. You're asking for ten thousand spirit stones. That's enough to buy your sect outright, three times over. You're offering me nothing but promises and potential. And now you're telling me that the one thing that makes your sect valuable is off the table?"

"My sect isn't valuable because of the technique. It's valuable because of the people. The technique is just a tool."

"And tools can be replicated."

"Not this one. It's tied to the individual cultivator's spiritual resonance. Even if I gave you the manual, you couldn't use it without years of adaptation."

Huo's eyes narrowed. "You're lying."

"I'm not."

"You're a terrible liar, Ren Wei. Your left eye twitches when you're being dishonest."

Ren cursed internally. The man was observant. *Fine.* "The technique has safeguards. If I share it with outsiders, it self-destructs. Literally. The manual burns itself, and the knowledge in the cultivator's mind erases."

"That's... extreme."

"That's necessary. The cultivation world is full of thieves and murderers. I built my sect to survive that reality."

Huo was silent for a long moment. Then he laughed—a full, genuine laugh that filled the room. "You're insane. You know that? Absolutely insane."

"I've been told."

"And I think that's exactly what this stagnant, rotting cultivation world needs." Huo pushed the jade token across the desk. "I'll accept your terms. No technique. But I want something else instead."

"What?"

"I want first refusal on any new disciples you recruit. If you find someone with exceptional talent, I want to know about them before anyone else does. Exclusive access to your talent pipeline."

Ren considered this. It was still a significant concession, but it wasn't the technique. "For how long?"

"Five years."

"Three."

"Four, and I waive the exclusivity clause on investment. You can take money from others, as long as you give me the same terms."

"Deal."

They shook hands. Huo's grip was firm, professional. "I'll have the contract drawn up by tomorrow. We'll sign at noon."

"Looking forward to it."

Ren stood, his heart pounding with a mixture of relief and anxiety. He'd done it. He'd secured the investment. New Dao Sect was going to survive.

He was halfway to the door when Huo spoke again.

"Oh, one more thing."

Ren turned.

"I've heard rumors," Huo said casually, "that the Celestial Sword Sect has hired a 'problem solver.' Someone outside the normal sect structure. A mercenary."

The blood drained from Ren's face. "What kind of mercenary?"

"An assassin. A good one. They call him the 'Wandering Blade.' He's killed three sect leaders in the past year alone. And I heard a very interesting piece of information this morning—he was seen entering the city last night."

Ren's hand went to his sword. "Where?"

"I don't know. But I do know his methods. He doesn't strike directly. He studies his targets, finds their weaknesses, their people. He attacks the foundation, not the building."

*Their people.*

"Excuse me," Ren said, and ran.

He burst through the door into the waiting area. Mei was there, sitting in a chair, flipping through a financial ledger with the intensity of a hawk watching prey. Li Chen stood by the window, his hand on his sword, his eyes scanning the street.

"Where's Xiao?" Ren demanded.

Mei looked up, confused. "He stayed at the temple. Said he was working on a new dish."

"No." Ren's voice cracked. "We need to get back. Now."

"What's wrong?"

"The Celestial Sword Sect hired an assassin. The Wandering Blade. He's here. And he's going after our people."

Li Chen was already moving toward the door, his face hard. "How long do we have?"

"I don't know. But if he's as good as they say..."

They ran.

The streets of the mortal city blurred past as they sprinted toward the temple. Ren's mind raced faster than his feet. *Xiao. Alone. Defenseless. Cooking.*

They burst through the temple gates twenty minutes later, lungs burning, swords drawn.

The courtyard was quiet. Too quiet.

Smoke rose from the kitchen chimney. Normal. Peaceful. The kind of quiet that made Ren's skin crawl.

"Xiao!" he shouted.

No response.

He ran toward the kitchen, Mei and Li Chen close behind. The door was open. Steam billowed out, carrying the scent of something savory and rich.

And blood.

Ren slammed through the door, his sword already swinging.

The kitchen was a mess. Pots overturned. Ingredients scattered across the floor. A knife embedded in the wall.

And in the center of the room, tied to a chair with rope made from his own apron strings, sat Xiao.

His eyes were wide. His mouth was gagged. But he was alive.

Ren cut the ropes, pulled the gag free. "What happened? Who did this?"

Xiao gasped for air, his hands shaking. "I... I was cooking. The door opened. I thought it was you. And then there was this... this *presence*. I couldn't move. I couldn't breathe. And then a voice said..."

"Say what?"

Xiao's face went pale. "He said 'Tell your sect master that the Wandering Blade sends his regards. And that next time, I won't miss.'"

Ren's blood ran cold.

*Next time.*

Not today. Not yet. A warning. A message.

The Wandering Blade wasn't here to kill Xiao.

He was here to prove he *could*.

"Mei," Ren said, his voice hard as steel. "Cancel the contract."

"What?"

"We can't take Huo's money. Not now. If the Wandering Blade is coming for us, we need to disappear. We need to go underground."

"But the sect—"

"The sect is nothing if we're dead. We need to regroup, rethink, and find out who the hell this 'Wandering Blade' is before he finds us."

Li Chen stepped forward, his face grim. "I know who he is."

Ren turned. "What?"

"I've heard stories in the underworld. The Wandering Blade isn't just an assassin. He's a *collector*. He doesn't kill for money—he kills for techniques. Every target he takes down, he steals their cultivation method. He's built his entire style from the corpses of other cultivators."

The room went silent.

"And he's after ours," Mei whispered.

Ren's hands curled into fists.

*He'll have to go through me first.*

title: The Poison in the Porridge
content:
The negotiation lasted three hours.

Ren emerged with a jade slip containing the preliminary agreement, his hand shaking slightly from the strain of maintaining composure. Consortium Master Huo had been thorough—too thorough. He'd asked about revenue projections, disciple retention rates, competitive positioning against existing sects. Questions that Ren had prepared for, but barely.

"The terms are reasonable," Huo had said at the end, sliding the jade slip across the obsidian desk. "Five thousand spirit stones upfront, another fifteen when you reach fifty disciples. In exchange, Azure Pond receives twenty percent of all future revenue, first right of refusal on any partnership deals, and a seat on your 'advisory board.'"

Ren had nodded, signed, and walked out before Huo could change his mind.

Now he stood in the pavilion's main hall, the jade slip clutched in his palm like a lifeline. Mei was already at his side, her eyes scanning his face for any sign of failure.

"Well?"

"We got it."

Mei's stoic mask cracked, just slightly. "How much?"

"Five thousand now. Enough to fix the temple, buy supplies, and maybe—" He stopped, frowning. Something was wrong.

The room was too quiet.

The information brokers, the city guards, the wandering cultivators who usually filled the pavilion's common area—they were all gone. In their place stood a dozen men in black robes, their faces hidden behind metal masks, their cultivation bases radiating a cold pressure that made Ren's spiritual sense scream warning.

"Congratulations on your funding," said a voice from behind him.

Ren turned slowly. A woman stood in the doorway of the study, her robes a deep crimson that seemed to drink the light. She was beautiful in the way a blade was beautiful—sharp, deadly, and designed to cut. Her cultivation base was hidden, but Ren had spent enough years around powerful cultivators to know danger when he felt it.

"Who are you?" he asked, his voice steady despite the cold sweat trickling down his spine.

"My name is irrelevant. But I represent a party who is... concerned about your new partnership with Azure Pond." She smiled, and there was nothing warm about it. "Consortium Master Huo has been making enemies. Powerful ones. And those enemies would prefer that his investments do not succeed."

Mei stepped forward, her hand drifting toward the storage ring where she kept her formation flags. "The New Dao Sect has no quarrel with anyone. We just signed our first deal. We're nobody."

"Exactly." The woman's smile widened. "You're nobody. Which means no one will notice when you disappear."

The black-robed men moved as one, their hands forming identical hand seals. A formation activated, lines of crimson light spiderwebbing across the floor, the walls, the ceiling. The air grew thick, heavy, pressing against Ren's lungs like a physical weight.

"A trapping formation," Mei breathed, her eyes wide. "Grade Four, maybe higher. They're serious."

"Leave them," Li Chen said, stepping out from behind a pillar. The drunk sword master had been so still, so quiet, that even the black-robed men hadn't noticed him. His hand rested on his sword hilt, his eyes clear despite the morning's drinking. "I'll hold them off. You two run."

"Li Chen—"

"I said run." His voice was flat, final. "I've been useless for years. Let me be useful once more."

The crimson-robed woman laughed, a sound like breaking glass. "How touching. A sacrificial last stand. But I'm afraid I can't allow any of you to leave. You see, the information you carry—the terms of your agreement with Huo, the weaknesses in your sect's structure—it's all very valuable. To the right buyer."

"You're a spy," Ren said, the pieces clicking into place. "You're not here to kill us. You're here to extract information."

"Sharp. Pity you didn't figure it out before walking into my trap." She gestured, and the black-robed men completed their formation. The pressure intensified, and Ren felt his knees buckle, his spiritual energy draining away like water through a sieve.

Mei was already on the ground, gasping. Li Chen remained standing, but his face was pale, his sword hand trembling.

"If you cooperate," the woman said, walking toward Ren with the casual grace of a predator, "I'll make it quick. If you don't—" She pulled a small vial from her sleeve, filled with a viscous green liquid that hissed and smoked at the edges. "I have other methods."

Ren's mind raced. The formation was suppressing their cultivation. The black-robed men were professionals, likely from a major sect or a rival consortium. The woman was clearly the leader, her cultivation likely at least Foundation Establishment if not higher.

They were outmatched, outnumbered, and outplanned.

But Ren hadn't built New Dao Sect by accepting defeat.

"You know," he said, forcing a calm he didn't feel, "for a spy, you're pretty bad at gathering intelligence."

The woman paused, her eyebrow arching. "Oh?"

"We're a startup. That means we're broke, desperate, and willing to try anything. Do you really think we'd walk into a meeting with a major investor without contingency plans?"

He reached into his robe and pulled out a small jade talisman—crudely carved, barely holding together, but functional. Mei's eyes widened when she saw it.

"Ren, that's—"

"Explosive formation talisman," he said, holding it up. "Grade Two. Not enough to kill anyone here. But enough to collapse this building and bury the evidence."

The woman's smile faltered, just slightly. "You're bluffing."

"Try me." Ren's hand tightened on the talisman. "I've got nothing to lose. My sect is a crumbling temple with three disciples and a debt to the most dangerous investor in the city. If I die here, at least I take your information network down with me."

Silence stretched between them, taut as a drawn bowstring.

Then the woman laughed—a genuine laugh, surprised and admiring. "You're insane."

"I've been told."

She considered him for a long moment, then waved her hand. The formation dissolved, the pressure released, and the black-robed men retreated to the shadows.

"Consider this a warning," she said, her voice soft but carrying an edge of steel. "Azure Pond is playing games you don't understand. Consortium Master Huo is not your friend. And the enemies he's made will not stop with one failed ambush."

She turned and walked toward the exit, her robes flowing behind her like a river of blood.

"I'll be watching, Ren Wei. Try not to die before you become interesting."

She was gone before he could respond.

Ren let out a breath he didn't realize he'd been holding, his legs giving out beneath him. Li Chen caught him before he hit the ground, the drunk sword master's face unreadable.

"That was stupid," Li Chen said.

"Did it work?"

"...Yes."

"Then it wasn't stupid." Ren pulled himself upright, his hand still shaking around the jade slip. "We need to get back to the temple. Now. Before she changes her mind."

Mei was already packing her formation flags, her movements quick and efficient. "Who was she? What sect does she work for?"

"I don't know." Ren's jaw tightened. "But I intend to find out."

They left the Azure Pavilion through a back exit, taking alleyways and side streets, checking for tails every few steps. The city that had seemed so welcoming that morning now felt like a hunting ground, every shadow hiding a potential assassin.

When they finally reached the temple's gates, Ren collapsed against the worn stone, his body aching with exhaustion.

"We can't trust anyone," he said, his voice hollow. "Not Azure Pond. Not the other sects. Not even the mortals in the city."

"So what do we do?" Mei asked.

Ren looked up at the crumbling temple, at the broken roof tiles and the cracked walls, at the three disciples who had trusted him with their futures.

"We build," he said. "We get stronger. We make ourselves too dangerous to touch."

He pulled out the jade slip, the deal with Consortium Master Huo still fresh and binding.

"And we find out who our real enemies are—before they find out who we are."

A shadow moved at the edge of the temple grounds.

Ren spun, his hand reaching for a weapon he didn't have.

But it was just a letter, pinned to the gate by a small dagger. The seal was unfamiliar—a coiled serpent eating its own tail.

Mei pulled the letter free and read it aloud:

"Ren Wei of New Dao Sect—Your performance today was noted. The Serpent's Circle has use for someone with your... unconventional methods. We will contact you again when the time is right. Do not disappoint us."

No signature. No return address.

Just the seal of a serpent, and the promise of more games to come.

Ren stared at the letter, his mind racing.

He had wanted investors.

He had wanted allies.

Instead, he had walked into a web of conspiracies, power struggles, and predators who saw him as nothing more than a pawn to be moved.

"I think," Li Chen said quietly, "we just became very popular."

Ren crumpled the letter in his fist.

"Good," he said, his voice cold. "Let them come. We'll show them what a startup can really do."

But even as he said it, he couldn't shake the feeling that they had just stepped onto a battlefield they didn't understand—and that the first casualty would be their innocence.

title: The Heavenly Demon's Offer
content:
The negotiation lasted six hours.

Ren emerged from the study with a headache, a signed contract, and the distinct feeling that he'd just made a deal with a snake who was *letting* him think he'd won. Consortium Master Huo had agreed to everything — the initial investment, the profit-sharing structure, even the clause about non-interference in sect operations.

Too easily.

"Let me see it," Mei demanded, snatching the contract from his hands before he could blink. She scanned the jade slip, her lips moving silently as she read, her expression shifting from suspicion to confusion to outright alarm.

"This is... actually good," she said, her voice flat with disbelief. "No hidden clauses. No variable interest rates. The arbitration terms are standard. Huo even threw in a networking introduction to the Southern Trade Alliance."

"Which means?" Li Chen asked, emerging from the shadows where he'd been leaning against a wall, pretending to be decorative.

"Which means he wants something we haven't offered yet," Mei said. "Or he knows something we don't."

Ren took the contract back, rolling it carefully into his sleeve. "Or maybe he just sees potential in our model. Not everyone in the cultivation world is stuck in the past."

"There's no way Huo would sell me a piece of land I already own. There must be something wrong with it," said the master.

Li Chen stepped forward, pulling a small, dented copper mirror from his robes. It was one of his few possessions that wasn't a weapon or a bottle — a scrying tool he'd won in a card game three years ago and never quite trusted.

"That's what I'm afraid of."

They left the Azure Pavilion with the contract, a bag of spirit stones — the first tranche of investment — and a growing sense of dread that none of them could articulate.

The walk back to the temple was quiet. The sun had set, and the street lanterns cast long, dancing shadows across the cobblestones. Mei kept glancing over her shoulder. Li Chen's hand stayed near his sword hilt. Even the usually unflappable Chef Wu, who'd stayed behind to guard the temple, had left a note that read simply: "Something's wrong. Hurry back."

They found him in the main hall, surrounded by the shattered remains of what had once been their meditation cushions. His cleaver was in his hand, dripping with something that wasn't blood — a viscous, black substance that sizzled where it touched the floor.

"Trouble," Chef Wu said, not bothering with greetings. "Came about an hour after you left. Three of them. Qi Condensation peak, maybe Foundation Establishment. Hard to tell — they were wearing cloaks that blocked spiritual sense."

"Where are they now?" Ren asked, his heart rate climbing.

"Dead." Chef Wu gestured at the black substance. "But not before they left that."

He pointed with his cleaver at the wall behind the altar. Carved into the stone, in characters that seemed to writhe and twist even as Ren looked at them, was a message:

*THE HEAVENLY DEMON SECT SENDS ITS REGARDS. YOUR MORTAL PETTY AMBITIONS AMUSE US. THE NEXT VISIT WILL NOT BE SO... GENTLE.*

Below it, a seal — a coiled serpent devouring a lotus flower, the symbol of the Heavenly Demon Sect.

The largest and most feared demonic sect in the entire eastern continent.

The sect that had, according to rumor, single-handedly destroyed three major orthodox sects in the last century. The sect that operated from a floating fortress hidden somewhere in the Storm Sea. The sect that even the Celestial Sword Sect — with all its arrogance and power — avoided provoking.

"Gentle?" Mei repeated, her voice dangerously quiet. "They broke into our home, killed our meditation cushions, and left a threatening message carved into our wall. And they call *that* gentle?"

"The cushions are the least of our problems," Li Chen said, kneeling to examine the black substance on the floor. He touched it with a single finger, then immediately pulled back, hissing. The tip of his finger was already blistering. "Demonic qi. Concentrated. This wasn't just a warning — it was a demonstration. They wanted us to know exactly how outmatched we are."

Ren stared at the message, his mind racing. The Heavenly Demon Sect. Why would they care about a tiny startup sect in a mortal city? They operated on a scale that made the Celestial Sword Sect look like a village school. They had resources, armies, cultivators at the Nascent Soul level and beyond.

New Dao Sect was, by comparison, barely a speck of dust on their boots.

"Someone hired them," Ren said slowly, the pieces clicking into place. "This isn't random. This is a message from someone who wants us to know that they can reach us anywhere. At any time."

"Consortium Master Huo?" Mei suggested.

"No. If Huo wanted us dead, he wouldn't have invested. He'd have just sent assassins." Ren shook his head. "This is someone who wants us *scared*. Someone who wants us to give up. Someone who's watching to see how we react."

Li Chen stood, wiping his burned finger on his robes. "The Celestial Sword Sect?"

"Possible. But they'd have sent their own people, not hired demonic cultivators. They're too proud to outsource their revenge." Ren started pacing, his footsteps echoing in the empty hall. "No, this is someone who has connections to the demonic sects. Someone who can afford to pay for a visit like this. Someone who—"

He stopped.

The investor.

Not Huo. The *other* investor. The one Huo had mentioned during the negotiation — a rival consortium that had also expressed interest in New Dao Sect. A consortium that had, apparently, been "disappointed" when Ren chose to partner with Azure Pond.

"What's the name of Huo's competitor?" Ren asked.

Mei frowned, thinking. "The Golden Lotus Investment Group. Why?"

"Because I think they just sent us a welcome gift."

The rest of the night was spent reinforcing the temple's defenses. Li Chen pulled out every formation flag he owned, arranging them in patterns that made even Mei's eyes cross. Chef Wu brewed a potent spiritual tea that was supposed to help with demonic qi contamination — it tasted like burnt soil, but it worked. Mei, for her part, spent the entire time on her communication talisman, calling in favors, gathering intelligence, and generally making it clear that she was not going to let some demonic thugs ruin her business plans.

Ren sat in the center of the main hall, the contract from Azure Pond spread out before him, and tried to think.

The Golden Lotus Investment Group. He'd heard of them, vaguely — a shadowy organization that operated across the boundaries between orthodox and demonic sects, funding anyone who could pay their exorbitant interest rates. They were rumored to have ties to the Heavenly Demon Sect, and to several other demonic organizations that didn't even have names.

If they'd wanted to invest in New Dao Sect, and Ren had chosen Huo instead...

"This is going to escalate," he muttered to himself. "They're not going to stop with a warning. They're going to hit us where it hurts."

The question was: where?

Their disciples? They only had three, and they were all present. Their assets? They had a crumbling temple and a bag of spirit stones. Their reputation? They barely had one to damage.

And then it hit him.

"The mortal city," Ren said aloud, making Mei look up from her talisman. "They're going to attack the mortal city. To show us that they can. To prove that our 'integration with mortal society' — the thing that makes us unique — is actually our biggest weakness."

Mei's face went pale. "If demonic cultivators attack the city, the imperial authorities will blame us. We're the only sect nearby. We're supposed to protect them."

"Exactly." Ren stood, his decision made. "We need to evacuate the city. Tonight."

"Evacuate an entire city of ten thousand people? Based on a hunch?" Li Chen asked, his voice skeptical. "Even if we had the authority, we don't have the manpower. It would take days."

"Then we warn them. We set up defenses. We prepare for the worst." Ren grabbed his sword from where it leaned against the wall. "Mei, you're with me. We're going to the city lord's mansion. Li Chen, stay here and fortify the temple. Chef Wu, start brewing every combat-grade pill you can manage."

"And if we're wrong?" Mei asked.

"Then we've wasted a night and looked paranoid." Ren met her eyes. "And if we're right, we've saved ten thousand lives."

They moved.

The city lord — a portly mortal named Wei Chenglei who had purchased his position through family connections rather than merit — was not pleased to be woken at midnight by a ragged cultivator with a wild story about demonic attacks.

"I appreciate your concern, Sect Master Ren," Wei said, his voice dripping with condescension, "but I have my own intelligence networks. If there were any threat to my city, I would know about it. Now, if you'll excuse me—"

The explosion cut him off.

A pillar of black fire erupted from the eastern district, lighting up the night sky like a demonic sunrise. Screams followed, distant but growing louder. The sound of buildings collapsing. The clash of steel.

And above it all, a voice that seemed to come from everywhere at once:

"SECT MASTER REN WEI. THE HEAVENLY DEMON SECT EXTENDS A FORMAL INVITATION. SURRENDER YOUR SECT, YOUR ASSETS, AND YOUR MORTAL PETS, AND WE WILL ALLOW YOU TO DIE QUICKLY."

The city lord's face went gray.

Ren turned to the window, watching the flames spread.

"Too late," he whispered.

Mei grabbed his arm, her nails digging into his skin. "What do we do?"

Ren looked at the contract in his hand, the spirit stones in his pouch, the fledgling sect that was supposed to be his fresh start.

Then he looked at the burning city, the innocent people, the demonic cultivators who had just declared war on everything he was trying to build.

And he made his choice.

"We fight," he said. "We fight, and we don't stop fighting until they're dead or we are."

He walked toward the door, his sword in his hand, his heart pounding with a mixture of terror and something else — something that felt almost like purpose.

"Mei, find Li Chen and Chef Wu. Tell them to bring everything we have. We're going to remind the Heavenly Demon Sect why mortals have survived for ten thousand years without their 'protection.'"

"And if we don't survive?"

Ren paused at the threshold, looking back at her with a grim smile.

"Then at least we made them remember our name."

He stepped out into the burning night.

title: The Shareholder Revolt
content:
The temple gates exploded inward.

Ren looked up from his spreadsheets, tea sloshing over the rim of his cup. Wood splinters rained across the courtyard. Three figures stood in the smoke, their robes immaculate, their auras pressing down like mountains.

"Ren Wei," the lead figure said, her voice carrying the weight of absolute authority. "You have seventy-two hours to vacate these premises."

He recognized her. Elder Xue from the Celestial Sword Sect's Disciplinary Hall. She'd been the one who stamped his termination papers. Behind her stood two enforcers in black and gold, their cultivation bases hovering at Core Formation — far beyond anything New Dao Sect could currently match.

"Elder Xue," Ren said, rising slowly. "To what do I owe the pleasure? I don't recall scheduling a visit."

"The pleasure is entirely yours." She stepped forward, her boots crunching on broken wood. "This land has been claimed by the Celestial Sword Sect under Article 47 of the Sect Territories Act. Your occupancy is no longer valid."

Mei appeared from the kitchen, still holding her cleaver. Blood dripped from the blade — chicken blood, from lunch prep, but the effect was menacing. "Article 47 requires proof of abandonment. We've been here for three months. We've paid taxes. We have witnesses."

"Your witnesses have been... persuaded to recant." Elder Xue smiled. It was not a pleasant expression. "The merchant who sold you supplies? He now remembers delivering to an empty building. The local magistrate who registered your claim? He's been transferred to a border post. Your paper trail is gone."

Ren's stomach dropped. This wasn't a dispute. This was an erasure.

"Seventy-two hours," Elder Xue repeated. "After that, we will use force."

She turned and walked away, her enforcers following. The smoke dissipated like it had never existed. Only the shattered gate remained, evidence that anything had happened at all.

---

"We're screwed."

Mei slammed her cleaver into the cutting board. "Not helpful, Li Chen."

"Truth hurts." The drunk sword master poured himself another cup. "Celestial Sword doesn't make moves like this unless someone high up ordered it. Article 47 requires Sect Leader approval. The sect leader personally wants us gone."

Ren paced the main hall. His disciples sat around the central table — Mei, Li Chen, and Xiao Bao, who had finally emerged from the kitchen after hearing the commotion. The chef looked pale, her hands trembling slightly.

"Why now?" Ren muttered. "We've been under their radar for months. What changed?"

"The investor meeting," Mei said quietly. "Huo."

Everyone went silent.

Ren stopped pacing. "Huo wouldn't betray us. He signed the contract. He's getting twenty percent of future revenue."

"Contracts don't matter to people like him." Li Chen's voice was bitter. "I've seen it before. Big consortiums make deals with small sects, then sell that information to larger sects for favors. Huo probably traded our location and business model to Celestial Sword in exchange for access to their spirit stone mines."

"That's speculation."

"It's probability."

Xiao Bao raised her hand hesitantly. "Master? I might have something."

"What?"

"I was delivering food to the Jade District yesterday. A merchant's birthday banquet. I overheard some Celestial Sword disciples talking." She swallowed. "They said the sect had been watching us since the investor meeting. They said someone named 'Consultant Zhou' had been advising them on how to handle 'the startup problem.'"

Ren's blood ran cold.

Consultant Zhou. He'd heard that name before, in hushed whispers at the Night Market. Zhou was a rogue cultivator who'd been expelled from the Heavenly Calculation Sect for "unethical business practices." He'd reinvented himself as a corporate raider, targeting small sects and dismantling them for parts.

"Huo sold us to a corporate raider," Ren said, the words tasting like ash.

"Or Huo *is* working with the raider," Mei corrected. "Either way, we're caught between two predators."

---

They worked through the night.

Ren sent messages to every contact he had — the information broker, the merchant who'd sold them the temple, even the grumpy formation master who'd helped them install basic wards. Responses came back empty. No one wanted to touch this. The Celestial Sword Sect's shadow was long, and no one was willing to stand in it.

By dawn, Ren had three options:

1. **Fight.** They had one Core Formation cultivator (Li Chen, when sober), two Qi Condensation (Mei and Xiao Bao), and Ren himself, who was barely at the peak of Qi Condensation. Against two Core Formation enforcers and whatever backup Elder Xue could summon. They'd lose.

2. **Flee.** Abandon the temple, take what they could carry, start over somewhere else. They'd done it before. But they'd lose everything — the investment from Huo, the reputation they'd built, the momentum that had taken months to create.

3. **Negotiate.** Try to find leverage. Something the Celestial Sword Sect wanted more than they wanted to crush New Dao Sect.

"Option three is the only one that doesn't end with us dead or destitute," Ren said, rubbing his eyes.

"Negotiate with what?" Mei demanded. "We have nothing they want."

"We have our business model."

"They can copy it."

"They can try." Ren pulled out a scroll, unrolling it across the table. "But I've been documenting everything. Our methods, our contracts, our customer acquisition strategies. If they destroy us, I'll release it all to the public. The Heavenly Calculation Sect, the Spirit Blade Alliance, every information broker in the city. Let them compete with a dozen copycat sects instead of just one."

Li Chen whistled. "Mutually assured destruction. I like it."

"It's a bluff," Xiao Bao said quietly.

"It's a bluff," Ren agreed. "But they don't know that."

---

The meeting was set for noon, at the Azure Pavilion.

Same place as the investor meeting. Same room. Ren wondered if Huo would be there, watching from the shadows, enjoying the show.

Elder Xue arrived alone this time, her aura restrained but still threatening. She sat across from Ren, her hands folded, her face expressionless.

"I'll be blunt," Ren said. "You can't destroy us without consequences."

"Consequences?" She raised an eyebrow. "From what? A collection of misfits and failures?"

"From the information I've leaked." Ren slid a scroll across the table. "This is a sample. Financial records, customer testimonials, operational details. If New Dao Sect disappears, this goes to every major sect in the region. Your attack becomes public knowledge. Other small sects will see you as a threat. Investors will pull back from the region."

Elder Xue read the scroll. Her expression didn't change.

"Interesting," she said. "But incomplete."

"What do you mean?"

She set the scroll down. "You're assuming the Celestial Sword Sect cares about public opinion. We don't. We've survived for three thousand years because we control the resources that matter — spirit stones, cultivation techniques, political connections. Your little information leak would be a footnote, forgotten in a week."

Ren's heart sank. She was right. He'd been bluffing with empty cards.

"However," Elder Xue continued, "I'm authorized to offer an alternative."

She slid another scroll across the table. This one was sealed with green wax, the mark of the Celestial Sword Sect's inner council.

Ren opened it. Read it. Read it again.

"You're joking."

"I don't joke, Ren Wei. The sect leader is impressed by your... unconventional methods. He's offering you a position. Chief Innovation Officer of the Celestial Sword Sect's New Ventures Division. You'd oversee all experimental projects. Your disciples would be absorbed into our ranks. The temple becomes an official satellite campus."

"And the price?"

"No more New Dao Sect. You dissolve the startup, surrender all intellectual property, and sign a non-compete clause for fifty years."

Ren stared at the contract. It was everything he'd ever wanted as a young cultivator — a position in the greatest sect in the region, resources beyond his imagination, respect from his peers.

It was also everything he'd fought against.

"If I refuse?"

"Then you have until sunset." Elder Xue stood. "Choose wisely, Ren Wei. The sect leader doesn't offer second chances."

She left.

Ren sat alone in the empty room, the contract in his hands, the weight of the decision pressing down on him like a collapsing mountain.

---

He returned to the temple at dusk.

Mei, Li Chen, and Xiao Bao were waiting. They'd packed. Three small bags sat by the door, ready to flee.

"Well?" Mei asked.

Ren held up the contract. Briefly, he explained the offer.

Silence stretched between them.

"Take it," Li Chen said finally. "It's a good deal. Better than we deserve."

"It's a cage," Xiao Bao whispered.

"It's survival," Li Chen countered.

Mei said nothing. She just watched Ren, waiting.

Ren looked at his disciples. Three misfits who'd believed in his crazy dream. A drunk who'd found purpose in protecting something. A cook who'd discovered cultivation through her art. A gambler who'd stopped running long enough to care.

"I'm not taking the offer," Ren said.

Li Chen opened his mouth to argue. Ren held up a hand.

"But I'm not letting them destroy us either. We're going to fight. Not with swords — with something they can't counter."

"What?" Mei asked.

Ren smiled. It was the smile of a man who had nothing left to lose, and therefore everything to gain.

"We're going public."

---

The news spread like wildfire.

By midnight, every information broker in the Jade District had copies of Ren's documents. By dawn, the major sects had received formal notices. By noon, Consortium Master Huo was pounding on the temple gates.

"Are you insane?" Huo's face was red, his composure shattered. "You've just declared war on the Celestial Sword Sect! Do you know what they'll do to you? To me? My reputation — "

"Your reputation was already damaged when you sold us to Consultant Zhou," Ren said calmly. "Consider this a penalty for breach of trust."

"I didn't — "

"Don't lie to me, Huo. I have proof." Ren held up a recording talisman. "One of your servants was quite talkative after a few drinks."

Huo's face went pale. "What do you want?"

"Nothing. Our contract is void. You get nothing. New Dao Sect is now a public entity. Anyone can invest. Anyone can buy shares. We're going to democratize cultivation funding."

"You'll destroy the entire system!"

"No." Ren's voice was steel. "We're going to change it."

Huo stormed off, muttering curses.

Mei appeared at Ren's side. "That was satisfying."

"It was stupid."

"Also satisfying."

Ren laughed. It was hollow, but real. "Get ready. The Celestial Sword Sect will respond within twenty-four hours. We need to be gone before they arrive."

"Gone where?"

Ren looked at the setting sun, painting the sky in shades of blood and gold.

"Somewhere they can't find us. Somewhere we can rebuild. I've been keeping a secret, Mei. A location I discovered during my years at the Celestial Sword Sect. A place they sealed away because they couldn't control it."

Mei's eyes widened. "You're not talking about — "

"The Hidden Realm of Eternal Dawn." Ren's voice dropped to a whisper. "The sect thinks it's destroyed. It's not

title: The Inheritance That Changed Everything
content:
The negotiation stretched into its third hour. Ren's throat was dry, his palms sweaty, but he kept his voice steady as Consortium Master Huo dissected his proposal with surgical precision.

"Your revenue projections assume a 300% growth rate," Huo said, tapping a finger on the jade tablet that displayed Ren's carefully crafted numbers. "In the cultivation world, even the most aggressive sects rarely exceed 40% annual growth. What makes you think you can achieve the impossible?"

"Because I'm not selling what they're selling," Ren replied. "Traditional sects offer prestige, protection, and access to techniques. They're luxury goods. I'm offering efficiency, innovation, and results. It's a different market."

"A market that doesn't exist yet."

"Every market starts somewhere. The first person to sell formation arrays thought the same thing."

Huo's smile didn't reach his eyes. "Bold. I like bold. But bold doesn't pay debts." He leaned back, steepling his fingers. "Let me make you a counter-offer. I'll invest three hundred high-grade spirit stones—"

Ren's heart nearly stopped. That was triple what he'd asked for.

"—in exchange for seventy percent equity in New Dao Sect. Full voting rights. And a seat on your 'board,' as you call it."

The room felt suddenly cold. Seventy percent meant Huo would own the sect. Ren would be a figurehead, at best.

"That's... not what we discussed," Ren said carefully.

"That's what I'm offering. Take it or leave it."

Ren thought of Mei waiting outside, of Li Chen's drunken optimism, of the crumbling temple that was somehow home. He thought of the Celestial Sword Sect's mocking laughter when he'd been cast out.

"I can't accept those terms," he said.

Huo's expression didn't change. "Then we have nothing to discuss."

"I understand." Ren stood, his legs shaky but his voice firm. "Thank you for your time, Consortium Master."

He was halfway to the door when Huo spoke again.

"Wait."

Ren turned. Huo was holding a small wooden box, plain and unremarkable.

"Before you go," Huo said, "I want you to have something. Consider it a gift. No strings attached."

Ren's instincts screamed *trap*. But curiosity was stronger. He took the box, opened it.

Inside was a jade slip. Ancient. Cracks running through its surface like rivers on a map.

"What is this?"

"A memory. One I've been holding for the right person." Huo's voice was strangely soft. "It contains information about your parents."

The world stopped.

"My parents died when I was three," Ren said. His voice was barely a whisper.

"Did they?"

The words hit like a physical blow. Ren's hands trembled as he looked at the jade slip. "Why would you have this?"

"Because I was there. When they... left." Huo's eyes were distant. "Your father was a genius. A formation master whose work rivaled the ancient immortals. Your mother was a strategist whose mind could see ten moves ahead of anyone alive. They were building something, Ren. Something that threatened the established order."

"Like what I'm building now."

"Exactly." Huo's smile was sad. "They were killed. Not by accident. Not by beasts. By the Celestial Sword Sect, the Jade Phoenix Palace, and seven other major sects working together. Your father had created a formation that could break the monopoly on spiritual energy distribution. Your mother had designed a business model that would have made the old hierarchies obsolete."

Ren's blood ran cold. "They were murdered. For innovation."

"For *threat*. There's a difference, though the powerful rarely see it." Huo stood, walked around the desk. "I've been watching you, Ren. From the moment you were expelled. I wanted to see if you had their fire. You do."

"Why are you telling me this now?"

"Because the Azure Pond Consortium isn't just an investment firm. We're a resistance. A network of people who believe the cultivation world can be better. Your parents were founding members." Huo's voice dropped. "And their killers are still out there. Still powerful. Still crushing anyone who tries to change the system."

Ren looked at the jade slip. "What's on this?"

"Everything. Their research. Their plans. The names of everyone involved in their deaths." Huo paused. "And the location of their final project. A formation that could rewrite the rules of cultivation itself."

"Why haven't you used it?"

"Because I'm not them. I'm a financier, not a visionary. I can move resources, but I can't build a revolution." Huo met Ren's eyes. "You can."

The jade slip felt impossibly heavy in Ren's hand.

"If I take this," Ren said slowly, "I become a target."

"You already are. The Celestial Sword Sect hasn't forgotten you. They're waiting to see if you'll become a problem." Huo's smile returned, sharp this time. "Take the jade slip. Learn the truth. And when you're ready, come back. We'll talk about investment again. On your terms."

Ren pocketed the jade slip. "I will."

"One more thing." Huo held up a finger. "Your disciples. The drunk sword master, the gambling formation expert, the chef who cultivates through cooking. They're not random misfits, are they?"

"What do you mean?"

"They were placed. Deliberately. By people who wanted to see if you had what it takes." Huo's eyes glittered. "Your mother had a saying: 'A sect isn't built on talent. It's built on loyalty. Find people the world has rejected, and they'll die for you.'"

Ren's mind raced. "Who placed them?"

"Figure that out yourself. Consider it your first test." Huo gestured toward the door. "Now go. And Ren?"

"Yes?"

"Don't trust anyone. Not even me."

---

Ren walked out of the study in a daze. Mei and Li Chen were waiting, both looking anxious.

"Well?" Mei demanded. "Did we get the investment?"

"No." Ren's voice was hollow. "But I got something else."

He told them everything. The counter-offer. The jade slip. The revelation about his parents.

When he finished, Li Chen was pale. "This is... this is huge."

"Too huge," Mei said, her voice sharp. "You're telling me your parents were revolutionary geniuses murdered by the entire cultivation establishment, and this random merchant just happened to have their secrets?"

"You don't trust him?"

"I don't trust anyone who gives you exactly what you want at exactly the right moment." Mei crossed her arms. "This could be a trap. A way to control you, or to turn you against the major sects for his own purposes."

"It could be," Ren admitted. "But it could also be the truth."

"We need to verify," Li Chen said. "Quietly. Let me dig into Huo's background, his connections. See if his story holds water."

"Do it." Ren looked at the jade slip. "I'm going to study this. Tonight."

"Alone?" Mei raised an eyebrow.

"Alone." Ren's voice was firm. "If it's a trap, I'd rather only I fall into it."

---

That night, in the crumbling temple that served as New Dao Sect's headquarters, Ren sat cross-legged on the floor. The jade slip rested in his palms, its surface cool and ancient.

He pressed it to his forehead.

The world dissolved.

He was standing in a memory. A workshop, filled with floating formations and glowing diagrams. A man and a woman worked side by side, their movements synchronized, their faces alight with passion.

His father. His mother.

"Ren, my son," his father's voice echoed, warm and distant. "If you're seeing this, then we failed. But you didn't. You survived."

His mother turned, her eyes meeting his across the void of time. "There's so much we wanted to tell you. So much we wanted to teach you. But the world is dangerous, and knowledge is the most dangerous thing of all."

"We hid our work in three places," his father continued. "This jade slip contains the first. The second is with someone you trust. The third is in a place you'll recognize when you're ready."

His mother stepped closer. "The formation we designed can break the monopoly on spiritual energy. It can give cultivation back to the people. But it's not complete. It needs a key."

"What kind of key?" Ren asked, though he knew they couldn't hear him.

"The blood of the ones who killed us," his father said, his voice turning cold. "Not for revenge. For access. The formation is locked. Only the spiritual signature of our murderers can unlock it."

Ren's blood ran cold.

"Find them," his mother said. "Find all of them. And when you do, you'll have the power to change everything."

The memory began to fade.

"Wait!" Ren shouted. "Who are they? Who killed you?"

But the vision was already crumbling, the faces of his parents dissolving into light.

Then, in the final moment before the memory vanished entirely, names appeared. Etched in fire, burning into his mind.

Nine names. Nine sects.

The Celestial Sword Sect. The Jade Phoenix Palace. The Seven Stars Pavilion. The Golden Lotus Temple. The Crimson Fang Sect. The Misty Peak Alliance. The Thunderclap Monastery. The Eternal Stream Sect. The Black Iron Clan.

And at the bottom, one more name, written in blood:

*The Azure Pond Consortium.*

Ren's eyes snapped open. He was gasping, drenched in sweat.

Huo. The man who had given him the jade slip. The man who claimed to be an ally.

His name was on the list.

Ren stared at the jade slip in his hands, his mind reeling. Everything was a lie. Or everything was the truth. He didn't know anymore.

But one thing was certain.

He was in far deeper than he'd ever imagined.

And the people who killed his parents were still watching.

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Auditor from Hell

The negotiation lasted four hours.

By the end, Ren's voice was hoarse, his hands were shaking, and he had successfully negotiated exactly nothing.

"Your proposal is interesting," Consortium Master Huo said, steepling his fingers. "But I need more than interesting. I need evidence. Proof that your methods work better than traditional sect structures."

"What kind of proof?"

Huo smiled. It was not a pleasant smile. "I want to audit your sect."

Ren's stomach dropped. "Audit?"

"Full financial and operational review. I'll send my best analyst to your temple. She'll observe your operations, interview your disciples, review your records. If she approves, I'll invest three times your initial ask. If she doesn't..." Huo shrugged. "Then we'll know your 'startup sect' is just another failed experiment."

"And if I refuse?"

"Then you walk out that door, and Azure Pond never does business with you again." Huo leaned back. "I'm giving you a chance, Ren Wei. Most people never get this far. Don't waste it."

Ren wanted to argue. Wanted to point out that his sect had no financial records, no operational documentation, no *anything* that would survive an audit. But he also knew this was the only offer he was going to get.

"Fine. Send your analyst. But I want something in return."

Huo raised an eyebrow.

"If she approves, the investment comes immediately. No more delays, no more 'due diligence.' And I want a clause that prevents Azure Pond from poaching my disciples."

Huo laughed. Actually laughed. "You think your disciples are worth poaching? A drunk sword master, a gambling formation expert, and a chef who cultivates by accident?"

"You've done your research."

"I always do. But fine. I agree to your terms." Huo extended his hand. "Do we have a deal?"

Ren shook it. The handshake was firm, professional, and felt like signing his own death warrant.

---

The analyst arrived three days later.

Her name was Yan Ling, and she was terrifying.

She was short, barely reaching Ren's shoulder, with sharp eyes that missed nothing and a notebook she never put down. She wore plain gray robes, no jewelry, no makeup. Her hair was pulled back in a severe bun. She looked like the kind of person who corrected grammar in official documents for fun.

"This is the sect?" she asked, staring at the crumbling temple. Her voice was flat, emotionless.

"This is it," Ren said, trying to sound proud. "New Dao Temple. Formerly the Temple of Eternal Wisdom, currently undergoing renovations."

"Renovations." She wrote something in her notebook. "I see cracked walls, a broken roof, and what appears to be a chicken coop where your meditation hall should be."

"That's the meditation hall. The chickens are... part of the ambiance."

Yan Ling stared at him. Ren felt his soul being judged.

"I'll start with the financial records," she said. "Where are they?"

Ren led her to his office — a converted storage room with a desk made of old planks and a chair that wobbled if you sat too aggressively. He opened a cabinet and pulled out a stack of papers.

"Our records," he said, trying to sound confident. "All transactions, income, and expenses."

Yan Ling took the stack, flipped through it, and went very, very still.

"There's blood on these pages."

"Occupational hazard."

"Some of them are written on napkins."

"We ran out of paper."

"And this one appears to be a receipt from a noodle shop."

"We had a team-building dinner."

Yan Ling closed the notebook. "Mr. Ren, I've audited thirty-seven sects in my career. I've seen corrupt treasurers, fraudulent ledgers, and embezzlement on a scale that would bankrupt nations. But this..." She held up the noodle shop receipt. "This is the first time I've seen a sect's financial records that include a customer loyalty card."

"We get a free bowl after ten purchases."

"I can see that."

---

The next three days were hell.

Yan Ling followed Ren everywhere. She watched his training sessions with Xiao Bao (who kept trying to cook the spirit herbs instead of cultivating with them). She observed Li Chen's information-gathering methods (which involved a lot of drinking, gambling, and questionable decisions). She interviewed Mei about her business strategies (Mei responded by trying to sell her an investment package).

"This sect is chaos," Yan Ling said on the third evening. She sat across from Ren at the dinner table, eating Xiao Bao's latest creation — a soup that tasted like spiritual energy and regret.

"Organized chaos," Ren corrected.

"There is nothing organized about this. Your formation expert is currently passed out in a ditch. Your sword master started a drinking contest with a group of mortal farmers and lost. Your chef almost burned down the kitchen trying to refine a spirit herb."

"Xiao Bao is learning."

"Learning how to cause explosions."

"Progress is progress."

Yan Ling put down her spoon. "Mr. Ren, I'm going to be honest with you. By every metric I use to evaluate a sect's viability, yours should have failed months ago. You have no stable income. No clear hierarchy. No strategic plan. Your disciples are misfits and outcasts. Your temple is falling apart. And your financial records are written on garbage."

Ren's heart sank.

"However," she continued, "I've also noticed something unusual."

"What?"

"Your disciples are loyal. Stupid, but loyal. They follow you not because they fear you, but because they believe in you. And you, Mr. Ren, are the strangest sect leader I've ever met."

"Is that a compliment?"

"It's an observation." She pulled out her notebook. "You're willing to fail. You accept risk as a normal part of operations. You learn from your mistakes instead of trying to hide them. And you treat your disciples as partners, not subordinates."

"Is that enough? For the investment?"

Yan Ling was silent for a long moment. Then she said, "I'll recommend approval. With conditions."

"What conditions?"

"First, you need proper records. No more napkins, no more receipts from noodle shops. I'll send you a template for a proper ledger. Second, you need a backup plan. If one of your disciples dies or leaves, your entire operation collapses. That's unacceptable. Third..."

She paused, and for the first time, her expression softened.

"Don't lose what you've built here. This sect is broken, messy, and probably doomed to fail. But it's also the most genuine thing I've seen in years. Most sects are built on politics, power, and greed. Yours is built on something else."

"What?"

"I haven't figured that out yet. But give me a few more days."

---

On the fifth day, everything went wrong.

Ren woke to the sound of shouting. He ran outside to find Yan Ling standing in the courtyard, her notebook clutched to her chest, staring at the sky.

"What's happening?" Ren demanded.

"Look," she said, pointing.

Ren looked up.

Three figures were descending from the clouds, their robes billowing, their auras blazing with power. They landed in the courtyard with a thunderous impact that cracked the stone tiles.

The leader was a woman in white and gold, her face cold, her eyes like winter frost. She carried a sword that hummed with killing intent.

"Ren Wei of the New Dao Sect," she said, her voice carrying the weight of absolute authority. "By order of the Celestial Sword Sect and the Alliance of Orthodox Sects, you are hereby charged with heresy, corruption of cultivation principles, and operating an unauthorized sect."

Ren's blood turned to ice.

"Who are you?" he managed.

"I am Elder Xue of the Celestial Sword Sect." She drew her sword, and the blade sang with spiritual energy. "And I am here to shut you down."

Yan Ling stepped forward. "You can't do that. The Azure Pond Consortium has a pending investment agreement with this sect. Legal protections apply—"

"The Azure Pond Consortium," Elder Xue interrupted, "has been informed that any association with this heretic will result in sanctions against your entire organization."

Yan Ling's face went pale. "You wouldn't dare."

"I already have." Elder Xue smiled. "Your consortium master received the message this morning. He has chosen to withdraw from all negotiations with the New Dao Sect."

Ren felt the world collapse around him. The investment. The deal. Everything he had worked for. Gone.

"Now," Elder Xue said, raising her sword, "you will disband this sect, surrender your cultivation, and face judgment for your crimes."

Mei appeared beside Ren, her face hard. Li Chen stumbled out of the temple, still half-drunk. Xiao Bao emerged from the kitchen, clutching a ladle like a weapon.

"Ren," Mei said quietly. "What do we do?"

Ren looked at his disciples. His broken, foolish, loyal disciples. Then he looked at Elder Xue, who represented everything he had fought against.

He straightened his spine.

"You want to shut us down?" he said, his voice steady despite the fear. "You'll have to go through all of us."

Elder Xue laughed. It was not a pleasant sound.

"So be it."

She raised her sword—

And the ground exploded.

---

When the dust cleared, a figure stood in the crater. Tall, broad-shouldered, wearing robes so patched they made Ren's look luxurious. He carried a gourd on his hip and a sword on his back.

"Sorry I'm late," the figure said, his voice rough as gravel. "Had to settle a bet."

Ren stared. "Who are you?"

The man grinned, revealing teeth that were somehow both yellow and sharp.

"Name's Old Shen. And I've been looking for you, Ren Wei."

"For what?"

Old Shen's grin widened. "I'm your first real investor. And I brought friends."

He whistled.

Behind him, a dozen figures emerged from the shadows. Cultivators, all of them. Some old, some young. All of them wearing expressions that said they had nothing left to lose.

"The Broken Swords Alliance," Old Shen said, spreading his arms. "We're sects that the orthodox powers destroyed. Outcasts. Survivors. And we've been watching you, Ren Wei."

Elder Xue's face twisted with rage. "You dare—"

"We dare," Old Shen interrupted. "Because Ren Wei here has something we need. A new way of doing things. A system that doesn't crush people like us."

He turned to Ren.

"So here's the deal, kid. We'll help you survive tonight. We'll give you resources, fighters, everything you need to build your sect. In exchange..."

He paused.

"You help us build ours."

Ren looked at Elder Xue. At his disciples. At the army of broken cultivators who had appeared from nowhere.

He had no choice.

"Deal," he said.

Elder Xue screamed and attacked.

And the battle for the New Dao Sect began.

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER 16: The Price of Ambition

Ren held his breath as Huo leaned back, steepling his fingers. The silence stretched, filled with the subtle hum of formations embedded in the walls.

"You're bold," Huo finally said. "I appreciate boldness. But boldness without substance is just stupidity wearing nice clothes." He pulled a scroll from his desk, unrolled it. Ren's proposal. Covered in red ink. "Your numbers are optimistic. Your timeline is aggressive. Your risk assessment is practically nonexistent."

"Optimistic because I believe in what we're building," Ren said. "Aggressive because the cultivation world doesn't wait for latecomers.
