"I hope you do." Huo's voice followed him to the door. "The cultivation world is changing, Ren Wei. The old order is crumbling. New powers are rising. You can either be part of that change, or you can be crushed by it. The choice is yours."

Ren stepped out of the study, the scrolls tucked into his robes.

Mei was waiting, her face tight with barely suppressed anxiety. Li Chen had appeared from somewhere, leaning against a wall with feigned casualness.

"Well?" Mei demanded.

"We need to talk," Ren said. "Somewhere private."

They found a teahouse three blocks away, rented a private room, and sealed the doors with a soundproof formation Li Chen had "borrowed" from a gambling den.

Ren laid out the contracts on the low table.

Mei read them in silence, her expression shifting from shock to calculation to cold fury.

"He's trying to own us," she said finally.

"He's trying to protect his investment," Ren replied. "Which means protecting us, whether we like it or not."

"Or controlling us." Li Chen poked the Geomantic Seal clause. "This thing would let them watch everything we do. Every conversation. Every deal. Every time you take a piss."

"Which is why we can't accept it." Mei slammed her hand on the table. "Ren, this is a trap. Huo wants to make us dependent on him, then squeeze us dry."

"I know." Ren's voice was quiet. "But if we don't accept, we have no backing. No protection. The Celestial Sword Sect will destroy us before we even get started."

"Then we find another investor."

"There *is* no other investor." Ren met her eyes. "You've done the research. You know the landscape. Every major consortium either demands more control than Huo or offers less protection. He's the best option we have."

Mei's face crumpled. "Then we're doomed either way."

"Maybe." Ren unrolled the second contract, the Mutual Defense Clause. "Or maybe we find a way to use his greed against him."

Li Chen leaned in. "What do you mean?"

"Huo wants to own us because he thinks we're valuable. He's right. But he's also wrong — he thinks our value comes from our current assets. Our temple. Our techniques. Our disciples."

Ren smiled, and it was the smile of a man who had nothing left to lose.

"But our real value," he continued, "is the *potential* to grow. To disrupt. To change the entire cultivation world's business model. Huo wants to control that potential. But if we move fast enough, grow quickly enough, we can become too big to control before his leash tightens."

Mei's eyes widened. "You're planning to grow so fast that Azure Pond can't keep up?"

"Exactly. By the time Huo realizes he's lost control, New Dao Sect will be too valuable to destroy. He'll have to let us run free, because the alternative is losing everything he invested."

"That's insane," Li Chen said.

"It's a startup." Ren's grin was feral. "We're supposed to move fast and break things. Including consortium contracts."

He picked up a brush, dipped it in ink, and signed both scrolls with a flourish.

Blood bloomed from the characters, sealing the contracts with spiritual binding.

Ren felt the Geomantic Seal take root in his chest, a cold weight settling over his spiritual sense. Azure Pond now had a window into his soul.

But they also had a shield.

"Let's go home," Ren said. "We have a lot of work to do."

They left the teahouse, stepping into the twilight streets of the Jade District.

None of them noticed the shadow that detached itself from a rooftop, following at a discreet distance.

None of them heard the whisper that carried on the wind, speaking directly into a communication talisman:

"The New Dao Sect has accepted. Master Huo's trap is set."

The voice on the other end laughed.

"Excellent. Tell Huo to proceed. And tell the Celestial Sword Sect that their little 'failed talent' is about to become very, very interesting."

The shadow vanished into the night.

And somewhere in the darkness, a plan far older and more dangerous than Ren Wei could imagine began to unfold.

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: THE LETTER FROM HEAVEN

Three weeks had passed since the Azure Pond investment.

Three weeks of construction, recruitment, and the kind of frantic energy that came from knowing you were racing against forces you couldn't see. Ren stood on the balcony of the New Dao Sect's new administrative building — still smelling of fresh timber and formation ink — and watched his sect grow.

Below, workers laid the foundation for what would become the East Wing. Disciples in matching white robes (the cheapest fabric Mei could find that didn't fall apart in rain) practiced formation diagrams in the courtyard. The drunk sword master, Old Xu, sat under a tree, a jug in one hand and a sword in the other, occasionally gesturing vaguely at students who had no idea if he was teaching or just hallucinating.

It was chaos. Beautiful, productive chaos.

"We've burned through thirty percent of the investment," Mei said, appearing at his side with a ledger that looked suspiciously bloodstained. "Two weeks ahead of schedule."

"That's... bad?"

"That's terrible. But I've already secured three more minor investors based on the construction progress. They see momentum. Momentum breeds confidence. Confidence breeds capital."

Ren turned to look at her. Mei's eyes had dark circles beneath them, and her robes were wrinkled — a sign of true stress, given she normally pressed them with wind techniques. "When do you sleep?"

"Sleep is inefficient. I've calculated that I can function on two hours of cultivation meditation per night for approximately six months before my organs start failing. By then, we'll either be successful or dead."

"Comforting."

"I thought so."

A shout rose from the courtyard. Ren leaned over the railing to see two disciples arguing over a formation diagram, their faces red, their gestures increasingly violent. Before he could intervene, Old Xu's sword flew from his hand without him moving, embedding itself in the ground between them. The disciples froze.

"Argue about the theory," Old Xu slurred, not opening his eyes. "Not the ego. The theory can be fixed. The ego requires decades of humility cultivation." He took a long drink. "Or a good concussion."

The disciples bowed and resumed their argument at a lower volume.

"He's actually good at this," Ren said, surprised.

"Drunk people tell the truth, and Old Xu's truth happens to be excellent sword advice," Mei replied. "I've considered bottling his breath and selling it as a cultivation aid."

"Please don't."

"Fine. But I'm marking that idea as 'future revenue stream.'"

A messenger approached from the main gate — a young man in the gray robes of the Imperial Courier Service, his face flushed from running. He carried a scroll sealed with golden wax, the mark of the Heavenly Court stamped into its surface.

Ren's blood ran cold.

"That's not good," Mei said quietly.

The messenger reached the base of the building and bowed. "Ren Wei of the New Dao Sect! I bear a summons from the Heavenly Court!"

The courtyard went silent. Even Old Xu opened one eye.

Ren descended the stairs, his legs moving on autopilot. He took the scroll from the messenger, noting the weight of it, the way it hummed with spiritual energy that made his fingers tingle. The seal was intact. Official. Unavoidable.

"Thank you," he said, his voice steady despite the chaos in his chest.

The messenger bowed again and departed, clearly eager to leave the suddenly tense atmosphere.

Ren broke the seal.

The scroll unfurled, revealing elegant calligraphy that seemed to glow with inner light. He read it once. Twice. Three times, because the words refused to make sense.

*By decree of the Heavenly Court:*

*The New Dao Sect is hereby recognized as an official cultivation sect under the jurisdiction of the Southern Territories. As such, it is required to send a representative to the upcoming Heaven's Mandate Conference, to be held at the Celestial Peak in three months' time.*

*Attendance is mandatory. Failure to appear will result in automatic dissolution of sect status and forfeiture of all assets.*

*Furthermore, the New Dao Sect has been selected to participate in the Trials of Foundation — a preliminary competition to determine which sects will receive additional Heavenly resources.*

*Representatives must be below the Core Formation stage. No exceptions.*

*Signed,*
*The Jade Emperor's Administrative Council*

"Let me see that," Mei said, snatching the scroll from his hands. She read it, her face cycling through shock, calculation, and finally a kind of resigned fury. "They're trying to kill us."

"That's a bit dramatic."

"No, it's practical. The Trials of Foundation are designed for established sects with deep benches of talent. They're going to send their best Qi Condensation and Foundation Establishment disciples — the ones who've been training for decades, who have access to spirit medicines and secret techniques and mentors who aren't perpetually drunk."

Old Xu had wandered over, his jug somehow still full. "She's right. The last time a new sect was invited to the Trials, they came back with three survivors out of twelve. The sect dissolved within a year."

"Three out of twelve," Ren repeated.

"Barely trained," Old Xu added. "Against sects that have been preparing for this for centuries. It's not a competition. It's a culling."

Mei's eyes narrowed. "The traditional sects want us gone. They couldn't crush us economically — I've structured our deals so that any direct attack would destabilize half the market. They couldn't assassinate us — too public, and we have allies now. But this? This is legal. This is tradition. They're using the system to eliminate us."

Ren took the scroll back, reading it again. "We could refuse."

"Then we're dissolved. All our assets seized. The investors lose everything. You think Huo would let that slide? He'd have us killed just to set an example."

"And if we attend?"

"Best case: we survive with heavy casualties. Worst case: we're erased completely."

Silence settled over the courtyard. The disciples had gathered, their earlier argument forgotten, their faces pale. They'd joined a startup sect, not a war. Most of them had been rejects from other sects, people with nowhere else to go. And now they might have nowhere to go forever.

Ren looked at them — at Mei's exhausted determination, at Old Xu's drunken wisdom, at the faces of disciples who had believed in his vision.

"Three months," he said.

"What?" Mei asked.

"We have three months until the Trials. That's ninety days. We've built this sect from nothing in less time than that. We can prepare."

"Ren, we have three Qi Condensation disciples. One of them is a chef who cultivates by accident. Our strongest fighter is a drunk who refuses to sober up. We don't have a single Foundation Establishment cultivator."

Ren turned to Old Xu. "How long would it take you to train a disciple to Foundation Establishment?"

Old Xu took a long drink. "Normally? Ten to fifteen years, depending on talent."

"And if you had unlimited resources? Spirit stones, medicines, techniques?"

"Six months. Maybe five. But the body needs time to adapt. You can't rush cultivation without breaking something."

"Then we break a few things."

Mei stared at him. "You're serious."

"I'm desperate. There's a difference." Ren looked at the scroll again, at the golden seal that represented the entire cultivation world's power structure. "They invited us because they think we're weak. Because they think we'll fail. Because they think we're just another startup that will burn out and disappear."

He rolled the scroll, tucking it into his robe.

"They're wrong."

---

Three days later, Ren sat in his office, surrounded by maps of the Celestial Peak, historical records of past Trials, and a growing pile of notes that looked like the ravings of a madman.

Mei had thrown herself into logistics, calculating resource requirements with the intensity of a general planning a campaign. Old Xu had actually stopped drinking — partially, at least — and had begun running the disciples through drills that looked less like training and more like medieval torture.

And Ren had made a decision.

He reached into his robe and pulled out a small jade token, one he'd kept hidden since his days at the Celestial Sword Sect. It was cracked, faded, and pulsed with a weak spiritual light.

A contact. Someone who owed him a favor. Someone who operated in the shadows between sects.

He pressed the token, and a voice echoed in his mind — rough, amused, and slightly drunk.

"Well, well. The failed disciple finally calls."

"I need help," Ren said.

"Everyone needs help. The question is: what are you willing to pay?"

Ren thought of his sect, his disciples, the dream he'd built from nothing.

"Everything."

The voice laughed, a sound like grinding stones.

"Then we have a deal. Meet me at the Black Lotus Pavilion in three days. Come alone. And Ren?"

"Yes?"

"Try not to die before then. I hate wasted investments."

The connection cut.

Ren stared at the token, his heart pounding.

He had no idea who he'd just called.

He only knew that he'd just made a deal with someone who operated outside the Heavenly Court's reach — and that if the Heavenly Court ever found out, the New Dao Sect wouldn't just be dissolved.

It would be erased.

The door opened. Mei walked in, her face pale.

"We have a problem."

"Just one?"

"The Azure Pond Consortium is pulling out."

Ren's blood turned to ice. "What?"

"Huo's representative just arrived. They're citing 'unforeseen market instability' and 'concerns about the upcoming Trials.' They're demanding immediate repayment of the investment, plus penalties."

"How much?"

Mei's voice was barely a whisper. "Everything we have. Plus the building. Plus the land. Plus our future cultivation rights for the next ten years."

Ren sat back, the room spinning around him.

They'd been betrayed. Not by an enemy — by their own investor. Huo had seen the Trials invitation as an exit opportunity. He'd invested in a startup, and now he was cashing out while the value was still high.

The worst part?

It was completely legal.

"Ren," Mei said, her voice cracking for the first time since he'd known her, "we're going to lose everything."

Ren looked at the jade token in his hand, then at the scroll from the Heavenly Court, then at Mei's terrified face.

"No," he said, standing up. "We're not."

He walked to the door.

"Where are you going?"

"To make a deal with the devil."

And he left, the door swinging shut behind him, leaving Mei alone in the office with the maps, the notes, and the growing sense that their startup was about to become a funeral.

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER 16: The Price of Truth

The negotiation lasted three hours.

Ren left the study with a splitting headache, a signed contract, and the distinct feeling that he'd just made a deal with a demon who wore a scholar's mask. Consortium Master Huo had agreed to everything—the initial investment, the resource pipeline, even the profit-sharing structure that favored New Dao Sect for the first two years.

Too easily.

"Congratulations," Huo had said, shaking his hand with a grip that felt like iron wrapped in silk. "I look forward to a long and profitable partnership."

Ren had smiled. Nodded. Walked out.

Now, standing in the reception area with Mei and Li Chen, he felt the weight of that handshake like a shackle.

"You look like someone who just sold his soul," Mei observed.

"Maybe I did."

Li Chen sniffed the air. "You smell like expensive tea and bad intentions. What happened in there?"

"He agreed to everything."

Silence.

"That's bad," Li Chen said.

"Very bad," Mei agreed. "What did you miss?"

Ren pulled out the contract—a scroll of spirit-silk that shimmered with binding formations. "I've read it five times. The terms are fair. The exit clauses are standard. There's nothing obviously wrong."

"Obviously," Mei repeated. "That's the problem."

They left the Azure Pavilion in silence, walking through the Jade District's pristine streets. Mortals and cultivators alike parted around them, sensing the tension radiating from the trio. A vendor selling spirit fruits took one look at Mei's face and decided to suddenly close his stall.

Back at the temple—still crumbling, still humble, but now *theirs*—Ren spread the contract on the main altar and summoned every disciple.

Master Zhuang arrived last, still clutching a gourd of wine. "You called, Sect Leader? I was in the middle of a very important meditation on the nature of impermanence."

"You were drinking."

"Impermanence is thirsty work."

Ren ignored him. "We have funding. Consortium Azure Pond has agreed to provide five thousand low-grade spirit stones monthly, with access to their resource network and distribution channels. In exchange, we give them thirty percent of gross revenue and right of first refusal on any future investment rounds."

The disciples exchanged glances.

"That's... good?" Little Feng asked, her voice uncertain.

"Too good," Master Zhuang said, suddenly sober. "Huo Guang doesn't make deals that benefit anyone but himself. I've heard stories—"

"What kind of stories?" Ren asked.

Master Zhuang took a long drink. "The kind that end with ambitious young sect leaders found dead in alleys, their sects absorbed into Azure Pond's portfolio. He's a shark, Ren. A very patient, very polite shark."

"Then why did he agree to our terms?"

No one had an answer.

The answer came three days later.

Ren was in the middle of a training session—teaching Little Feng the basics of sword forms while trying not to wince at her terrible footwork—when a messenger arrived. A young woman in Azure Pond colors, her expression professionally blank.

"Sect Leader Ren. Consortium Master Huo requests your presence at the Jade Pavilion for a ceremonial signing. All major sects in the region have been invited to witness the partnership."

The blood drained from Ren's face.

"All major sects?"

"Of course. It would be improper to formalize such an important alliance without proper witnesses. The Celestial Sword Sect has already confirmed their attendance. As has the Crimson Phoenix Sect, the Golden Lotus Temple, and the Black Wind Valley."

Ren's hand tightened around his practice sword. "I see."

"The ceremony is in three days. Consortium Master Huo looks forward to seeing you there."

She bowed and left.

Mei was at his side before the messenger had cleared the temple gates. "He's going to humiliate you in front of everyone."

"Worse. He's going to make an example of me. A public alliance with Azure Pond gives me legitimacy, but it also makes me a target. Every major sect in the region will see New Dao Sect as a threat—or a puppet."

"Which one are we?"

"Both. Neither. I don't know." Ren sat down heavily on a broken stone step. "This is a trap, but I can't see the teeth."

Over the next three days, Ren did the only thing he could: he prepared.

Mei worked the information network, bribing servants and guards for any scrap of intelligence about Huo's plans. Li Chen disappeared into the city's underworld, drinking with information brokers and playing cards with men who'd kill for a copper. Master Zhuang, surprisingly, proved useful—his decades of wandering had left him with contacts in unexpected places.

The picture that emerged was worse than Ren feared.

"Huo has been buying up debt from smaller sects for the past year," Mei reported on the second night, her face pale in the candlelight. "He's got hooks in half the independent cultivators in the region. If you default on your contract—"

"He owns us."

"Worse than that. He owns the *idea* of you. The contract has a clause that lets him license the 'New Dao methodology' to other sects if you fail to meet performance targets."

Ren felt sick. "I read the contract. That clause wasn't there."

"It's in the fine print. Literally. The characters are so small you'd need a magnifying formation to read them. I found it because I was looking for it."

"How do you know to look for something like that?"

Mei smiled, and it was not a pleasant smile. "Because my father did the same thing to his business partners. I recognize the pattern."

On the third day, Ren made a decision.

He gathered his disciples in the main hall—if you could call a room with three walls and a leaky roof a hall. The morning light filtered through holes in the ceiling, casting strange patterns on the floor.

"I'm going to the ceremony," he said.

"Sect Leader—" Little Feng started.

"I know it's a trap. But if I don't go, Huo wins. He'll spread word that New Dao Sect is afraid, that we're not serious players. The investment will dry up. We'll be finished before we've truly begun."

"Or," Master Zhuang said, "you go, and Huo makes a public example of you. Either way, you lose."

Ren nodded. "Unless I change the game."

He pulled out a second scroll—one he'd been working on all night. "This is a counter-proposal. A *public* counter-proposal. I'm going to stand up at the ceremony and offer terms that Huo can't accept without losing face, and can't reject without looking weak."

"What terms?" Mei asked.

"A public audit of Azure Pond's investment portfolio. Full transparency on their relationships with other sects. A mutual performance clause that applies to both parties equally. And—" Ren took a breath. "A challenge. If Huo wants to control New Dao Sect, he'll have to defeat me in single combat first."

The disciples stared at him.

"You're challenging a consortium master to a fight?" Little Feng's voice was barely a whisper. "Sect Leader, he's at the peak of Core Formation. You're—"

"Mid Qi Condensation. I know."

"He'll kill you."

"Maybe. But he won't do it publicly, in front of every major sect leader in the region. That would destroy his reputation. He needs to appear magnanimous. Reasonable. The aggrieved party."

Master Zhuang laughed—a bitter, broken sound. "You're betting your life on social pressure."

"I'm betting my life that Huo values his reputation more than my death. It's a different kind of cultivation, disciples. Not of qi, but of relationships. Of appearances. Of *face*."

Ren rolled up both scrolls and tucked them into his robes. "Prepare the temple. If I don't come back, Mei is in charge. Continue the work. Don't let New Dao Sect die."

He walked out before anyone could argue.

The Jade Pavilion was even more opulent than before. Lanterns of captured starlight hung from the ceiling. Tables groaned under the weight of spirit fruits and immortal delicacies. And everywhere—*everywhere*—were cultivators in the colors of the great sects.

Ren spotted them immediately. The Celestial Sword Sect delegation, led by Elder Wu, the man who'd fired him. The Crimson Phoenix Sect, their robes blazing like fire. The Golden Lotus Temple, serene and watchful. Black Wind Valley, dark and predatory.

And at the center of it all, Consortium Master Huo, smiling like a benevolent uncle.

"Ren Wei! Welcome, welcome." Huo spread his arms. "I'm so glad you could make it. Everyone, this is the young man I was telling you about. The founder of New Dao Sect. A visionary, truly."

The crowd turned to look at him.

Ren felt their gazes like physical weight. Assessment. Dismissal. Amusement. A few—very few—looked curious.

He walked forward, his heart hammering, his face calm.

"Consortium Master Huo. Thank you for the invitation. I have something I'd like to share with everyone before we proceed with the ceremony."

Huo's smile flickered. "Oh?"

Ren pulled out the second scroll. "A revised proposal. One that I believe will benefit not just Azure Pond and New Dao Sect, but every sect present today."

The room went silent.

Elder Wu's eyes narrowed. A Crimson Phoenix elder leaned forward, interest sparking in her gaze.

Ren unrolled the scroll and began to read.

He spoke of transparency. Of mutual accountability. Of a new model for sect-investor relationships that didn't rely on hidden clauses and debt traps. He spoke of challenges and opportunities, of the future of cultivation in a changing world.

And then he delivered the final line.

"To prove my commitment to this vision, I challenge Consortium Master Huo to a duel. Not to the death—simply to first blood. If I win, these terms are adopted. If I lose, I accept the original contract without modification."

The silence that followed was absolute.

Huo's face had gone very still. His eyes, usually warm and grandfatherly, had turned to chips of ice.

"You would challenge me. In my own pavilion. Before my guests."

"I would offer you a choice," Ren said, his voice steady despite the trembling in his hands. "Accept the challenge, and prove your commitment to fair dealing. Decline, and everyone here will know why."

The trap had teeth now.

And they were all pointed at Ren.

Huo stared at him for a long moment. Then, slowly, impossibly, he began to laugh.

"Young man," he said, "you have more courage than sense. But courage is its own kind of currency." He turned to the assembled crowd. "Very well. I accept your challenge. Let us see if your dao is as sharp as your tongue."

The crowd erupted.

And Ren realized, with cold certainty, that he had just made the biggest mistake of his life.

Because Huo wasn't afraid.

He was *delighted*.

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: The Little Girl Who Held Their Fate

The negotiation lasted six hours.

Ren emerged from the study with a parchment contract, a splitting headache, and the distinct feeling that he'd just sold his soul to a very polite demon. Consortium Master Huo had been pleasant throughout—offering tea, making small talk about market trends, complimenting Ren's "innovative approach to traditional cultivation structures."

He had also extracted concessions that made Ren's stomach turn.

"Thirty percent of future revenue? For a seed investment of five thousand spirit stones?" Mei had shrieked when she saw the contract. "Please tell me you negotiated."

"I got it down to twenty-five."

"Twenty-five percent of our *future*? For five thousand spirit stones? That's highway robbery!"

"It's venture capital," Ren said, rubbing his temples. "Welcome to the cultivation world's first term sheet."

They'd spent the rest of the day arguing. Li Chen, who had been drinking with the city guards, stumbled back to their rented room with useful intelligence: the Azure Pond Consortium was indeed legitimate, but they had a reputation for aggressive terms. "They call Huo the 'Smiling Butcher,'" Li Chen slurred. "Because he'll carve you up while complimenting your technique."

"Wonderful," Mei said. "We're in business with a serial killer."

"He's not a killer," Ren said. "He's a financier. There's a difference."

"The difference is paperwork."

Despite Mei's protests, the contract was signed. The New Dao Sect officially had its first institutional investor. Ren felt like he should be celebrating, but instead he felt like he'd just taken out a massive loan with his soul as collateral.

The first payment—two thousand spirit stones, with the rest coming in quarterly installments—arrived the next morning via courier. A jade box containing spirit stones that glowed with such pure energy that even Li Chen's eyes went wide.

"This is real," the drunk sword master said, holding a stone up to the light. "This is enough to buy a small mountain."

"It's enough to build one," Ren said. "Come on. We've got work to do."

---

The next three months were a blur of construction, recruitment, and the kind of administrative hell that Ren had never imagined when he dreamed of founding his own sect.

They hired mortal craftsmen to repair the temple's physical structure—new roof tiles, reinforced walls, a proper gate that didn't look like it would fall over if someone sneezed too hard. Formation experts (not the gambling addict variety) to establish basic defensive arrays. A cook who wasn't trying to accidentally cultivate through culinary experimentation.

The mortal disciples started arriving.

Word had spread through the merchant networks that the New Dao Sect was accepting "non-traditional applicants." The first wave was a motley collection: a failed scholar whose family had run out of money for his education; a former soldier with a crippled leg who could no longer serve; a young woman whose spiritual roots were too weak for any major sect to bother with.

Ren interviewed each one personally.

"Your spiritual root is rated at three percent efficiency," he told the young woman, whose name was Lin. "The Celestial Sword Sect would have rejected you before you finished your first sentence."

Lin's face fell. "I know. But I heard..."

"I'm not done," Ren said, holding up a hand. "The Celestial Sword Sect is wrong. Spiritual root efficiency measures one thing: how quickly you can absorb ambient qi. It doesn't measure intelligence, determination, or creativity. It doesn't measure how well you work in a team, or how you handle pressure, or whether you can think your way out of a problem when brute force fails."

Lin stared at him.

"The New Dao Sect doesn't care about your spiritual root," Ren continued. "We care about your potential. Can you learn? Can you adapt? Can you contribute to something larger than yourself?"

"Yes," she whispered. "Yes, I can."

"Good. Welcome to the sect. Your first assignment is inventory management. Mei will explain the system."

Mei's system was, of course, a spreadsheet so complex that it took Lin three days to understand it. But once she did, she ran it with terrifying efficiency, tracking every spirit stone, every resource, every disciple's contribution with the precision of a military quartermaster.

The former soldier—a man named Zhang Wei who had lost his leg to a demon beast attack—became the sect's first security officer. He couldn't fight anymore, but he could organize. He set up patrol routes, watch schedules, and a reporting system that meant Ren knew about any problem within fifteen minutes of it occurring.

The failed scholar—Chen Yu—was put to work on research. Specifically, researching the Azure Pond Consortium's other investments.

"If we're going to be partners with these people," Ren told him, "I want to know exactly who else they're partners with. And how those partnerships ended."

Chen Yu took to the task with the enthusiasm of a man who had something to prove. Within a week, he'd produced a dossier that made Ren's blood run cold.

The Azure Pond Consortium had invested in seventeen "startup sects" over the past decade.

All seventeen had failed.

More specifically, all seventeen had been absorbed by the consortium, their assets seized, their founders either dead or working as indentured cultivators for Azure Pond's other holdings.

"The pattern is always the same," Chen Yu said, his voice tight. "Initial investment, followed by 'management consulting' that gradually takes over operations. Then the consortium calls in a debt—some clause in the contract that the founder didn't read carefully enough—and suddenly the sect belongs to them."

"Show me the clause," Ren said.

Chen Yu pointed to a section of the contract that Ren had skimmed over during the six-hour negotiation. It was buried in a paragraph about "standard accounting procedures" and "audit rights."

'In the event that the invested entity fails to achieve specified growth metrics for two consecutive quarters, the investor reserves the right to assume operational control until such metrics are achieved.'

"Two quarters," Ren said. "That's six months. We've got three months left before the first audit."

"Three months to hit our targets," Mei said, appearing in the doorway. She'd been listening. Of course she had. "What are the targets?"

Ren pulled out the contract and read the fine print. His face went pale.

"Revenue of ten thousand spirit stones per quarter. A minimum of fifty active disciples. And at least one 'significant breakthrough in cultivation methodology' that can be patented and licensed."

"Ten thousand spirit stones?" Mei's voice rose to a shriek. "We're barely making two thousand! And we have twelve disciples, not fifty! And what the hell is a 'significant breakthrough' supposed to mean?"

"I don't know," Ren admitted. "I think Huo set us up to fail."

"Of course he did! He's the Smiling Butcher! He doesn't invest in success—he invests in failures he can eat!"

They were still arguing when Li Chen burst into the room, his eyes wild, his hand on his sword.

"We've got company," he said.

"What kind of company?"

"The kind that flies on swords and wears matching uniforms. Celestial Sword Sect. A delegation of twelve, headed by someone who looks very angry."

Ren's heart stopped.

The Celestial Sword Sect had found him.

"Everyone stay calm," he said, even as his hands started shaking. "Mei, hide the contract. Chen Yu, get the disciples into the basement. Li Chen..."

"Yes?"

"Don't start a fight unless I give the signal."

"And if you give the signal?"

Ren's eyes hardened. "Then we show them what a startup sect can do when it's cornered."

He walked out to meet his former employers, his heart pounding, his mind racing, already calculating the odds.

Twelve Celestial Sword cultivators, minimum Qi Condensation level, probably led by a Core Formation elder.

Against one Qi Condensation cripple, a drunk sword master, a merchant with a spreadsheet, and twelve mortal disciples.

The odds were terrible.

But Ren had never let terrible odds stop him before.

As he stepped through the temple gates, the leader of the delegation descended from his flying sword. He was tall, handsome in that generic cultivator way, with robes that probably cost more than Ren's entire sect.

When he spoke, his voice was ice.

"Ren Wei. By order of the Celestial Sword Sect's Grand Council, you are hereby charged with theft of sect property, unauthorized use of sect techniques, and founding a rival organization without permission. Surrender now, and your punishment will be... lenient."

"And if I don't surrender?"

The cultivator smiled. It was not a pleasant smile.

"Then we'll burn this pathetic little temple to the ground, with you inside it."

Ren looked at his sect—his crumbling temple, his misfit disciples, his impossible dream.

Then he looked at the man who had come to destroy it all.

"You know," Ren said, "I've been having a really bad week. A really, really bad week. And I've been trying to be patient, trying to be diplomatic, trying to play by the rules."

He took a step forward.

"But here's the thing about startups. We're used to failing. We're used to things going wrong. We're used to being told we can't do something."

Another step.

"So when someone shows up and threatens to destroy everything we've built, we don't back down. We don't surrender."

Another step. He was close enough to see the cultivator's eyes now. Close enough to see the faint flicker of uncertainty there.

"We pivot."

He raised his hand. Behind him, he heard the sound of twelve mortals chanting in unison, their voices shaky but determined.

They had been practicing this. Every day. For three months.

"Formation activation," Ren said. "Sequence Alpha. Target: the flying cultivator with the expensive robes."

The ground beneath the Celestial Sword delegation lit up with a formation so crude, so hastily constructed, so utterly unorthodox, that it took them a full three seconds to realize what was happening.

By then, it was too late.

The formation didn't attack them. It didn't trap them. It didn't even harm them.

It simply... transported them.

Thirty feet into the air.

Directly above the sect's newly constructed sewage treatment pond.

Ren watched as twelve of the Celestial Sword Sect's finest cultivators plummeted into the fetid water, their pristine robes ruined, their dignity shattered.

"That," he said, "is for firing me."

He turned and walked back into the temple, leaving the sound of sputtering, furious cultivators behind him.

"We need to pack," he said to Mei, who was staring at him with a mixture of horror and admiration. "We've got about five minutes before they dig themselves out, and then they're going to be very, very angry."

"Where are we going to go?"

Ren smiled. It was not a pleasant smile.

"We're going to find Consortium Master Huo. And we're going to renegotiate our contract."

"With what leverage?"

"With the knowledge that we just humiliated the Celestial Sword Sect's delegation. By the time the sun sets, every sect in the region is going to know about what happened here. And every sect is going to want to know who these crazy people are who just dumped twelve Celestial Sword cultivators in a sewage pond."

Mei's eyes lit up. "Marketing. That's brilliant."

"No," Ren said. "That's survival. They're the same thing in this business."

As they scrambled to pack, the sounds of angry cultivators rising from the sewage pond behind them, Ren allowed himself a moment of satisfaction.

The New Dao Sect was still standing.

Barely.

But standing.

And in the cultivation world's startup ecosystem, that was already more than most could claim.

title: The Mountain's Dark Secret
content:
The meeting with Consortium Master Huo had ended better than Ren had hoped. A tentative agreement. A small investment. A foothold.

That had been three weeks ago.

Now, standing at the edge of the Spirit Root Mountain range, Ren watched his disciples set up camp and wondered if he'd made a terrible mistake.

"The formation is unstable," Little Feng announced, her fingers tracing patterns in the air. "Something is disrupting the spiritual energy flow. It's like the mountain is... breathing wrong."

"Breathing wrong?" Mei looked up from the cooking fire she was struggling to start. "That's your technical assessment?"

"I'm a formation prodigy, not a poet. Give me a break."

Ren rubbed his temples. The Azure Pond investment had come with strings attached—the largest being that New Dao Sect needed to secure a proper cultivation site within three months. Consortium Master Huo had been generous with spirit stones but ruthless with timelines.

And this mountain, supposedly abandoned for centuries, was their best option.

"We should scout ahead," Li Chen said, appearing silently beside Ren. The drunk sword master looked unusually sober. "I don't like this place. The qi feels... wrong."

"Wrong how?"

"Like it's been tampered with. Artificially altered."

Ren considered this. Tampered spiritual energy could mean anything from a broken formation to a hidden sect to something far worse. But they needed answers, and they needed them fast.

"Fine. You and I will scout. Mei, keep the fire going. Little Feng, stabilize whatever you can. We'll be back before dark."

The mountain path wound upward through ancient trees that seemed to lean toward them, watching. The air grew thick, heavy, carrying a faint metallic taste that Ren couldn't place.

"Smell that?" Li Chen asked.

"Blood. Old blood. And something else."

"Soul stones."

Ren stopped. Soul stones were illegal. They were used in forbidden cultivation techniques that drained the life essence of living beings. The Celestial Sword Sect had executed three elders for using them just last year.

"Are you sure?"

"I've smelled enough death to recognize it." Li Chen's hand moved to his sword. "We should go back."

"Not yet. We need to know what we're dealing with."

They pressed forward, the trees thinning to reveal a clearing. And in that clearing, a structure that made Ren's blood run cold.

It was a pagoda, seven stories tall, built from black stone that seemed to absorb light. The walls were covered in formation scripts—not the elegant patterns of legitimate cultivation, but the jagged, hungry symbols of blood cultivation. The ground around it was dead, the soil black and cracked.

And at the pagoda's base, a corpse.

Fresh. Still bleeding.

"Someone got here before us," Li Chen whispered.

Ren approached carefully, his spiritual sense extended. The corpse was a man in his forties, wearing robes that marked him as a scout for the Azure Pond Consortium. His eyes were wide open, his mouth frozen in a scream, his chest torn open.

His heart was missing.

"This is bad," Ren said. "Really, really bad."

"Understatement of the century." Li Chen drew his sword. "The Azure Pond scout was investigating the same site we're supposed to claim. If the Consortium finds out there's a blood cultivator operating here..."

"They'll think we're involved. Or that we concealed evidence. Either way, our investment disappears and we become enemies of one of the most powerful financial organizations in the region."

A sound came from the pagoda. A scraping, dragging sound. Like something heavy being pulled across stone.

Ren and Li Chen shared a look.

"Run?" Li Chen asked.

"Run."

They turned and sprinted down the mountain, the sound growing louder behind them. Branches whipped at their faces. Roots tried to trip them. The air grew colder, the metallic taste stronger.

They burst into the camp, gasping, and Ren immediately started shouting.

"Pack up! Now! We're leaving!"

"What? Why?" Mei stood, her cooking fire forgotten.

"Blood cultivator. Dead Azure Pond scout. We need to—"

The ground shook. A massive crack split the earth between them, sending them stumbling. From the crevice, a voice emerged—ancient, dry, and amused.

"Leaving so soon? And here I thought you were my new neighbors."

Ren turned slowly. A figure stood at the edge of the clearing, wrapped in robes of deepest black, his face hidden by a hood. But his eyes were visible. They glowed red.

And in his hand, he held a beating heart.

"The Azure Pond scout was rude," the figure said, his voice carrying a smile. "He arrived without an invitation. Didn't even knock. I had to teach him manners."

Ren's mind raced. This was a Foundation Establishment cultivator at least. Possibly higher. Against a Qi Condensation sect leader, a drunk sword master, a gambling formation expert, and a chef who'd accidentally cultivated once.

The odds were terrible.

"We don't want trouble," Ren said, keeping his voice steady. "This mountain is unclaimed. If you've established a residence here, we'll find somewhere else."

"Oh, how generous." The figure stepped closer, and the temperature dropped. "But I'm afraid I can't allow that. You've seen my pagoda. You've seen my... collection. Word gets out, and I'll have the Azure Pond Consortium, the Celestial Sword Sect, and a dozen other factions tearing down my door."

"We won't tell anyone."

"Of course you won't. Because you'll be dead."

The blood cultivator raised his hand, and the ground beneath them began to crack. Red light pulsed from the fissures, hungry and eager.

"Little Feng!" Ren shouted. "Formation! Now!"

The girl's hands moved faster than Ren had ever seen, throwing spirit stones into a pattern that erupted into golden light. The cracks in the earth stopped spreading, and the blood cultivator stumbled back, his red eyes widening in surprise.

"A formation prodigy? Interesting." He laughed, a dry, rasping sound. "I haven't had a challenge in decades. This will be fun."

Li Chen attacked without warning, his sword blazing with qi. The blade struck the blood cultivator's defenses and shattered, sending shards of metal flying. Li Chen flew backward, crashing into a tree.

"Your sword is weak," the cultivator said. "Your qi is thin. Your sect is a joke."

Mei threw a pot of boiling oil at him. He didn't even bother to dodge—the oil evaporated before it touched his robes.

Ren had exactly one option left.

"New Dao Sect! Formation of the Eternal Scholar!"

His disciples looked at him like he'd grown a second head.

"Boss, we don't have a formation called that," Little Feng said.

"We do now. Follow my lead and pretend we know what we're doing."

Ren began making complicated hand gestures, completely made up on the spot. His disciples, to their credit, did the same. They looked ridiculous. Embarrassing. Like a group of street performers who'd never learned the routine.

The blood cultivator paused, his head tilted.

"What trickery is this?"

"No trickery," Ren said, pouring every ounce of confidence he had into his voice. "This is the secret technique of the New Dao Sect. Passed down through generations of scholars. It detects deception in the spiritual realm."

"Impossible."

"Is it?" Ren's hands kept moving. "I can see it now. The corruption in your core. The stolen lives screaming in your dantian. The debt you owe to the heavens."

The blood cultivator's eyes narrowed.

"You're bluffing."

"Am I?"

For a long, terrible moment, no one moved. The blood cultivator's gaze bored into Ren, searching for weakness. Ren stared back, his heart pounding so hard he was sure it would burst.

Then the cultivator smiled.

"You're interesting, little sect leader. I haven't been entertained in a long time." He threw something at Ren's feet. The Azure Pond scout's jade token, still wet with blood. "Keep the mountain. Consider it a gift."

"I don't understand."

"You will. Soon." He turned, walking back toward the pagoda. "But remember this, Ren Wei of the New Dao Sect: I know your face. I know your scent. And if you betray me, I will find everyone you've ever loved and make them watch as I devour your soul."

He disappeared into the pagoda, and the ground sealed behind him.

Ren collapsed to his knees, gasping. His disciples rushed to him.

"What the hell was that?" Mei demanded. "What was that 'formation'?"

"I made it up. On the spot. Using nothing but desperation and the power of looking confident."

"You're insane."

"Probably."

Li Chen limped over, his broken sword still in his hand. "We can't stay here. He'll change his mind. Or he'll decide we're too much of a risk."

"No," Ren said slowly, picking up the jade token. "We *have* to stay."

"Why?"

"Because Consortium Master Huo sent that scout. Which means he knew about this place. Which means he sent us here deliberately."

Realization dawned on Mei's face.

"He wanted us to find the blood cultivator."

"He wanted us to either die or make a deal with him." Ren's hands were shaking, but his voice was steady. "The Azure Pond Consortium isn't just investing in us. They're testing us. And if we run now, we fail."

"So what do we do?"

Ren looked at the jade token, then at the pagoda, then at his disciples—his broken, beautiful, disastrous disciples.

"We do what we always do," he said. "We treat this like a business problem."

"Explain."

"The blood cultivator has a resource—this mountain, with its tampered spiritual energy. The Azure Pond Consortium wants access to that resource but can't be seen dealing with a criminal. We're the middlemen. The plausible deniability."

"You want to partner with a murderer."

"I want to survive." Ren's eyes hardened. "And if that means making a deal with the devil, then I'll negotiate the best damn terms anyone's ever seen."

He stood, brushing the dirt off his robes, and started walking toward the pagoda.

His disciples followed, because they were idiots, because they believed in him, because they had nowhere else to go.

And because, against all reason, they thought he might actually pull this off.

Behind them, the blood cultivator's laughter echoed through the mountain, patient and hungry.

The negotiation was about to begin.

title: Chapter 19
content:
CHAPTER 19: The Due Diligence Nightmare

The negotiations lasted three hours.

Ren emerged from the study with a headache, a signed memorandum of understanding, and the distinct feeling he'd just made a deal with a very patient snake. Consortium Master Huo had agreed to a "trial investment" — ten thousand spirit stones up front, with another forty thousand contingent on meeting specific milestones within six months.

The terms were brutal. Ren had accepted them anyway.

"You look like someone who just sold his soul," Mei observed as they walked through the Jade District's pristine streets.

"I sold a third of my sect instead. Same thing, different paperwork."

Li Chen fell into step beside them, reeking of wine and information. "I've got news. Bad news, mostly. Want the good news first?"

"No."

"Too bad. Good news: the Azure Consortium isn't just a money lender. They have connections in the Heavenly Assembly. If we actually succeed, we might get official recognition faster than expected."

"And the bad news?"

"The Heavenly Assembly just passed a new regulation. All new sects must undergo a 'Compliance Review' within thirty days of their founding. Failure results in immediate dissolution."

Ren stopped walking. "We've been operating for three months."

"Exactly. The regulation is retroactive."

Mei's face went pale. "They're targeting us specifically. Someone in the Assembly must know about New Dao Sect."

"The Celestial Sword Sect," Ren said, the pieces clicking together. "They didn't just fire me. They've been watching. Waiting to see if I'd actually try to compete. Now that I've gotten real funding, they're making their move."

Li Chen nodded grimly. "The Compliance Review requires a full audit of your sect's resources, personnel, cultivation methods, and financial records. If anything is irregular — and everything about us is irregular — they can shut us down."

"How long do we have?"

"Twenty-seven days."

---

They returned to the temple to find the chef, Old Zhang, arguing with a chicken.

"I told you," Zhang said, pointing at the bird with a cleaver, "you're supposed to be dinner. Stop trying to cultivate."

The chicken, a scrawny thing with feathers that had somehow taken on a faint golden sheen, was perched on the temple's broken bell, glaring at Zhang with unsettling intelligence.

"It's been eating the spirit grain," Mei said, sighing. "I think it's actually forming a core."

"We're not having a cultivator chicken," Ren said flatly.

"Too late," Li Chen said. "I checked its aura. It's at Qi Condensation, first level. It's technically a monstrous beast now."

The chicken clucked smugly.

Ren put his head in his hands. "Twenty-seven days. We have twenty-seven days to turn a crumbling temple, a drunk, a gambler, a chef, and a chicken into a legitimate sect that can pass a Heavenly Assembly audit."

"The drunk has a name," Li Chen muttered.

"I know. I'm using it for dramatic effect."

They gathered in the main hall — the only room with a complete roof — and spread out their resources on a cracked stone table. Ten thousand spirit stones in a storage ring. A signed contract with the Azure Consortium. A chicken that had somehow achieved enlightenment before any of them.

"We need to prioritize," Mei said, her business instincts taking over. "The Compliance Review will check four things: cultivation resources, personnel records, financial transparency, and sect governance structure. We fail any one, we're done."

"Our cultivation resources are a joke," Li Chen said. "We have one formation manual that I stole, half a sword technique that Ren remembers from his Celestial Sword days, and a recipe for braised pork that Old Zhang claims is a cultivation method."

"It is!" Zhang protested. "I reached Qi Condensation through my cooking. That's valid!"

"The chicken did too," Ren pointed out. "That doesn't mean the Assembly will accept 'accidentally eating spiritual ingredients' as a legitimate cultivation path."

Mei pulled out a piece of charcoal and started writing on the wall. "First problem: we need a proper cultivation method. Something that looks official. Second problem: we need personnel files for all disciples, including cultivation history and background checks. Third problem: we need financial records that show our funding came from legitimate sources."

"The Azure Consortium investment covers the last one," Ren said. "The contract is legal. But we need to show how we're spending the money."

"And the biggest problem?" Li Chen asked.

Mei drew a circle around a single word: GOVERNANCE.

"Sects are supposed to have a hierarchy. A founding master, elder positions, disciple ranks, clear promotion paths. We have... whatever this is."

"We have me," Ren said. "Founder and sect master."

"And that's fine for the top. But we need at least three elders to form a governing council. The Assembly requires it for official recognition."

Li Chen raised his hand. "I'm not being an elder. I don't do meetings."

"Too bad. You're the formation expert. That makes you the Array Elder."

"What about the drunk part? Is that in the job description?"

"It can be. Cultivators are eccentric. It's practically expected."

Old Zhang coughed. "I suppose I'm the Alchemy Elder?"

"You're the Culinary Elder," Ren said. "It's a legitimate field. The Gourmet Sect in the Eastern Provinces has twelve branches."

"That's actually true," Li Chen said, surprised. "I met one of their disciples once. He tried to cook my sword."

"And the chicken?" Mei asked.

They all looked at the chicken, which had followed them inside and was now pecking at a crack in the floor.

"The chicken is the Guardian Beast," Ren said, with no small amount of desperation. "It guards the sect's spiritual grain stores."

"That's actually not bad," Mei admitted. "Guardian beasts are a standard sect feature. We just need to register it and get it a spirit beast contract."

"I'm not signing a contract with a chicken."

"You might have to. The Assembly will want proof of ownership."

The chicken clucked, and somehow it sounded offended.

---

The next three weeks were a blur of bureaucratic nightmare.

Ren wrote and rewrote the sect charter seventeen times, each version more absurdly formal than the last. He invented a founding history, a set of core philosophies, and a three-hundred-year plan for sect development that was entirely fictional but beautifully formatted.

Li Chen, under protest, designed a formation array for the temple that would qualify as a "sect protection formation" — a simple spiritual barrier that looked impressive but offered about as much protection as a paper umbrella. He spent two days arguing with the chicken about where to place the formation nodes.

"You're not a formation master," he told the chicken. "Stop trying to help."

The chicken pecked at the ground, tracing a surprisingly accurate reinforcement rune.

Li Chen stared at it. "That's... actually correct."

The chicken clucked smugly again.

Old Zhang focused on creating a "sect cuisine archive" — a collection of recipes that he claimed were cultivation methods. He wrote them on spirit parchment, complete with detailed diagrams and cultivation effects. Most of them were variations on braised pork.

Mei handled the finances. She created ledgers, expense reports, and a five-year projection that showed New Dao Sect becoming the most profitable sect in the Eastern Provinces by year three. She was careful to make the numbers optimistic but not impossible — ambitious enough to impress the reviewers, realistic enough to be believed.

"This is fraud," Li Chen said, looking at the projections.

"It's creative accounting," Mei corrected. "There's a difference."

"Is there?"

"Yes. One gets you executed. The other gets you promoted."

---

On day twenty-four, they received a message from the Heavenly Assembly.

The Compliance Review would occur in three days. A team of three reviewers would arrive at the temple to conduct the audit. They would inspect everything, interview everyone, and render a final verdict.

Included with the message was a list of the reviewers:

*Reviewer One*: Elder Fang of the Celestial Sword Sect. The very man who had fired Ren.

*Reviewer Two*: Master Xu, a formation specialist from the Heavenly Array Society. Known for being thorough, meticulous, and impossible to bribe.

*Reviewer Three*: Lady Feng, a governance expert from the Jade Court. Her reputation was that she had dissolved seventeen sects in the past decade for "structural irregularities."

"The Celestial Sword Sect put Elder Fang on the team," Ren said, his voice hollow. "They're not just trying to fail us. They want to humiliate me personally."

"We could run," Li Chen suggested.

"Where?"

"Somewhere without Compliance Reviews. The Demon Desert. The Northern Wastes."

"We have a contract with the Azure Consortium. If we disappear, they'll hunt us down."

"So we're trapped."

"Welcome to sect leadership."

The chicken hopped onto the table, pecked at the message, and looked at Ren with what might have been sympathy.

"Even the chicken thinks we're doomed," Li Chen said.

"Stop projecting your own despair onto the chicken."

"I'm not projecting. Look at its eyes. That's the look of someone who knows they're about to be cooked."

---

The day of the review arrived.

Ren woke before dawn, dressed in his best robes, and stood at the temple entrance watching the sun rise over the mortal city below. The mist curled around the broken pagoda, and somewhere in the distance, a bell tolled.

Mei joined him, her face pale but determined. "I've triple-checked everything. The ledgers are perfect. The formation is solid. The chicken has been trained to sit still and look impressive."

"Can it actually be trained?"

"No. It's a chicken. But it's been following Old Zhang around all morning, and I think they've reached an understanding."

Li Chen stumbled out of the dormitory, still drunk from the night before. "They're here."

Ren's heart stopped. "What?"

"The reviewers. They arrived early. I saw them at the city gate an hour ago. They're probably halfway up the mountain by now."

"Early? They're not supposed to come until noon."

Li Chen shrugged. "They're trying to catch us unprepared. Surprise inspection."

Ren's mind raced. The temple wasn't ready. The formation wasn't fully activated. The chicken was still wearing Old Zhang's apron from the cooking demonstration they'd planned for later.

"Everyone to your positions," Ren said, his voice steady despite the panic in his chest. "We're going to sell this or die trying."

"And if we fail?"

Ren looked at the mist-shrouded mountain path, where three figures were just becoming visible through the morning gloom.

"Then we find out if the Azure Consortium accepts chickens as payment."

title: The Poison in the Porridge
content:
The morning after the Azure Pavilion meeting, Ren woke to find a letter pinned to his chest with a throwing knife.

He didn't scream. He didn't even flinch. He simply plucked the knife free, unfolded the paper, and read:

*"Your business model is cute. Your ambition is admirable. Your death is inevitable. — The Seven Kill Syndicate"*

Ren stared at the letter for a long moment. Then he looked at the knife. It was cheap iron, the kind you could buy from any street vendor. The handwriting was deliberately sloppy, written with the left hand or a brush held wrong. No spiritual signature. No tracking formation. Nothing useful.

"Mei," he called.

She appeared in the doorway, already dressed, a ledger in one hand and a cup of tea in the other. "What?"

He held up the letter.

She read it, her expression unchanged. "Seven Kill Syndicate. They're a mid-tier assassin group. Operate in the eastern provinces. Mostly Qi Condensation and early Foundation Establishment. Nothing special."

"Nothing special? They just threatened to kill me."

"Threats are cheap. Execution is expensive." She took a sip of tea. "The question is who hired them."

"The Azure Pavilion. Has to be. Huo was too polite. No one is that polite without wanting something."

Mei shook her head slowly. "No. Huo wouldn't use assassins. He'd use lawyers. Accountants. Tax audits. The Seven Kill Syndicate is low-class work. Someone's trying to scare you, not kill you. Yet."

Ren dressed quickly, his mind racing. The New Dao Sect now had four disciples, a crumbling temple, a partial formation array, and a death threat. Standard operating procedure for a startup in the cultivation world.

"Where's Li Chen?" Ren asked.

"Still sleeping. He had a long night."

"Wake him. We need to—"

A scream cut through the morning air. High. Sharp. Coming from the kitchen.

Ren and Mei ran.

They found Chen Wei, the chef disciple, standing over a bubbling pot of porridge. His face was pale. His hands were shaking. And the porridge was glowing a sickly green.

"Master," Chen Wei whispered. "Someone poisoned the breakfast."

Ren looked at the pot. The green glow pulsed like a heartbeat. He didn't need to be a poison expert to recognize a Soul-Dissolving Curse — a slow-acting toxin that corroded a cultivator's spiritual root from the inside. A week of eating this, and you'd be dead. A month, and your soul would be so damaged that reincarnation would be impossible.

"Who had access to the kitchen?" Ren asked.

"Everyone. No one. The kitchen is open. I left for five minutes to get vegetables from the garden."

"Five minutes. That's all they needed." Ren knelt beside the pot, studying the curse formation. It was crude — amateur work, in fact. A proper Soul-Dissolving Curse required precise spiritual resonance. This was slapdash, inefficient, and easy to detect.

That was suspicious.

"Don't eat the porridge," Ren said. "Obviously."

"What do we do?" Mei asked. "Cancel everything. Go dark. Find the poisoner."

"No." Ren stood up, brushing dust from his knees. "We do the opposite. We double down."

"Double down? Someone just tried to kill us."

"Someone just *advertised* that they're afraid of us. The Seven Kill Syndicate letter was a warning. The poisoned porridge was a test. They want to see how we react. If we hide, they know we're weak. If we fight, they know we're dangerous."

"And what are we?"

Ren smiled. It wasn't a nice smile. "We're a startup. We don't have the luxury of fear."

---

The plan was simple: Ren would meet with the Azure Pavilion again, this time with a counter-offer. Mei would trace the poison's origin through the city's underground herb market. Li Chen would find the Seven Kill Syndicate's local contact and extract information.

They split up at the market square, the morning sun casting long shadows across the cobblestones. Merchants were setting up stalls. Children were running through the streets. A normal day in a normal city.

Ren walked toward the Azure Pavilion, his robes freshly patched, his posture confident. He was a CEO. He was a sect leader. He was a man with a death threat and a poisoned breakfast, and he was *going to close this deal.*

The pavilion looked the same as yesterday — jade walls, golden roof, guards who could kill him with their pinky fingers. But today, there was a new energy in the air. Something sharp. Something waiting.

"Ren Wei," the receptionist said, her smile even emptier than before. "Consortium Master Huo is not available today."

"Then I'll speak to his assistant. Or his secretary. Or the janitor. I'm not leaving until I get a meeting."

The receptionist's smile flickered. "I'm afraid that's not possible."

"Isn't it?" Ren placed a small jade slip on the counter. "This contains our revised proposal. I'll wait one hour. If I don't get a meeting by then, I'll take this proposal to the Scarlet Phoenix Consortium instead. I hear they're looking for new investments."

The receptionist's eyes narrowed. The Scarlet Phoenix Consortium was Azure Pond's biggest rival. A calculated threat.

She took the jade slip. "Wait here."

Ren waited. He counted the tiles on the ceiling. He counted the guards' footsteps. He counted his own heartbeats.

Fifty-three minutes later, a door opened.

"Master Ren." A young man in blue robes bowed. "Consortium Master Huo will see you now."

---

The study was the same. The desk was the same. But Huo was different. His gentle smile was gone, replaced by something colder. More calculating.

"You threatened to take our proposal to the competition," Huo said.

"I made a business decision."

"Business decisions have consequences."

"Consequences are the foundation of business. Without consequences, there's no risk. Without risk, there's no reward." Ren sat down without being invited. "You read my revised proposal?"

"I skimmed it." Huo pushed the jade slip across the desk. "You're offering us 30% equity in exchange for a formation master, a supply contract, and protection from sect interference. That's a terrible deal."

"The formation master and supply contract are worth 15% each. The protection is a freebie."

"Protection is never free."

"Fine. 10% for the formation master. 10% for the supply contract. 10% for protection. That's a better deal."

Huo laughed. It was a dry, humorless sound. "You're negotiating like a street merchant."

"I learned from the best. The Celestial Sword Sect's procurement department. They could haggle a starving beggar out of his last rice cake."

For a moment, something flickered in Huo's eyes. Recognition? Respect? Ren couldn't tell.

"Your proposal has merit," Huo said slowly. "A new sect, unbound by tradition, operating on principles of efficiency and innovation. It's risky. It's unpredictable. It's exactly the kind of investment Azure Pond needs to stay relevant."

"So you're interested?"

"I'm interested in the *idea*. I'm not interested in *you*."

Ren's smile didn't waver. "What's the difference?"

"The difference is control. I want majority equity. I want veto power over all major decisions. I want the right to appoint my own overseer to your sect."

"That's not an investment. That's a takeover."

"That's business."

Ren stood up. "Then business is over."

"Sit down, Ren Wei."

"I don't take orders from people who want to steal my sect."

"I'm not stealing it. I'm *saving* it. You're a Qi Condensation cultivator with a crumbling temple and a death threat. You think you can survive without me?"

"I think I can survive *because* of you. Your interest validates my existence. Your rejection makes me a target. You've already made your choice, Consortium Master Huo. The question is whether you're brave enough to follow through."

Huo was silent for a long moment. Then he reached into his desk and pulled out a scroll.

"Sign this."

Ren took the scroll. It was a simple contract — Azure Pond Consortium would provide a mid-level formation master, a one-year supply contract, and a formal declaration of non-aggression with the Celestial Sword Sect. In exchange, New Dao Sect would give up 25% equity and one seat on its leadership council.

No veto power. No majority control.

Ren read it three times. Then he read it a fourth time.

"There's a poison clause," he said.

"Standard. If you die, the contract dissolves."

"Convenient."

"Practical." Huo's smile returned. "I'm not in the business of investing in dead men."

Ren pulled out a brush. Signed his name. Pressed his spiritual seal.

The contract glowed gold, then faded.

"Pleasure doing business," Ren said.

"The pleasure is mine." Huo's eyes were cold. "Don't make me regret this."

---

Ren left the pavilion feeling like he'd just won a battle he didn't understand. The contract was good. The terms were fair. But Huo had given in too easily. A man like Huo didn't fold without a reason.

Something was wrong.

He found Mei at the market, standing over a dead merchant. The merchant's face was frozen in terror, his throat slit from ear to ear.

"Seven Kill Syndicate," Mei said. "I found their contact. He found me first."

"Dead?"

"Dead. But not before leaving a message." She held up a bloody piece of paper. On it was written:

*"The poison was a warning. The next one won't be."*

Ren's blood ran cold.

"Where's Li Chen?"

"I don't know. He was supposed to meet me here an hour ago."

They ran.

---

They found Li Chen in an alley behind the gambling district. He was sitting against a wall, a bottle of wine in one hand, a bloody dagger in the other.

He looked up as they approached. His eyes were clear. His voice was steady.

"Seven Kill Syndicate," he said. "I found their local leader. We had a conversation."

"A conversation?"

"He talked. I listened. Then he stopped talking."

Ren knelt beside him. "What did he say?"

Li Chen took a long drink of wine. "He said the contract came from inside the Azure Pavilion. Not Huo. Someone in his organization. Someone who doesn't want this deal to go through."

"The poisoner?"

"The poisoner was a test. The letter was a warning. The real target isn't you, Ren."

"Then who?"

Li Chen looked at him, and for the first time since they'd met, the drunk sword master's eyes held something like fear.

"The real target is the sect. Someone wants New Dao Sect destroyed. Not because of what you are — but because of what you could become."

A shadow fell over them.

Ren looked up.

A woman stood at the entrance to the alley. Beautiful. Elegant. Dressed in robes of pure white silk, embroidered with golden clouds.

She smiled.

"Ren Wei," she said, her voice like honey and broken glass. "I've been looking for you."

"Who are you?"

"My name is Lin Xue. I represent the Celestial Sword Sect's Internal Affairs Division."

Ren's heart stopped.

"I'm here to offer you a deal."

title: The First Impossible Task
content:
Consortium Master Huo's smile didn't waver. His fingers tapped a slow rhythm on the obsidian desk — once, twice, three times — before he leaned back in his chair.

"Help each other," he repeated, savoring the words like fine wine. "An interesting philosophy. Most sect leaders come to me begging. They offer their disciples, their techniques, their very dignity in exchange for spirit stones. But you? You offer partnership."

"Is that a problem?"

"Not at all. It's refreshing." Huo reached into his robe and produced a scroll, unrolling it across the desk. The paper was old, the edges yellowed, the characters written in a script so ancient Ren could barely read it. "I have a proposition for you, Sect Leader Ren. A test of sorts."

Ren's instincts screamed. Everything about this felt wrong — the too-easy meeting, the too-friendly conversation, the too-perfect opportunity. But he'd come here for a reason. He'd starved for this chance.

"I'm listening."

"The Azure Pond Consortium has a problem. Three months ago, we financed an expedition to the Crimson Wastes — a team of Foundation Establishment cultivators sent to retrieve a specific artifact from the ruins of an ancient sect. They succeeded. They also died. All of them."

Ren kept his face neutral. The Crimson Wastes were a death sentence for anyone below Core Formation. The fact that Huo had sent Foundation Establishment cultivators at all suggested either desperation or extreme confidence in his team.

"The artifact was taken," Huo continued. "By whom, we don't know. But we do know where it's being held — the Black Lotus Sect's southern fortress."

"The Black Lotus Sect?" Ren's voice came out tighter than he intended. "They're one of the Seven Great Sects."

"Indeed. Which is why I can't send my own people. An overt attack would mean war. But a small, unnoticed team? A team that doesn't exist in any official records? That might work."

Ren understood now. This wasn't a test. This was a trap designed to look like a test.

"You want my sect to steal from the Black Lotus Sect."

"I want you to retrieve what's mine." Huo's smile finally vanished, replaced by something colder, harder. "Your proposal spoke of 'agility' and 'innovation.' Well, here's your chance to prove it. The fortress is defended by at least three Core Formation elders, a garrison of fifty Qi Condensation disciples, and formations that would take a master a week to crack. No one expects an attack from a sect that doesn't exist yet."

"And if I refuse?"

"Then we have nothing more to discuss." Huo rolled the scroll back up, his movements deliberate. "But consider this, Ren Wei — if you succeed, you won't just get investment. You'll get my personal backing. My connections. My protection. The New Dao Sect will go from a startup to a serious player overnight."

*And if I fail*, Ren thought, *I'll be dead. Or worse, alive and wishing I was dead.*

"What's the artifact?"

Huo's eyes glittered. "A jade slip containing a technique called the Thousand Reflection Scripture. It allows the user to create perfect copies of themselves — not illusions, not clones, but genuine duplicates with independent consciousness. The original sect that created it was destroyed three thousand years ago for violating the natural order."

"And you want this technique why?"

"Because I'm a collector of rare things. And because knowledge, like capital, should never be monopolized by a single entity." Huo leaned forward. "Do we have a deal?"

Ren's mind raced. He was a Qi Condensation cultivator leading a sect with no resources, no reputation, and three misfit disciples who could barely function as a team. The Black Lotus Sect was one of the most powerful organizations in the cultivation world. This wasn't a challenge — it was suicide.

But that was exactly the point.

If he said no, he'd never get another chance. The other investors would hear about this meeting. They'd know he'd been offered a path and refused to walk it. The New Dao Sect would die before it was born.

If he said yes, he might die. But he might also win.

"One condition," Ren said.

Huo raised an eyebrow. "Name it."

"Full payment upfront. Fifty thousand spirit stones. Non-refundable."

The consortium master laughed — a genuine sound, rich and warm. "You have guts, boy. I'll give you that." He reached into his storage ring and produced a jade token, sliding it across the desk. "Take this to the Azure Pavilion's treasury. They'll give you twenty thousand now. The rest when you deliver the artifact."

"Thirty now. Twenty on delivery."

"Twenty-five. Final offer."

"Done."

Ren took the token. His hand was steady, but his heart felt like it was trying to escape his chest. He'd just committed his sect to a mission that would get them all killed. And somehow, he felt more alive than he had in years.

---

Mei was waiting for him in the reception area, her face carefully blank. Li Chen stood by the door, arms crossed, looking like he'd rather be anywhere else. The moment Ren emerged, they both straightened.

"Well?" Mei asked.

"Let's talk outside."

They walked through the Jade District in silence, past merchants selling spirit tools and cultivators showing off their latest breakthroughs. The opulence of the city felt obscene now, a constant reminder of how far they had to climb.

Ren waited until they reached a small tea house on the edge of the district before speaking. He ordered three cups of the cheapest tea — the owner gave him a look that suggested he knew exactly how poor they were — and laid out the situation.

"He wants us to steal from the Black Lotus Sect," Ren said.

Li Chen choked on his tea. "I'm sorry, I think I misheard. You said 'steal from the Black Lotus Sect.' As in, the sect that once burned an entire city to the ground because they suspected a traitor was hiding there?"

"That's the one."

"And you said yes?"

"I said yes."

Mei stared at him for a long moment. Then she did something unexpected — she laughed. Not a nervous laugh, not a bitter laugh, but a genuine, full-throated laugh that drew stares from the other customers.

"You're insane," she said, wiping tears from her eyes. "You're completely, utterly insane. I love it."

"You're not angry?"

"Angry?" Mei shook her head. "Ren, I spent twenty years working for the Celestial Sword Sect. Do you know how many 'impossible' missions I watched them cancel because the elders were too afraid to take risks? The Black Lotus Sect is dangerous, yes. But they're also arrogant. They've been at the top for so long that they've forgotten what it means to be hunted."

Li Chen set down his tea, his face pale. "I'm going to die. I'm going to die for a startup that doesn't even have a proper building."

"Technically, we have a building," Ren said. "It's just falling apart."

"That's not comforting!"

Ren spent the next hour outlining his plan. It was rough, full of holes, and relied on a dozen things going exactly right. But it was also the only plan they had.

Phase one: Intelligence. Li Chen would go to the city where the Black Lotus fortress was located, posing as a wandering merchant. He'd spend a week gathering information — guard rotations, formation layouts, the schedules of the Core Formation elders.

Phase two: Infiltration. Mei would use her business connections to secure a delivery contract with the Black Lotus Sect. She'd arrange for a shipment of 'rare ingredients' to arrive at the fortress, with Ren hidden inside the cargo.

Phase three: Theft. Ren would enter the fortress, locate the artifact, and escape before anyone noticed it was gone.

Phase four: Escape. They'd meet at a pre-arranged location and flee the region before the Black Lotus Sect could organize a pursuit.

"There are at least seventeen ways this could kill us," Mei said when he finished.

"I counted twenty-three."

"Then why are we doing this?"

Ren looked at his two disciples — the drunk sword master who'd given up on his own potential, the businesswoman who'd been crushed by the system they were trying to overthrow. He thought about the crumbling temple, the empty coffers, the dream that had seemed so impossible just a week ago.

"Because the alternative is to give up," he said. "To accept that we're not good enough, not strong enough, not worthy enough to build something new. I've spent my whole life being told I'm not enough. I'm tired of hearing it."

Mei reached across the table and squeezed his hand. "Then let's prove them wrong."

Li Chen sighed, the sound heavy with resignation. "I'm going to need more wine."

---

They left the tea house as the sun began to set, the sky painted in shades of orange and red. The city was alive with evening activity — lanterns being lit, street vendors calling out their wares, cultivators flying overhead on swords and spiritual artifacts.

Ren watched them pass, these cultivators who'd been born into power, who'd never known what it meant to fight for every scrap of progress. He hated them, a little. But he also envied them.

"One day," he said quietly, "we'll be flying up there too."

Mei looked at him, her expression unreadable. "Is that what you want? To be like them?"

"No." Ren shook his head. "I want to be better."

They walked back to the temple in the gathering darkness, the three of them moving as a unit for the first time. It wasn't much — a drunk, a schemer, and a failure. But it was something.

It was a start.

When they reached the temple gates, Ren stopped. A letter was pinned to the door, the paper crisp and white, the characters written in gold ink that seemed to glow in the fading light.

He pulled it free and read it.

His blood ran cold.

*Sect Leader Ren,*

*I know what you've agreed to. The Black Lotus Sect has been expecting such an attempt. The artifact is a fake — a trap designed to catch anyone foolish enough to try and steal it.*

*Meet me at the Drunken Phoenix Tavern in Maple Town, three days from now. Come alone.*

*I have a better offer.*

*— A Friend*

Mei read over his shoulder. Her face went pale. "Ren..."

"Someone knows," he said, his voice hollow. "Someone in the Azure Pond Consortium knows about the mission."

Li Chen grabbed the letter, read it twice, then crumpled it in his fist. "We can't trust this. It could be a trap from the Black Lotus themselves, trying to flush us out."

"Or it could be real." Ren stared at the shattered remains of his plan. "Either way, we're in deeper than I thought."

He looked up at the sky, where the first stars were beginning to appear. Somewhere out there, an enemy he couldn't see was watching him.

And the game had only just begun.

title: Chapter 5
content:
CHAPTER 5: The Price of Ambition
