They walked in silence for a moment. The mortal city hummed around them, merchants closing their stalls, servants hurrying home, the distant sound of music from a teahouse. Normal life, happening in parallel to their world of spiritual politics and financial warfare.

"What else did you change?" Ren asked.

"The repayment schedule. The interest rate. The definition of 'default.' And I added a clause that voids the entire contract if any Azure Pond employee attempts to recruit New Dao Sect members without your written consent."

"That's... good."

"It's also illegal in three provinces. But Huo won't report it, because then he'd have to explain why he didn't catch it."

Mei smiled. It was the smile of someone who genuinely enjoyed financial warfare.

They reached the edge of the Jade District, where the golden lanterns gave way to the flickering oil lamps of the merchant quarter. Li Chen was waiting at a noodle stall, a bowl of broth in one hand and a stack of papers in the other.

"You look terrible," he said to Ren.

"Feel worse. What's the intelligence?"

Li Chen slid a paper across the counter. "Jade Lotus Consortium is moving into the northern territories. They've acquired three small sects in the last month. Hostile takeovers, from what I can tell. Offering debt they can't repay, then foreclosing on their cultivation grounds."

"We're in the south."

"We're about to be in their path. Jade Lotus doesn't like competition. And Azure Pond just made us a competitor."

Ren stared at the paper. The characters swam before his eyes. "How long do we have?"

"Depends on how fast they move. If they're smart, they'll wait until we've spent the spirit stones, then hit us when we're vulnerable."

"That's what I would do."

"Good thing you're not a consortium master."

They finished their noodles in silence. Ren's mind raced through scenarios, contingency plans, desperate gambles. He'd built New Dao Sect on the idea that speed and innovation could beat entrenched power. But speed meant nothing if you ran headfirst into a wall.

By the time they returned to the temple, the moon was high and the disciples were asleep. All except for Xiao Bao, who sat in the meditation hall with a candle and a stack of recipe books.

"Master," she said without looking up. "I've been thinking."

"That's dangerous."

"I know. But I think I've found something."

She pushed a book toward him. It was a cookbook. A very old, very worn cookbook, the pages yellowed and the characters faded. But the recipe she'd marked was different. It was written in a script he didn't recognize, the strokes angular and sharp, like broken bones.

"What is this?"

"I found it in the archives. Under the kitchen. There's a hidden compartment behind the old stove."

"There's a hidden compartment behind the old stove?"

"Was. I broke it when I tried to move the stove to clean. But I found this." She tapped the book. "It's a recipe for something called 'Soul-Steeping Broth.' It uses ingredients I've never heard of. Spirit fungus that only grows in places where cultivators died. Water from a lake that reflects the moon differently than it should. And..." She hesitated. "And blood. Cultivator blood."

Ren felt the temperature in the room drop.

"Xiao Bao, that's demonic cultivation."

"I know." Her voice was barely a whisper. "But Master, the recipe works. I... I tested it. Just the first step. I used some of my own blood, just a drop, in a regular broth. And the spiritual energy in the soup... it was different. Stronger. Almost alive."

"Destroy it."

"Master—"

"Destroy the book. Burn it. Bury the ashes. And forget you ever saw it."

Xiao Bao's face crumpled, but she nodded. She gathered the book and walked toward the kitchen, her steps heavy.

Ren sat alone in the meditation hall, the candle flickering, his heart pounding.

Demonic cultivation. In his sect. Under his nose.

He'd known the temple was old. He'd known it had history. But this? This was a death sentence. If the major sects found out that New Dao Sect possessed demonic texts, they wouldn't bother with a trial. They'd burn the temple to the ground and salt the earth beneath it.

He needed to tell Mei. He needed to tell Li Chen. He needed to figure out what else was hidden in this cursed temple.

But first, he needed to sleep.

He didn't.

The next morning dawned grey and cold. Ren found Xiao Bao in the kitchen, staring at a pot of boiling water. The book was gone, but her hands were shaking.

"Did you burn it?"

"Yes, Master."

"All of it?"

"Every page."

He wanted to believe her. He really did. But he'd seen the look in her eyes when she talked about the broth. The hunger. The fascination.

"Xiao Bao, I need you to promise me something."

"Anything, Master."

"If you ever find another book like that, you come to me first. Before you read it. Before you touch it. Before you even think about it. Understand?"

"Yes, Master."

He left the kitchen and found Mei in the main hall, already dressed and ready for the day's work. She took one look at his face and her smile faded.

"What happened?"

"Nothing. Just a long night."

"You're a terrible liar."

"I'm an excellent liar. I'm just too tired to practice."

Mei studied him for a moment, then nodded. "Fine. Keep your secrets. But we have a meeting with the formation material supplier in three hours, and you look like you've been dragged through a battlefield."

"I'll wash up."

"Do more than that. Put on your good robes. The ones that don't have holes."

"My good robes have holes."

"Then buy new ones. We have spirit stones now. Act like it."

Ren spent the morning in a daze. They visited the supplier, negotiated prices, inspected materials. He smiled when he needed to, nodded when he should, and kept his thoughts locked behind a mask of professionalism.

But his mind kept returning to Xiao Bao. To the recipe. To the way her hands had shaken.

Something was wrong.

He felt it in his bones, the same way he'd felt the Celestial Sword Sect's betrayal coming months before it happened. A creeping dread that settled in his chest and refused to leave.

By evening, they'd secured the materials for the next three months. The temple's formation network would be operational by the end of the week. They were ahead of schedule. On budget. Growing.

It should have been a triumph.

Instead, Ren sat on the temple steps, watching the sun set over the mortal city, and felt like he was standing on the edge of a cliff.

His jade slip vibrated.

A message. Encrypted. From an unknown sender.

He opened it.

*"I know what you found. Meet me at the Red Lantern Teahouse. Midnight. Come alone. — A Friend"*

Ren stared at the message until the characters blurred.

Someone knew about the book.

Someone was watching.

And he had no idea who.

title: Chapter 4
content:
CHAPTER 4: The Price of Ambition

Consortium Master Huo's smile didn't waver, but something shifted in his eyes. A calculation, a weighing of possibilities. He steepled his fingers and leaned back in his chair.

"Resources," he repeated, savoring the word like fine wine. "You want spirit stones. Cultivation aids. Perhaps a proper headquarters instead of that crumbling temple in the eastern hills."

Ren kept his face neutral. Of course Huo knew about the temple. The man probably knew what Ren had eaten for breakfast three days ago.

"Among other things," Ren said. "New Dao Sect has three disciples currently. We need at least thirty to achieve critical mass. That requires recruitment, which requires reputation, which requires results. I have a plan for those results, but the plan needs seed funding."

"Seed funding." Huo chuckled. "You really do think in business terms. Most cultivators would ask for 'patronage' or 'support.' They'd grovel, promise eternal loyalty, offer their swords in service. But you're asking for an investment. A calculated risk with expected returns."

"Is that a problem?"

"On the contrary." Huo opened a drawer and pulled out a scroll, unrolling it across his desk. It was a map of the region — the Jade District, the mortal city, the surrounding mountains and forests. "It's refreshing. Most cultivators are terrible businessmen. They think power solves everything. They don't understand leverage, timing, market positioning."

He tapped a point on the map — a valley about fifty li from the temple. "There's a spirit stone mine here. Small, depleted, abandoned by the Violet Flame Sect three years ago. They said it was worthless."

Ren studied the map. "But you don't think so."

"I know it's not depleted. The Violet Flame Sect's miners were corrupt. They reported false yields, pocketed the difference, and when the sect sent inspectors, they sabotaged the remaining veins to hide their theft. The sect believed the mine was empty and abandoned it."

"And you want me to take it."

"I want you to *claim* it. Officially, as New Dao Sect territory. Reopen the mine, extract the stones, and pay Azure Pond a thirty percent royalty." Huo's smile sharpened. "In return, I'll provide the initial capital — ten thousand spirit stones, a set of basic formation flags, and a letter of credit with the Jade District's equipment suppliers."

Ten thousand spirit stones. Ren's mind raced. That was enough to repair the temple, buy proper cultivation resources for his disciples, and fund recruitment for six months. It was also a trap, and he knew it.

"The Violet Flame Sect won't be happy," he said slowly. "They'll see this as theft."

"They abandoned the mine. Legally, it's unclaimed territory. Anyone can take it."

"Legally, yes. But the Violet Flame Sect isn't known for respecting legal niceties when they want something."

Huo's smile never wavered. "That's your problem, not mine. I'm an investor, Ren Wei. I provide capital. You provide execution. If you can't handle the competition, perhaps you're not ready for this venture."

The words stung, but Ren forced himself to think. The mine was a risk, but it was also an opportunity. If he could secure it, New Dao Sect would have a steady income stream. More importantly, it would prove that his model worked — that a small, agile sect could compete with the established powers.

But first, he needed to survive the Violet Flame Sect's response.

"I'll need more than formation flags," Ren said. "I need defensive arrays. Something that can hold off a Qi Condensation cultivator for at least an hour."

"Reasonable. I'll add a set of basic defensive formation discs to the package. They're not top-tier, but they'll buy you time."

"And I need information. Who in the Violet Flame Sect oversaw the mine? Who's most likely to retaliate?"

Huo raised an eyebrow. "You're thorough. I like that." He pulled another scroll from his drawer, this one covered in names and notes. "The mine was overseen by Elder Zhao Ming. He's the one who declared it depleted and recommended abandonment. My sources say he's been living beyond his means since then. New mansion, new concubines, new cultivation treasures."

"Bribed by the miners to look the other way."

"Almost certainly. If you move quickly, you can establish control before Elder Zhao realizes what's happening. By the time he reports the loss to his sect, you'll already be fortified."

Ren nodded slowly. The plan was forming in his mind — a quick strike, a rapid fortification, a defensive posture that would make any attack costly. It was risky, but every good investment was.

"One more thing," Huo said. His voice dropped, becoming almost intimate. "I've heard rumors about your sect. About a disciple who cultivates through cooking, and a sword master who's never sober, and a formation expert who prefers gambling to study. These are not traditional strengths, Ren Wei."

"They're strengths nonetheless."

"Perhaps. But if you fail, don't expect a second chance. Azure Pond Consortium invests once. If that investment fails, we cut our losses and move on. You understand?"

Ren met his eyes. "I understand."

They signed the contract on spirit paper — a document that glowed with binding energy, ensuring both parties fulfilled their obligations. Ren walked out of the study with the scroll tucked under his arm and a heavy pouch of spirit stones in his inner pocket.

Mei was waiting for him, her face a careful mask of professional interest. Li Chen stood by the door, pretending to examine a painting while actually watching every person in the room.

"Well?" Mei asked.

"Get the others. We're going mining."

---

The journey to the valley took two days. Ren used the time to explain the plan to his disciples, laying out the strategy on a crude map drawn in the dirt.

"The mine has three entrances," he said, pointing. "Main tunnel, emergency exit, and a ventilation shaft that might be large enough for a person. We need to secure all three within the first hour, before anyone in the Violet Flame Sect realizes what's happening."

"Securing them against what?" Granny Zhou asked. She was sharpening her cleaver, the blade gleaming in the firelight. "Ghosts? Demons? Angry miners?"

"Worse. Angry cultivators." Ren looked at each of them in turn. "Elder Zhao Ming of the Violet Flame Sect is going to be very upset when he finds out his secret is exposed. He'll come with whatever force he can muster. We need to be ready."

"Why not just kill him?" Li Chen asked. "One dead elder, problem solved."

"Because killing an elder of a major sect starts a war. Holding territory that's legally unclaimed starts a negotiation. We want negotiation, not war."

"Negotiation implies we have something they want," Mei pointed out. "What do we have?"

Ren smiled. "We have the truth. And we have a wealthy investor who's very interested in exposing corruption in the established sects. Azure Pond Consortium has been looking for leverage against the Violet Flame Sect for years. This mine gives them exactly that."

"You're using your investor as a threat," Granny Zhou said slowly. "Clever."

"I'm using everyone as everything. That's how startup sects work. We don't have raw power, so we have to be smarter, faster, more adaptable." Ren stood up, dusting off his robes. "Now, let's go take what's ours."

---

The mine entrance was exactly as Huo had described — a gaping hole in the mountainside, surrounded by rusted equipment and crumbling sheds. The air smelled of wet stone and decay.

Ren held up a hand, signaling his disciples to stop. "Mei, check for formations."

Mei closed her eyes, her fingers tracing patterns in the air. "There's a residual alert array. Old, weak, barely maintained. It's linked to a signal talisman somewhere in the mine."

"Can you disable it?"

"Child's play." She pulled a small pouch from her belt, scattering powder in a circle around her feet. Her hands moved in complex patterns, her lips murmuring words that made the air hum. The powder glowed, then faded.

"Done. The array won't trigger when we enter."

"Good. Li Chen, you're on point. Granny Zhou, cover the rear. Mei, stay close to me." Ren drew his sword — a simple iron blade, chipped and worn — and stepped into the darkness.

The mine tunnel stretched ahead, supported by wooden beams that groaned under the weight of the mountain. Lanterns hung at irregular intervals, their flames weak and sputtering. The deeper they went, the more the walls changed — from rough stone to smooth, worked rock, then to veins of crystalline material that caught the light.

Spirit stones. Not the pure, refined stones used for cultivation, but raw ore. And there was a lot of it.

"This place isn't depleted," Mei whispered. "There's enough raw material here to fuel a small sect for years."

"Elder Zhao was very busy," Ren said. "Look at the walls. The main veins are still intact. They only mined the surface deposits, then claimed the rest was empty."

"Bastards," Granny Zhou muttered. "Waste not, want not."

They reached the central chamber — a vast cavern where the main mining operation had taken place. Broken tools, abandoned carts, and piles of rubble littered the floor. But at the far end, a wooden door stood intact, a formation glowing faintly on its surface.

"Elder Zhao's office," Ren guessed. "Probably full of evidence."

Li Chen stepped forward, his hand on his sword. "Let me check for traps."

He moved with surprising grace for a drunkard, his feet silent on the stone floor. He examined the door, the formation, the surrounding walls, then nodded. "Clean. The formation is just a lock."

"Mei?"

"Already on it." She pulled out a set of lockpicks — not the crude metal tools of a mortal thief, but delicate implements carved from spirit jade, inscribed with tiny formation scripts. Thirty seconds later, the door swung open.

The office was exactly what Ren expected. A desk covered in ledgers and correspondence. Shelves lined with jars of spirit stones. A hidden compartment behind a loose stone, containing a ledger with a different set of numbers — the real mining records.

"This is it," Ren said, holding up the ledger. "Proof of the fraud. With this, we can—"

A sound echoed through the mine. Footsteps. Many of them, moving in unison.

"Company," Li Chen said, his voice flat.

"How many?"

"At least a dozen. Cultivators, by the sound of their footsteps. Light, precise, deliberate."

Ren's mind raced. They'd been too slow. Elder Zhao must have had someone watching the mine, someone who reported their arrival. The response was already here.

"How long until they reach us?"

"Three minutes. Maybe four."

Ren looked around the office. The ledgers, the spirit stones, the evidence. He couldn't carry it all. But he didn't need to.

"Mei, grab the ledgers and the correspondence. Granny Zhou, take the spirit stones. Li Chen, with me."

"Where are we going?"

Ren smiled — a thin, sharp expression that held no warmth. "To meet our guests. We're going to welcome them to New Dao Sect territory."

"And if they don't leave peacefully?"

Ren's hand tightened on his sword. "Then we show them what a startup sect does to competitors."

He walked out of the office, his disciples falling into formation behind him. The footsteps grew louder, closer

title: The Fine Print That Kills
content:
Ren held his breath as Consortium Master Huo studied him with those gentle, knowing eyes. The silence stretched, filled only by the soft hum of formation arrays embedded in the walls.

"You're bold," Huo finally said, steepling his fingers. "I appreciate boldness. Most cultivators who come to me are either desperate or delusional. You seem to be both, but at least you're aware of it."

"I prefer 'strategically optimistic,'" Ren said.

Huo laughed—a genuine sound, not the polite tittering of a man used to flattery. "I like you, Ren Wei. That's unfortunate for you. It means I'll give you a better deal than I give most. But still a deal that favors me. Business is business."

He slid a scroll across the desk. It was sealed with golden wax, the mark of the Azure Pond Consortium pressed into its surface. A koi fish swimming through a circle of coins.

"Three thousand spirit stones," Huo said. "Upfront. A fixed interest rate of fifteen percent per annum. No collateral required beyond a lien on your sect's future revenue. Standard terms for a venture of this risk level."

Ren's heart raced. Three thousand spirit stones was more than he'd hoped for. It would cover the temple renovations, basic equipment for his disciples, and leave enough for operating expenses for at least six months. The interest rate was high but not predatory.

"What's the catch?" Ren asked.

Huo's smile widened. "No catch. But there is an *option*."

He produced a second scroll. This one was smaller, the wax seal a different color—crimson rather than gold.

"If your sect achieves certain milestones within the first year, I have the right to convert my debt into equity. Twenty percent ownership of New Dao Sect."

Ren's blood went cold.

"That's—"

"Standard for high-risk investments," Huo interrupted smoothly. "Think of it as an incentive. If you succeed, I succeed with you. If you fail, I lose my investment. We're partners, Ren Wei. Partners with aligned interests."

Partners. Right. Partners where one partner could own a fifth of everything Ren had built.

"I need to discuss this with my—"

"You can discuss it with your disciples," Huo said, "but the offer is time-sensitive. I have another meeting in two hours with a promising young talisman maker. The investment pool is limited."

Ren stared at the crimson scroll. Twenty percent. It sounded reasonable on paper. In practice, it meant Huo could veto major decisions, demand access to sect secrets, and eventually push Ren out of his own organization.

But without the investment, there was no organization to protect.

"I'll need an hour," Ren said.

"Take two. The tea here is excellent."

---

Mei was pacing when Ren emerged. Li Chen had found a chair in the corner and was pretending to meditate, though his eyes tracked every movement in the room.

"Well?" Mei demanded.

"We have an offer. Three thousand spirit stones."

Her eyes lit up. "That's—"

"There's a conversion clause. Twenty percent equity if we hit milestones."

The light died. "That's *insane*. He wants to own us?"

"It's an option, not a guarantee. If we hit the milestones, we're already successful. Twenty percent of something valuable is better than a hundred percent of nothing."

Li Chen opened one eye. "What are the milestones?"

Ren pulled out the scroll he'd copied into his memory. "First: recruit fifty disciples within six months. Second: achieve at least one documented breakthrough to Foundation Establishment within the sect. Third: secure a partnership with at least one major faction."

Silence.

"That's..." Mei started.

"Impossible," Li Chen finished.

"Difficult," Ren corrected. "Not impossible. Fifty disciples is aggressive but doable if we market effectively. Foundation Establishment is harder—that requires a cultivator to reach the second major realm. None of us are close."

"You're at Qi Condensation stage four," Mei pointed out. "I'm at stage three. Li Chen is at stage two. The drunk sword master is at stage six but he's unreliable. The gambling formation expert is at stage one on a good day. And the chef cultivator is barely at stage one."

"Thank you for that thorough summary of our inadequacies."

"I'm being realistic. You asked me to be your CFO. This is what CFOs do."

Ren rubbed his temples. "The third milestone is the real problem. A partnership with a major faction means we need one of the established sects or noble houses to officially recognize us. That's... politically complicated."

"Because we're a joke," Li Chen said, not unkindly. "The startup sect with three misfits and a crumbling temple. Why would any major faction associate with us?"

"Because we offer something they don't," Ren said. "Innovation. Agility. We're not bound by tradition. We can move faster, adapt quicker, take risks they can't."

Mei crossed her arms. "You're describing a feature, not a benefit. What's the benefit?"

Ren had been thinking about this since he read the proposal. "Access to untapped talent. The major sects only recruit from established families or through their own exams. There are thousands of potential cultivators in the mortal cities who never get a chance because they don't have connections or resources. We can find them, train them, and create a pipeline that the major sects can draw from."

"That's actually... not terrible," Mei admitted.

"It's not terrible," Li Chen agreed. "It's also not proven."

"That's why it's a *startup*." Ren stood up straight. "I'm going to accept the offer. But I'm going to negotiate better terms. Twenty percent is too high for the risk they're taking. Ten percent, with a lower conversion threshold."

"You think he'll agree?"

"I think he'll counter. And then I'll counter his counter. And eventually we'll find a number that makes both of us uncomfortable enough to sign."

---

The negotiation took three hours.

Huo started at twenty percent. Ren countered with five. Huo laughed and offered fifteen. Ren said eight. Huo went to twelve, but added a clause that gave him first right of refusal on any future funding rounds.

Ren agreed, but only if the conversion option expired after eighteen months instead of twelve.

Huo considered. Then he smiled.

"You're good at this, Ren Wei. Have you considered a career in finance?"

"I considered it. But I'd rather build something than just count money."

"A philosophical difference without a practical distinction." Huo extended his hand. "Ten percent equity upon achieving two of the three milestones. Conversion option expires at eighteen months. First right of refusal on future rounds. Do we have a deal?"

Ren took his hand. The grip was firm, the skin warm. A cultivator's grip, but not aggressive.

"We have a deal."

The seals were broken. The contracts were signed. Spirit stones were transferred—three thousand of them, appearing in a storage ring that Huo provided as a "signing bonus."

Ren walked out of the Azure Pavilion feeling like he'd just sold a piece of his soul.

But at least his soul had a price.

---

The return to the temple was quiet. Mei kept glancing at Ren, her expression unreadable. Li Chen walked behind them, his hand resting on his sword hilt, alert for threats.

They reached the temple gates as the sun was setting, painting the crumbling walls in shades of gold and crimson.

"We did it," Mei said, her voice flat. "We actually did it."

"We did it," Ren agreed. "Now the real work begins."

They entered the main hall. The drunk sword master was passed out in a corner, surrounded by empty jars. The gambling formation expert was hunched over a table, muttering about hexagrams and probability matrices. The chef cultivator was in the kitchen, the smell of burning rice drifting through the temple.

"Home sweet home," Li Chen muttered.

Ren was about to respond when he felt it. A shift in the spiritual energy around them. A pressure, like the air itself was getting heavier.

Someone was coming.

Not someone. *Multiple* someones. Powerful someones.

The temple gates exploded inward.

Standing in the doorway was a woman in white robes, her hair bound in a severe bun, her eyes cold as winter ice. Behind her, a dozen cultivators in matching uniforms—the emblem of the Celestial Sword Sect embroidered on their chests.

"Ren Wei," the woman said, her voice carrying the weight of absolute authority. "You are hereby charged with violation of the Celestial Sword Sect's non-compete clause, theft of proprietary cultivation techniques, and unauthorized use of sect resources."

Ren's blood turned to ice.

"I'm sorry," he said, his voice steady despite his terror. "Non-compete clause?"

The woman's smile was razor-thin. "Did you think we wouldn't have lawyers?"

She threw a scroll at his feet. It unrolled to reveal page after page of dense legal text, all bearing the seal of the Celestial Sword Sect's legal department.

"You signed a five-year exclusivity agreement when you joined our sect," she continued. "That agreement contains a clause prohibiting you from establishing or joining any competing cultivation organization within ten thousand li of our sect's territory. You've been out of our employ for precisely three weeks."

Three weeks. Ren's mind raced. He'd been so focused on building his sect that he hadn't even thought about the paperwork.

"This is ridiculous," Mei said, stepping forward. "He was fired. You can't enforce a non-compete against someone you terminated."

"Actually," the woman said, producing another scroll, "the terms of termination specify that the non-compete remains in effect for a period of twelve months following separation, regardless of cause. It's all right here. Signed by Ren Wei himself."

Ren stared at the scroll. He'd signed so many documents when he joined the Celestial Sword Sect. Forms, waivers, agreements. He'd skimmed most of them, trusting that they were standard.

That had been a mistake.

"You have twenty-four hours," the woman said. "Disband your so-called sect. Return any property that might be considered sect assets. And report to the Celestial Sword Sect's legal department to discuss the terms of your compliance."

"And if I refuse?"

The woman's smile didn't waver. "Then we escalate. The Azure Pond Consortium might be interested to know that their new investment is built on a foundation of legal violations. I imagine they'll want their spirit stones back. With interest."

Ren's hand went to his storage ring. The three thousand spirit stones suddenly felt like a trap.

"Twenty-four hours," the woman repeated. "Don't be late."

She turned and walked away, her disciples following like shadows. The gates hung broken, the evening light streaming through the gap.

Ren stood in the middle of his temple, surrounded by his misfit disciples, holding a legal document that might destroy everything he'd built.

Mei touched his arm. "Ren?"

"We need a lawyer," he said. "And a miracle."

The chef cultivator emerged from the kitchen, holding a blackened pot. "Dinner's ruined," he said.

Ren laughed. It was a broken sound, edged with hysteria.

"Of course it is."

title: The Contract Has Teeth
content:
Ren's words hung in the air like a sword waiting to fall. Consortium Master Huo's gentle smile didn't waver, but his eyes sharpened—the first crack in the scholarly facade.

"Bold words from a sect leader whose entire operation fits inside a crumbling temple," Huo said, leaning back. His fingers tapped a rhythm on the obsidian desk. "But boldness without backing is just noise. Show me why I should invest in noise."

Ren reached into his robe and pulled out a scroll—not the formal proposal Huo had already read, but a smaller document, stained with tea and hastily written. He unrolled it on the desk.

"This is my real proposal," Ren said. "The one I didn't put in writing because I didn't want it intercepted."

Huo's eyebrow rose. He scanned the document, his expression shifting from amusement to curiosity to something Ren couldn't read. The room's ambient spiritual energy hummed, pressing against Ren's skin like an unspoken threat.

"You're proposing we disrupt the spirit stone market," Huo said slowly. "Directly."

"Indirectly," Ren corrected. "New Dao Sect doesn't sell spirit stones. We sell *access*. A subscription model—monthly fees for cultivation guidance, formation repairs, pill prescriptions. No upfront costs. No binding contracts. Just consistent value delivered to mortal towns and small sects that can't afford traditional rates."

"And how do you make money?"

Volume. Ren had run the numbers a hundred times. A thousand customers paying small amounts beat ten customers paying large amounts—especially when the large customers could crush you for displeasing them.

Huo stared at the document for a long moment. Then he laughed.

Not a mocking laugh. A genuine one, surprised and warm.

"You're either a genius or the biggest fool I've met this decade," Huo said. "Possibly both."

"I get that a lot."

"Here's my counter-offer." Huo pulled a jade slip from his sleeve and slid it across the desk. "Two thousand low-grade spirit stones. A building in the Jade District—deed transferred to New Dao Sect. Access to Azure Pond's merchant routes for supplies."

Ren's heart stopped. That was more than he'd hoped for. Way more.

"—in exchange for seventy percent equity," Huo finished.

Ren's heart restarted just to break.

"Seventy percent?" He kept his voice level, barely. "Consortium Master, that's not an investment. That's a takeover."

"It's a valuation," Huo said, still smiling. "Your sect has no assets, no revenue, and three disciples—one of whom is wanted for gambling debts in three provinces. I'm taking a significant risk."

"Li Chen's debts are under control."

"Are they? Because I have a file that says he owes the Black Lotus Syndicate four hundred spirit stones from a bet gone wrong last month."

Ren's blood went cold. He hadn't known about that. Li Chen had said he'd cleared his accounts. *Had* he lied, or was Huo's intelligence just that good?

Huo saw the hesitation and pressed his advantage. "Seventy percent. Take it or leave it. But if you leave it, I'll have to reconsider our arrangement. I don't appreciate wasted time."

Ren's mind raced. Seventy percent meant Huo controlled the sect. Every major decision would go through Azure Pond. The New Dao Sect would become a subsidiary, not a startup.

But two thousand spirit stones. A building. Merchant routes.

With that, he could actually build something. Train disciples properly. Expand to other towns. Create the subscription network he'd dreamed of.

Without it, he'd be back to begging for scraps within a month.

"I need time to think," Ren said.

"You have until tonight." Huo stood, the meeting clearly over. "My final offer. Accept it, or find another investor. But I should warn you—Azure Pond has a long memory. Those who refuse our generosity rarely find generosity elsewhere."

It wasn't a threat. It was a promise.

Ren left the study feeling like he'd just negotiated with a demon. Mei and Li Chen were waiting in the lobby, their faces expectant.

"Well?" Mei asked.

"Seventy percent equity," Ren said flatly.

Mei's expression darkened. "That's robbery."

"It's business," Li Chen said, surprising them both. "High risk, high reward. He's betting on us failing, so he wants maximum payout if we succeed."

"Or he's betting on us succeeding and wants maximum control," Ren replied. "Either way, we lose."

They walked back to the temple in silence, the weight of the decision pressing down on them. The misty streets of the mortal city seemed duller than before, the colors washed out.

Back at the temple, something was wrong.

The front door was open.

Ren's hand went to his sword—a cheap iron blade, barely sharp enough to cut vegetables, but it was all he had. Mei and Li Chen flanked him, their own weapons drawn.

They crept inside.

The main hall was ransacked. Scrolls scattered across the floor. The offering table overturned. The small spirit stone lamp they used for cultivation was smashed, its crystals glittering like broken stars.

And in the center of the room, sitting cross-legged on the debris, was a woman.

She was beautiful in the way a blade is beautiful—sharp, precise, dangerous. Her robes were black silk embroidered with silver lotuses. Her eyes were the color of amber, and they fixed on Ren with predatory focus.

"Ren Wei," she said. Her voice was low, melodic, completely at odds with the destruction around her. "I've been looking for you."

"Who are you?" Ren demanded, his sword shaking in his grip.

"My name is Lian Feng. I represent the Black Lotus Syndicate." She smiled, and it was the coldest thing Ren had ever seen. "Your disciple Li Chen owes us four hundred spirit stones. He's missed three payment deadlines. The Syndicate doesn't appreciate missed deadlines."

Li Chen paled. "I was going to pay—"

"Silence." Lian Feng didn't even look at him. Her eyes stayed on Ren. "The Syndicate is willing to forgive the debt. In exchange for a favor."

"A favor," Ren repeated. "What kind of favor?"

"Consortium Master Huo has a vault beneath the Azure Pavilion. Inside it is a jade box, sealed with nine formation locks. I want you to steal it for me."

The room went quiet.

"You want me to rob my potential investor," Ren said slowly, "for a criminal syndicate."

"I want you to choose," Lian Feng corrected. "Work with us, and your disciple's debt disappears. My organization will also provide funding—no equity required. A loan, at reasonable interest. Much more generous than what Huo offered."

"And if I refuse?"

Lian Feng's smile didn't waver. She pulled a small token from her sleeve—a black jade pendant shaped like a lotus flower. She placed it on the floor between them.

"Then Li Chen dies tonight. And we'll consider the debt settled with his blood."

Mei grabbed Ren's arm. "Don't. This is a trap. She's playing you against Huo."

"Of course it's a trap," Lian Feng said cheerfully. "But it's the only game in town. Huo wants to own you. I want to use you. Which is worse?"

Ren looked at Li Chen. His disciple's face was ashen, his hands trembling. The drunk sword master who could down a bottle and still cut through a formation was terrified.

Because he knew what the Black Lotus did to people who crossed them.

"What's in the box?" Ren asked.

Lian Feng's smile widened. "That's not your concern. Your concern is getting it for me. You have three days."

She stood, brushed the dust from her robes, and walked toward the broken door. She paused at the threshold.

"Oh, and Ren Wei? Don't try to run. We'll find you. We always do."

Then she was gone, swallowed by the mist outside.

The three of them stood in the ruined temple, the black jade pendant glinting on the floor like an accusation.

"What do we do?" Mei whispered.

Ren stared at the pendant. Two offers. Two traps. One way out, maybe, if he was clever enough.

"We play both sides," he said slowly. "And we make sure neither of them wins."

Li Chen looked up, hope flickering in his eyes. "You have a plan?"

"No." Ren picked up the pendant, feeling its cold weight. "But I'm going to make one. And it's going to be the most insane thing this world has ever seen."

Mei sighed. "Why do I always end up in these situations?"

"Because you're my partner," Ren said. "And because we're going to build something that changes everything."

He looked at the pendant, then toward the direction of the Azure Pavilion.

"Even if we have to burn the old world down to do it."

The mist swallowed the temple as night fell, and somewhere in the Jade District, Consortium Master Huo received a message on a jade slip. He read it once, twice, and then smiled.

"So," he murmured, "the Black Lotus has made their move. Good. Let's see how our little startup founder handles real pressure."

He tapped the desk, and a formation activated, sealing the vault beneath the pavilion with nine new locks.

Just in case.

title: The Fine Print That Bites
content:
The study smelled of old paper and ambition. Consortium Master Huo leaned back in his chair, steepling his fingers, and studied Ren with the patient attention of a predator who had already decided when to strike.

"I appreciate directness," Huo said. "Most petitioners waste my time with flattery and false humility. You present your case like a businessman, not a beggar. Refreshing."

Ren kept his expression neutral. "Then you've considered my proposal?"

"I've considered many things." Huo reached into his robe and produced a jade slip, placing it on the obsidian desk between them. The slip glowed with inner light, characters shifting across its surface like living things. "I had my analysts evaluate your sect's potential. The results were... interesting."

Ren didn't reach for the slip. "Interesting how?"

"Your 'minimum viable product' approach is unprecedented in the cultivation world. Traditional sects spend centuries building foundations before expanding. You're building the tracks while the train is running." Huo's smile sharpened. "It's either genius or suicide. Possibly both."

"I prefer 'calculated risk.'"

"Of course you do." Huo tapped the jade slip. "My analysts identified three critical vulnerabilities in your model. First, your reliance on mortal infrastructure. Your temple is in a mortal city, which means your supply chains, information networks, and recruitment pool are all mortal-scaled. One major sect decides to blockade you, and you starve."

"We have contingency plans."

"Your contingency plans involve running. I read them." Huo's eyes glinted. "Second, your talent pipeline. You have three disciples. One is a drunk whose cultivation is crippled by his own addiction. One is a formation expert whose gambling debts make her a security risk. One is a chef who 'accidentally' cultivates. Your entire sect's combat capability relies on a former Celestial Sword Sect reject who once lost a spar to a training dummy."

Ren's jaw tightened. "I lost that spar because the dummy was rigged with a paralysis formation."

"Excuses are not assets, Ren Wei." Huo leaned forward. "But I'm not here to mock you. I'm here to offer a solution."

The word *solution* hung in the air like a blade.

"I'll invest," Huo said. "One hundred high-grade spirit stones upfront. Access to Azure Pond's merchant networks. Protection against hostile sects — within reason. In exchange..."

Ren's heart rate didn't change. He'd been expecting this. "In exchange for what?"

"Forty-nine percent equity in New Dao Sect."

The number hit Ren like a physical blow. Forty-nine percent. A controlling interest, if the remaining shares were split among the disciples. He kept his face still, but his mind raced.

"That's not standard," he said carefully.

"Nothing about your sect is standard." Huo's tone was pleasant, utterly unbothered. "I'm taking a risk on an unproven entity with three misfits and a crumbling temple. Forty-nine percent compensates for the probability that you'll fail within the year."

"And if we don't fail?"

"Then I own nearly half of a rising power. We both win."

Ren was silent for a long moment. The terms were predatory, but not unreasonable by cultivation world standards. Major sects took ninety percent from new affiliates. Forty-nine was practically generous.

But the cost wasn't just financial. With that much equity, Huo could block any major decision. New disciples. New locations. New revenue streams. Every move Ren made would require the consortium master's approval.

He was trading independence for survival.

"I'll need to discuss this with my disciples," Ren said.

"You have one hour." Huo gestured to the door. "The Azure Pavilion has excellent tea. My assistants will provide refreshments while you deliberate. But Ren Wei?"

Ren paused at the door.

"The offer expires at noon. After that, the terms change."

The door closed behind him with a soft click.

---

Mei and Li Chen were waiting in the reception area, surrounded by cups of tea they hadn't touched. Mei stood the moment Ren appeared, her eyes searching his face.

"Well?"

"Forty-nine percent."

Mei's expression didn't change, but her hands tightened into fists. Li Chen let out a low whistle.

"That's aggressive."

"That's a takeover," Mei corrected. "He's not investing. He's buying control."

"Technically, he's buying influence," Ren said, sitting down heavily. "Forty-nine percent doesn't give him operational control, but it gives him veto power over anything major. New disciples, new locations, new partnerships. We'd need his approval for everything."

"Then we say no." Li Chen shrugged. "We've survived this long."

"We've survived on luck and scraps." Ren rubbed his temples. "We're in a mortal city. Our strongest member is a drunk who can barely hold a sword. If a single Foundation Establishment cultivator decides we're a threat, we're dead. Huo's protection is worth something."

"It's worth everything," Mei said quietly. "That's the problem."

Ren looked at her. "Explain."

Mei's eyes were distant, calculating. "Forty-nine percent means he's invested in our success. But it also means he can afford to let us fail — and then buy the rest of the sect at a discount from our heirs. If we accept, we're not just partners. We're hostages to his patience."

"Then we negotiate."

"With what leverage?" Li Chen asked. "We're here because we need him. He knows it. The only reason he's offering at all is because he sees potential. But he also sees desperation."

Ren was quiet. His mind churned through options, each one more desperate than the last. Refuse the offer and risk the sect's collapse. Accept and risk losing everything anyway. Try to find another investor, but who would take a chance on them now?

"There's one more option," he said slowly.

Mei and Li Chen leaned in.

"We accept," Ren said. "But we structure the deal so that the equity doesn't vest immediately. Performance-based milestones. If we hit certain targets within a year, the equity ratio shifts in our favor. If we fail, he gets more. It's a gamble, but it gives us time to grow strong enough to buy him out."

Mei's frown deepened. "That's still giving him leverage."

"It's giving him *incentive* to help us succeed. If his equity only fully vests when we hit our targets, he's motivated to support us, not sabotage us." Ren met her eyes. "And it buys us time."

"Time for what?"

"Time to find a better option."

Li Chen chuckled darkly. "That's not a plan. That's a hope."

"It's the best we've got."

Mei was silent for a long moment. Then she nodded. "Do it. But I want to see the contract before you sign anything."

Ren stood. "I'll bring it to you."

---

He returned to Huo's study with his counteroffer ready. The consortium master listened without interrupting, his expression unreadable. When Ren finished, Huo was silent for a full thirty seconds.

"Performance-based equity vesting," Huo repeated. "With milestones tied to revenue, disciple count, and combat capability. If you meet all targets within a year, your equity stays at fifty-one percent. If you miss, I gain up to sixty percent."

"Correct."

Huo leaned back, tapping his fingers on the desk. "It's risky for me. If you fail, I own more of a failing enterprise."

"Then help us succeed."

A thin smile crossed Huo's face. "I like you, Ren Wei. You think like a businessman, even when you're playing a cultivator's game." He reached into his robe and produced a scroll of spirit parchment, unrolling it across the desk. "I had my scribes prepare a preliminary contract. You'll find it... accommodating."

Ren read the contract carefully. Every clause. Every subclause. The fine print that could hide traps. It took him twenty minutes, and by the end, his head ached from the density of it.

But it was clean. No hidden fees. No automatic renewals. No clauses that would transfer ownership upon his death or disappearance.

He signed.

Huo signed.

The contract glowed with spiritual light, the characters sealing themselves into the parchment with the weight of celestial law.

"Pleasure doing business, partner," Huo said, extending his hand.

Ren shook it. The grip was firm, but not crushing. A businessman's handshake, not a warrior's.

"I'll have the spirit stones delivered to your temple by evening," Huo continued. "And I'll send an envoy to discuss the first milestone. I suggest you focus on recruitment. Three disciples won't conquer the cultivation world."

"We're not trying to conquer it."

"No?" Huo's smile widened. "Then why are you here?"

Ren left the study without answering.

---

The walk back to the temple was quiet. Mei and Li Chen flanked him, their silence heavy with unspoken thoughts. The mortal city buzzed around them, merchants hawking their wares, children playing in the streets, the smell of cooking food drifting from open windows.

It felt like another world. A simpler world.

"Something's wrong," Mei said suddenly.

Ren stopped. "What?"

"The contract. You said it was clean. That's the problem." Mei's eyes were sharp, focused. "Huo is a predator. Predators don't leave clean contracts unless they've hidden the trap somewhere else."

"I checked every clause."

"Then the trap isn't in the contract." Mei's voice dropped. "It's in the relationship."

Li Chen frowned. "What do you mean?"

Mei turned to face them fully. "Think about it. Huo now owns forty-nine percent of New Dao Sect. That means he has access to our financial records. Our recruitment logs. Our strategic plans. He knows everything we do, and he can use that information however he wants."

"He's bound by the contract's non-disclosure clauses."

"Contracts can be broken." Mei's voice was flat. "And even if he doesn't break it, he can use what he learns to anticipate our moves. He's not just an investor. He's a mole inside our organization."

Ren's blood went cold.

"He's been inside our organization," he said slowly, "for the past thirty minutes."

Mei's face went pale.

They ran.

---

The temple was intact when they arrived. The wards were undisturbed. The doors were locked. Everything looked normal.

But the moment Ren stepped inside, he knew.

The scroll was on his desk. A simple bamboo tube, sealed with green wax. No markings. No sender.

He broke the seal and unrolled the message.

*Your disciple is safe. For now.*

*Your next investor meeting is in three days.*

*Don't be late.*

*— A Friend*

Ren's hands trembled as he read the words again. His disciples were accounted for. Lin was drunk in the kitchen. Old Man Zhang was passed out in the courtyard. Little Chef was... where was Little Chef?

"Little Chef!" Ren shouted.

No answer.

He ran through the temple, checking every room. The kitchen was empty, a half-chopped vegetable on the cutting board. The meditation room was vacant. The storage cellar was undisturbed.

Little Chef was gone.

Ren stood in the empty kitchen, the scroll clutched in his hand, and felt the first cold touch of true fear.

The investor meeting wasn't a choice.

It was a trap.

And he'd walked right into it.

title: The Price of Momentum
content:
Ren held Huo's gaze, refusing to blink. He'd learned that trick from watching debt collectors intimidate shopkeepers. Never show weakness. Never look away first.

Huo smiled, a thin curve that didn't reach his eyes. "You speak well, Ren Wei. But words are cheap. What can your sect actually deliver?"

Ren pulled out his bamboo scroll, unrolled it on the obsidian desk. "Three months. I'll establish a working sect with at least fifty disciples, a stable income stream, and a proprietary cultivation method that doesn't suck."

Huo raised an eyebrow. "Doesn't suck. Your terminology is... refreshing."

"I'm a startup founder. We speak the language of results, not poetry."

Huo's finger traced the scroll, pausing at the numbers. "You've overestimated your revenue projections by thirty percent. And your cost estimates don't account for bribery, protection payments, or the inevitable sabotage from established sects."

Ren's heart sank. The man was good. "How do you know about the sabotage?"

"Because I've funded twelve sects in the past decade. Seven are still standing. The other five?" Huo's smile turned cold. "Their founders learned the hard way that the cultivation world doesn't welcome change. Old money fights back."

"So you're warning me off?"

"I'm testing you." Huo leaned back. "Your proposal is aggressive, borderline reckless. But it's also the first new idea I've seen in thirty years. Everyone else comes to me with the same tired plans. Build a bigger hall. Hoard more spirit stones. Crush your enemies. Boring."

Ren's fingers tightened on the scroll. "So what are you offering?"

"Full funding. Fifty thousand spirit stones upfront. Access to my cultivation network. Protection from the major sects for the first year."

Ren's mouth went dry. That was triple what he'd hoped for. "What's the catch?"

Huo laughed, a genuine sound that filled the room. "Smart boy. The catch is that I own forty percent of New Dao Sect. Not a partnership. An equity stake. I get a seat on your leadership council. I get veto power over major decisions for the first five years."

Ren's mind raced. Forty percent? Veto power? That was worse than the Celestial Sword Sect's indentured apprenticeship program.

"I'll give you twenty percent, advisory status only, no veto."

"Thirty-five percent, veto limited to financial decisions above ten thousand spirit stones."

"Twenty-five percent, but you provide introductions to three major investors within six months."

Huo's eyes glittered. "Thirty percent, introductions to five investors, and you take one of my people as your finance director."

Ren's jaw tightened. A plant. Someone to report his every move back to Azure Pond.

"Deal," he said, extending his hand. "But your finance director reports to me. And I can fire them if they interfere with cultivation operations."

Huo shook his hand, his grip firm. "Acceptable. I'll have the contracts drawn up by tonight. We sign tomorrow at noon."

Ren stood, his legs slightly unsteady. He'd done it. He'd actually done it.

"One more thing," Huo said as Ren reached the door. "I've heard rumors about your disciples. A drunk sword master. A gambling formation expert. A chef who cultivates by accident."

Ren turned, his expression neutral. "They're talented."

"They're liabilities." Huo's voice hardened. "Keep them in line, Ren Wei. Or I will."

The threat hung in the air like a blade.

Ren walked out.

---

Mei met him in the reception area, her eyes hungry for information. "Well?"

"Thirty percent equity. Full funding. A finance director we don't get to choose."

Mei's face cycled through several emotions before settling on grudging acceptance. "Could be worse. Could be fifty percent."

"He threatened the disciples."

"Of course he did. That's what investors do. They find your weaknesses and squeeze." Mei grabbed his arm, pulling him toward the exit. "Come on. Li Chen found something."

They found the drunk sword master in a teahouse two streets away, nursing a cup of rice wine that he'd probably refilled seventeen times. He looked up as they entered, his eyes sharp despite the alcohol.

"There's a problem," Li Chen said, not bothering with pleasantries.

"There's always a problem," Ren replied, sitting down heavily. "What is it this time?"

"The major sects know about your meeting with Huo. The Celestial Sword Sect has already sent a delegation to the city. They're staying at the Golden Lotus Inn, three blocks from here."

Ren's blood ran cold. "How many?"

"Twelve. All at least Qi Condensation level. Their leader is Elder Bai, the same man who fired you."

Mei's hand found Ren's shoulder. "They're here to stop you."

"No," Ren said slowly, pieces clicking into place. "They're here to test me. If I can't handle a delegation, Huo will pull his funding. This is the first challenge."

Li Chen drained his cup. "So what's the plan?"

Ren thought. Fighting was suicide. Running would prove weakness. Negotiation was impossible — Elder Bai hated him.

"We do what startups do best," Ren said finally. "We outmaneuver them."

---

The next morning, Ren woke to find a letter slipped under his temple's broken door.

*Ren Wei,*

*Congratulations on your funding. I always knew you had potential — just not at my sect. Consider this a friendly warning: the path you've chosen leads to ruin. I've seen a hundred would-be founders try to break the system. They all failed.*

*Come talk to me before you sign those contracts. Maybe I can offer you a way out.*

*Elder Bai*

*P.S. Your temple's property deed is technically still owned by the Celestial Sword Sect. We lent it to you as a "probationary assignment." You might want to check the small print.*

Ren's hands shook. The temple. The one asset he actually owned. If Bai was telling the truth...

"Mei!" he shouted. "Get the deed!"

She appeared from the kitchen, flour on her face — she'd been trying to make breakfast cultivation pills again. "What's wrong?"

"Bai claims we don't actually own the temple."

Mei's face went pale. She ran to the back room, rummaged through their documents, and emerged with a yellowed scroll. Her eyes scanned it once, twice, three times.

"Oh no."

"What?"

"It's true. The deed says 'temporary stewardship granted to Ren Wei, revocable at any time by the Celestial Sword Sect Council.' We've been squatting."

Ren slammed his fist against the wall. The rotten wood cracked.

"He planned this. Fired me, gave me the temple as a 'favor,' and kept the deed as leverage. The moment I succeed, he pulls the rug."

Mei's eyes hardened. "Then we make sure he can't pull it."

"How?"

"By making the temple worth more to us than to them." She grabbed his arm. "Come on. We're going to the mortal city records office."

---

The records office was a dusty building in the administrative district, staffed by bored bureaucrats who couldn't cultivate but had memorized every law in the book. Mei charmed the clerk with a smile and a few spirit stones, and twenty minutes later, they had their answer.

"Temple property can be transferred," the clerk said, adjusting his spectacles. "If the current steward has occupied it continuously for more than three years without dispute, they can file for adverse possession. It becomes theirs."

Ren's heart leaped. "How long have I been there?"

Mei counted on her fingers. "Three years and two months. We're close."

"Close isn't enough," the clerk said. "You need exactly three years of *uninterrupted* occupation. If the original owner can prove you left for more than thirty consecutive days during that period..."

Ren's stomach dropped. He'd left for a month when the Celestial Sword Sect summoned him for a "voluntary" labor assignment. A month exactly.

"Bai planned this," he whispered. "He knew."

Mei grabbed his arm again. "Then we change the plan. We don't fight the deed. We make the deed irrelevant."

"How?"

"We build something so valuable that the Celestial Sword Sect looks petty for trying to take it back. Public opinion matters, Ren. Even in the cultivation world."

Ren stared at her. "You want to weaponize reputation."

"I want to weaponize *everything*." Mei's eyes blazed. "This is a startup. We move fast, we break things, and we make sure our enemies look like bullies when they try to stop us."

The clerk cleared his throat. "There is one more option. You could challenge Elder Bai to a formal debate. Winner takes the temple. It's an old legal tradition, rarely used anymore, but technically valid."

Ren and Mei exchanged glances.

"A debate?" Ren said slowly. "Against a man with fifty years more experience?"

"He's a traditionalist," Mei said, thinking. "He'd never expect a startup founder to use legal loopholes. You could win."

"Or I could get humiliated in front of the entire city."

"Risk is part of the job description." Mei grinned. "You wanted to build a sect? This is how you build a sect. By taking stupid risks and somehow surviving."

Ren looked at the deed in his hands, then at the clerk's hopeful face, then at his disciple's insane confidence.

"Set up the debate," he said. "Tomorrow afternoon. Public square."

Mei whooped. The clerk scribbled notes. And somewhere in the city, Elder Bai was probably laughing at how easily Ren fell into his trap.

But Ren had a secret weapon.

He had nothing left to lose.

---

The next afternoon, the public square was packed. Cultivators, mortals, merchants, beggars — everyone had heard about the challenge. A startup founder versus an elder of the Celestial Sword Sect. David versus Goliath, but with cultivation.

Ren stood on one side of the raised platform, his robes patched but clean. Li Chen stood behind him, sober for once. Mei had her ledger out, already calculating how to monetize the spectacle.

Elder Bai arrived in a cloud of spiritual pressure, his robes immaculate, his face a mask of disdain. He stepped onto the platform like he owned it — because technically, he did.

"Ren Wei," Bai said, his voice carrying across the square. "I'm surprised you showed up. I thought you'd run."

"I'm a founder, Elder Bai. We don't run. We pivot."

The crowd murmured, confused by the term.

Bai's lip curled. "You and your ridiculous business jargon. You think the cultivation world runs on spreadsheets and quarterly reports?"

"I think it runs on value creation. Something your sect hasn't done in a century."

The crowd gasped. Direct insult to an elder. This was going to be good.

Bai's face reddened. "You insolent—"

"Three years ago," Ren interrupted, "you fired me for 'insufficient talent.' But I've been studying. I've learned that talent isn't fixed. It's cultivated. Just like spirit power. Just like business."

"You're wasting my time."

"Am I?" Ren pulled out a scroll. "Because I've prepared a full analysis of the Celestial Sword Sect's declining revenue, shrinking disciple pool, and outdated cultivation methods. Want me to read it aloud?"

Bai's eyes widened. "You wouldn't dare."

"Elder Bai, I'm a startup founder. I have nothing to lose and everything to gain. Watch me."

Ren unrolled the scroll

title: The Soul Collateral Clause
content:
The negotiation lasted six hours.

Ren emerged from the study with trembling hands, a splitting headache, and the distinct feeling he'd just wrestled a spirit beast and lost. Consortium Master Huo had picked apart every assumption, questioned every number, and demanded proof for every claim. By the end, Ren had conceded on valuation, equity split, and timeline expectations.

But he'd gotten the investment.

Three thousand spirit stones upfront. Access to Azure Pond's distribution network. A guaranteed meeting with the Jade Market's merchant council. In exchange, Huo's consortium would own forty percent of New Dao Sect, with a right of first refusal on any future funding rounds.

Standard terms. Predatory, but standard.

"The contract is binding," Huo had said, sealing it with his blood sigil. "I look forward to seeing what you build, Ren Wei. Don't disappoint me."

Ren didn't miss the threat buried in those words.

---

Mei and Li Chen were waiting outside, surrounded by cups of tea that had long gone cold. Mei jumped to her feet the moment Ren appeared, her eyes scanning his face for answers.

"Well?"

"Three thousand spirit stones. Forty percent equity. Distribution access."

Mei's eyes went wide. "Three *thousand*? That's—that's more than the annual budget of a fourth-rank sect!"

"It's also forty percent of everything we'll ever build." Ren rubbed his temples. "He knows we're desperate. He used it."

Li Chen grunted from his chair. "At least we're not dead. I call that a win."

"Low bar, Senior Brother."

"The only bar that matters."

They left the Azure Pavilion with a storage ring containing the first installment — one thousand spirit stones, delivered immediately — and a jade slip containing the contract terms. Ren felt the weight of it in his pocket like a lead brick.

The walk back to the temple was silent.

---

The next three weeks were a blur of activity.

With actual resources, Ren could finally implement the plans he'd been sketching for months. The temple's foundation was reinforced with spirit stone dust mixed into the mortar. New buildings rose in the outer courtyard — a dormitory for future disciples, a meditation hall, a proper alchemy workshop. Mei hired a team of mortal craftsmen to repair the walls and commissioned a proper sect banner from a silk merchant who specialized in cultivation-grade materials.

Li Chen, for his part, finally had the budget to purchase real intelligence. He returned from his nightly excursions with stacks of reports: sect movements, market prices, political shifts in the mortal kingdoms below. Ren devoured them all, building a mental map of the cultivation world's intricate power structures.

The drunk sword master, Old Wen, spent his days in the meditation hall, staring at a wall. Ren wasn't sure what he was doing, but the man's spiritual pressure had increased noticeably. Either he was actually cultivating, or he'd found a way to meditate while hungover.

Probably both.

The gambling formation expert, Little Feng, had finally paid off his debts and was now designing a defensive array for the temple. He'd promised it would be "the finest formation outside the major sects." Ren had demanded regular progress reports and was actually impressed by the diagrams.

And the chef, Big Huang, had discovered that spirit stones could be ground into powder and used as seasoning. The result was a stir-fry that temporarily boosted qi circulation. Ren immediately added "qi-boosting cuisine" to the sect's service offerings.

They were growing. Slowly, but genuinely.

---

The problem came on a Tuesday.

Ren was reviewing accounts in his new office — a converted storage room with a desk, a chair, and a window that actually opened — when a messenger crane landed on his windowsill. The bird was made of folded paper, animated by a simple formation, and carried a scroll sealed with Azure Pond's insignia.

He broke the seal and read.

*Ren Wei,*

*Per Section 14, Subsection C of our agreement, I am exercising my right to appoint a liaison to oversee operations. Representative Jiang will arrive within the week to conduct an initial audit and establish reporting protocols.*

*I trust you will extend him every courtesy.*

*Huo Feng*
*Consortium Master, Azure Pond*

Ren read the letter three times, each time feeling colder.

Section 14, Subsection C. He'd skimmed that part, assuming it was standard oversight language. But "liaison" in the cultivation business world meant one thing: a spy. An enforcer. Someone who would report every decision, every expense, every whisper back to Huo.

He'd signed away not just equity, but control.

---

Representative Jiang arrived four days later.

He was a thin man with a perpetual sneer, dressed in robes that cost more than Ren's entire sect. His cultivation was at the eighth level of Qi Condensation — respectable, but not threatening. What made him dangerous was the jade token pinned to his chest: the mark of Azure Pond's internal audit division.

"Master Ren," Jiang said, not bothering to bow. "I trust you've prepared the financial records?"

Ren forced a smile. "Of course, Representative Jiang. Right this way."

The tour took two hours. Jiang examined everything — the new buildings, the formation diagrams, the spirit stone accounts. He asked pointed questions about expenditures, questioned the necessity of the qi-boosting cuisine research, and frowned at the number of spirit stones dedicated to "intelligence gathering" (Li Chen's department).

When they reached Ren's office, Jiang produced a scroll and began writing.

"Initial findings," he said, not looking up. "Operational efficiency: moderate. Resource allocation: questionable. Oversight protocols: nonexistent. I'll need weekly reports on all major decisions. Monthly budget reviews. Quarterly strategic assessments."

"That's... a lot of paperwork."

"That's the minimum required to protect Azure Pond's investment." Jiang finally looked up, his eyes cold. "I'm sure you understand. Consortium Master Huo is *very* protective of his assets."

The threat was clear. Follow the rules, or face consequences.

---

That night, Ren gathered his core team in the meditation hall.

Old Wen sat in the corner, polishing his sword with a rag that looked like it had once been a shirt. Little Feng was arranging formation flags in a pattern that might have been strategic or might have been a gambling system. Big Huang had brought samples of his new recipes, which everyone was eating because they were delicious and also because refusing a cultivator-chef's food was considered rude.

Mei and Li Chen sat on either side of Ren, their faces grim.

"We have a problem," Ren said.

"Just one?" Li Chen snorted. "I can name at least seven."

"The liaison. Jiang. He's here to control us."

Mei nodded. "I figured. What are we going to do about it?"

Ren spread his hands. "Legally? Nothing. The contract is binding. If we violate the terms, Huo can seize our assets and dissolve the sect."

"Then we work around him."

"How?"

Mei smiled — a sharp, predatory expression that Ren had learned to fear. "We give him exactly what he asks for. Reports. Audits. Data. But we control what the data says."

"You're suggesting we cook the books?"

"I'm suggesting we *frame* the narrative. There's a difference."

Li Chen leaned forward. "She's right. The mortal merchant houses do it all the time. Report everything in the most favorable light. Obscure your real margins. Make it look like you're spending more than you are on growth, then hide your true profits."

Ren considered this. It was risky. If they were caught, Huo would have every right to destroy them.

But if they weren't caught...

"Fine," he said. "We do it. But we need someone who knows the accounting tricks. Someone who's worked with Azure Pond before."

Everyone looked at Little Feng.

The formation expert blinked. "Why are you all staring at me?"

"You used to work for the Golden Coin Sect," Mei said. "They're Azure Pond's biggest competitor. You must know their methods."

"That was years ago! And I was just a junior auditor!"

"Good enough." Ren stood. "Little Feng, you're our new financial advisor. Congratulations."

"But—"

"You're also getting a raise. Five spirit stones per month."

Little Feng's protests died in his throat. "...Fine. But I'm not responsible if we get audited by the Heavenly Revenue Bureau."

"The what?"

"Never mind. You don't want to know."

---

The next morning, Jiang arrived at Ren's office with a stack of forms.

"Daily expense reports," he said, dropping them on the desk. "I need them by noon."

Ren looked at the stack. It was easily three hundred pages.

"For *one day*?"

"Consortium Master Huo is thorough." Jiang's smile was thin. "I'm sure a sect leader of your caliber can handle a little paperwork."

He left without another word.

Ren stared at the forms. His eye twitched.

"I'm going to kill him," he muttered. "I'm going to kill him and feed his body to Big Huang's experimental recipes."

"Please don't," came a voice from the doorway. Big Huang stood there, holding a steaming bowl. "I have standards."

Ren sighed. "What's that?"

"Breakfast. Soul-reviving porridge. It'll help with the headache you're about to get."

Ren took the bowl. It was surprisingly good.

"Thanks."

"Don't mention it." Big Huang hesitated. "Also, I've been meaning to tell you something."

"What?"

"The qi-boosting stir-fry? I've been testing it on the local mortals. They're showing signs of minor qi deviation."

Ren froze. "What?"

"Nothing serious. Just some dizziness and nosebleeds. But I think the spirit stone powder is too concentrated. I need to dilute it."

"How many mortals have you tested it on?"

"Twenty-three."

"Big Huang!"

"What? They volunteered! I paid them!"

Ren buried his face in his hands. This was going to be a long day.

---

Three weeks passed.

Jiang's presence became a constant, grinding irritation. He demanded reports on everything, questioned every expense, and found fault in every decision. Ren spent more time filling out forms than actually building his sect. Morale was plummeting.

But they were making progress.

Little Feng's defensive array was complete. It wasn't flashy — just a subtle formation that would alert them to intruders and slow down anyone who tried to break in. But it was a start.

Old Wen had finally emerged from his meditation, claiming to have achieved a breakthrough in his sword technique. Ren hadn't seen it yet, but the man's eyes were clearer and his hands steadier than they'd been in months.

Mei had secured three new contracts with local merchants, offering "cultivation consultation services" that were really just her showing up and looking impressive. The merchants didn't care about actual cultivation advice; they just wanted to say they'd hired a cultivator. It was a racket, but it was profitable.

And Big Huang's qi-boosting cuisine had become a minor sensation. The local mortals couldn't get enough of it, despite the occasional nosebleed. Ren had ordered him to dilute the recipe and stop testing on humans, but the damage was done.

For the first time, New Dao Sect was actually working.

Then the letter arrived.

It was delivered by a spirit crow, its eyes glowing with malevolent intelligence. The scroll was sealed with black wax, stamped with a sigil Ren recognized immediately: the Celestial Sword Sect.

He broke the seal with shaking hands.

*Ren Wei,*

*It has come to our attention that your so-called "sect" has been engaging in unauthorized cultivation activities within territory traditionally claimed by the Celestial Sword Sect. Specifically, the development of "qi-boosting cuisine" using spirit stone powder constitutes a violation

title: Chapter 10
content:
CHAPTER 10: The Price of a Name

The negotiation lasted six hours.

Ren emerged from the study feeling like he'd been wrung through a spiritual press. His throat was dry, his head throbbing, and his robes were soaked with sweat despite the cooling formations embedded in the walls. But in his storage ring sat a jade contract, signed in Consortium Master Huo's own blood, promising an initial investment of five thousand low-grade spirit stones.

Five thousand.

He'd been prepared to beg for five hundred.

Mei was waiting in the reception area, chatting animatedly with the receptionist about something called "vertical integration of supply chains." The receptionist looked simultaneously fascinated and terrified.

"Well?" Mei asked, her eyes sharp.

Ren held up the storage ring. "We're funded."

Mei's face broke into a grin so wide it looked painful. "How much?"

"Five thousand. Initial tranche. With milestones for additional funding based on disciple recruitment and mission completion rates."

"Five—" Mei grabbed his arm, her nails digging in. "Ren. That's enough to buy a real headquarters. Actual training facilities. A formation array that doesn't leak spiritual energy into the sewers."

"Don't spend it yet," Ren said, though his own heart was racing. "First tranche is conditional on us completing the Azure Pond's initial assessment. They want to verify our capabilities before releasing the funds."

"What kind of assessment?"

"Standard due diligence. They want us to complete a B-rank mission within thirty days. Solo sect effort."

Mei's grin faltered. "B-rank? Ren, that's a mid-level demon beast or a rogue cultivator at the peak of Qi Condensation. We have three disciples. One of whom is drunk, one of whom is missing half his fingers from gambling debts, and one of whom thinks 'cultivation' means 'adding more chili oil.'"

"We have a sword master who once cut a waterfall in half," Ren countered.

"Twenty years ago! Before he discovered rice wine!"

"We have a formation expert who can trace arrays in his sleep."

"Only when he's winning at cards!"

Ren took a deep breath. "Do you trust me?"

Mei stared at him for a long moment. Then she sighed, the fight draining out of her. "Against my better judgment. Yes. What's the mission?"

---

The Azure Pond's mission board was a massive jade tablet that floated in the center of their main hall, constantly updating with new requests from across the region. Ren had expected something manageable—a territorial dispute between minor sects, a haunted mine that needed clearing, maybe a rogue beast that had wandered too close to a village.

What he got was worse.

"Subdue the Three Winds Bandits," he read aloud, his voice flat. "A group of rogue cultivators has been terrorizing trade routes through the Crimson Gorge. Estimated strength: three at mid Qi Condensation, one at late Qi Condensation. Leader known as 'Wind Blade' Zhao. Wanted alive for trial by the Azure Consortium."

Three mid-stage cultivators and a late-stage.

Ren was at early Qi Condensation. His disciples were... variable.

"This is a death sentence," Li Chen said, emerging from the shadows behind a pillar. He looked tired, his eyes bloodshot, but his hand was steady on his sword hilt. "I've heard of Wind Blade Zhao. He used to be an inner disciple of the Tempest Sect before he was expelled for 'excessive cruelty.' His wind techniques can slice through steel armor like paper."

"Then we don't wear steel armor," Ren said.

"That's not the point!"

"The point," Ren said, turning to face his disciples—all three of them had gathered now, drawn by the tension, "is that this is our first test. The Azure Pond wants to see if we're worth their investment. If we fail, we're back to nothing. If we succeed—"

"If we succeed, we're dead," Chen Wei interrupted. The chef was unusually sober, his eyes clear. "I've cooked for bandits before. The Three Winds aren't just random thugs. They're organized. They have lookouts, safe houses, informants in every village within fifty li. You can't just walk up to them and challenge them to a fight."

"No," Ren agreed. "That would be stupid."

"So what's your plan?"

Ren smiled. It was not a comforting smile. "We're going to use the one resource we have in abundance."

"What's that?"

"Insanity."

---

The plan, as Ren explained it over a map spread across the temple's broken altar, was simple in concept and suicidal in execution.

"We can't beat them in a direct fight," he said, pointing at the Crimson Gorge. "Their base is here, in a natural fortress. They have the high ground, defensive formations, and superior numbers. But they have one weakness."

"They're bandits?" Mei suggested.

"No. They're greedy."

Ren pulled out a stack of papers—fake documents he'd spent the night forging. "These are trade manifests. According to these, a 'merchant caravan' carrying ten thousand spirit stones worth of rare herbs and cultivation materials will be passing through the Gorge in three days."

"We don't have ten thousand spirit stones," Li Chen pointed out.

"We don't need them. We just need the bandits to *think* we do. I'm going to pose as the merchant. You three will be my guards. We'll walk right into their territory, let them 'capture' us, and then—"

"Get ourselves killed?" Chen Wei said.

"No. That's phase two."

---

Three days later, Ren found himself tied to a wooden post in the bandits' hideout, trying very hard not to panic.

The plan had worked perfectly. Too perfectly. The bandits had spotted them within an hour of entering the gorge. A dozen rogue cultivators had surrounded them, weapons drawn, laughing at the "merchant" who had wandered into their territory with only three guards.

Now Ren was in a cave lit by flickering torches, surrounded by men who looked like they'd kill him for the shoes on his feet. Wind Blade Zhao sat on a throne of stacked crates, a sword across his knees, his eyes cold and assessing.

"A merchant, you say?" Zhao's voice was soft, almost gentle. It made Ren's skin crawl. "With three guards who couldn't even put up a fight?"

"We surrender easily," Ren said, his voice trembling—not entirely an act. "We're businessmen, not fighters. Please, take the goods. Just let us go."

Zhao laughed. It was not a kind sound. "Let you go? You walked into my home with a fortune in spirit stones. Did you think I'd just pat you on the head and send you on your way?"

"I hoped—"

"Hope is for fools." Zhao stood, walking toward Ren with the casual grace of a predator. "I'm going to give you a choice. Join us, or die. Your guards too. We can always use fresh blood."

Ren's heart was pounding so hard he could barely hear himself think. This was the moment. The most dangerous part of the plan.

"I... I need to think about it," he said, buying time.

"Think fast. I'm not patient."

Behind Zhao, Ren saw a flicker of movement. A shadow that shouldn't be there. Chen Wei, who had somehow slipped his bonds, was crawling along the cave ceiling, a small pouch in his hand.

*Just a little longer.*

"I have a family," Ren said, his voice breaking. "A wife. Children. They need me."

"Then you should have stayed home." Zhao raised his sword.

The pouch flew.

Chen Wei's aim was perfect. The pouch landed at Zhao's feet, bursting open to release a cloud of fine red powder. Chili oil extract, mixed with crushed spirit herbs and a binding agent that made it cling to everything it touched.

Zhao screamed.

The powder was in his eyes, his nose, his mouth. He dropped his sword, clawing at his face, coughing and sputtering. Around him, the other bandits erupted into chaos, some blinded, some simply confused.

"Now!" Ren shouted.
