Li Chen exploded from his bonds, his sword singing as it left its sheath. Drunk or not, the man was a master. His blade moved like water, flowing through the bandits with surgical precision—not killing, but disabling. Tendons cut. Weapons knocked aside. Joints dislocated.

Mei was already working, her hands tracing formations in the air. A barrier went up around the cave entrance, trapping the bandits inside. A silence field followed, preventing them from calling for reinforcements.

Ren yanked his own ropes free—they'd been loose the whole time—and dove for Zhao's fallen sword. The bandit leader was still blinded, still choking, easy prey.

But easy prey was still dangerous.

Zhao's hand shot out, grabbing Ren's ankle. The grip was like iron. Ren crashed to the ground, the sword skittering away. Zhao rose above him, eyes red and streaming tears, but murderously clear.

"You think... a little spice... can stop me?"

He raised his fist.

And then he stopped.

A blade protruded from his chest.

Li Chen stood behind him, his expression calm, almost bored. "Actually, yes. It can."

Zhao fell.

For a long moment, the only sound in the cave was the heavy breathing of the survivors. The bandits were down. Li Chen was leaning on his sword. Mei was swaying on her feet, her formation work draining her. Chen Wei was still on the ceiling, looking down with wide eyes.

"We did it," Ren whispered.

Then the cave entrance exploded.

A figure walked through the smoke. Tall. Impossibly elegant. Wearing robes of pristine white that seemed to glow with their own light. A jade token hung from his belt—the symbol of the Celestial Sword Sect.

"Ren Wei," the figure said, his voice carrying the weight of absolute authority. "I've been looking for you."

Ren's blood turned to ice.

It was Elder Sheng. His former superior. The man who had personally signed his expulsion papers.

"You've been very busy," Sheng continued, stepping over the groaning bodies of the bandits. "Building a sect. Securing investors. Making a name for yourself." He smiled, and there was nothing kind in it. "But you forgot one thing, Ren."

"What's that?"

"The Celestial Sword Sect owns the rights to the name 'New Dao.' You're using our intellectual property."

Ren stared at him. "That's... that's ridiculous. New Dao isn't a trademark. It's a philosophy."

"It's ours now. We filed the paperwork three days ago. The Azure Consortium has already recognized our claim." Sheng's smile widened. "So here's your choice, Ren. Shut down your sect, surrender all assets, and sign over your disciples to us. Or face a lawsuit that will crush you so thoroughly that even the mortal courts will remember your name as a warning."

The storage ring on Ren's finger felt suddenly very heavy.

Five thousand spirit stones.

A signed contract.

And now this.

The cliff was steep, and the fall was long.

title: Chapter 11
content:
CHAPTER 11: The Price of Ambition

The negotiation lasted six hours.

Ren emerged from the study with a headache, a dry throat, and the feeling that he'd just survived a battle with a demon beast wearing human skin. Consortium Master Huo had dissected every claim, questioned every assumption, and poked holes in the business plan that Ren hadn't even known existed.

But in the end, they had a deal.

Two hundred high-grade spirit stones upfront. Access to Azure Pond's distribution network. A mentorship clause that gave Huo the right to appoint an "advisor" to New Dao Sect's board. In exchange, Azure Pond received forty percent equity and first refusal on all future funding rounds.

"Forty percent," Mei hissed as they walked out of the Jade District. "You gave away nearly half the sect!"

"The alternative was zero percent of nothing," Ren replied, rubbing his temples. "We were broke. We had three disciples, a crumbling temple, and a business model that consisted of 'figure it out as we go.' Huo gave us capital, connections, and legitimacy. Forty percent is expensive, but it's not unreasonable."

"It's unreasonable when he's practically running the sect through his 'advisor.'"

"The advisor only has veto power over major decisions. Operating decisions are still mine."

"For now."

Ren stopped walking. "Mei, I know you're worried. I am too. But this is how startups work. You give up control to get resources, then you grow fast enough that the control doesn't matter. By the time Huo realizes he's given us too much rope, we'll have built something too big to unravel."

"Or we'll have built a gallows and handed him the rope."

"That's the spirit."

They returned to the temple to find Li Chen sitting on the front steps, his sword across his knees, looking like he'd just watched his favorite dog die.

"What happened?" Ren asked.

Li Chen held up a jade slip. "Remember how I was gathering intelligence all night? Drinking with information brokers, gambling with city guards?"

"Yes."

"Turns out the information brokers were feeding me to the city guards. And the city guards were reporting to the local branch of the Celestial Sword Sect. And the local branch of the Celestial Sword Sect has been watching us since we arrived."

Ren's blood ran cold. "How long?"

"Since we left the temple. They knew about the meeting with Huo before we even walked into the Azure Pavilion."

Mei grabbed Ren's arm. "The deal. If the Celestial Sword Sect knows we're working with Azure Pond—"

"They'll try to block it," Ren finished. "Sabotage the distribution network. Lean on Huo to back out. Or worse."

"Or worse is already in motion," Li Chen said. He stood, his movements stiff. "I got a message from an old contact in the sect. They're sending an 'inspection team' to evaluate the temple. They're claiming that the land reverts to the Celestial Sword Sect if it's not used for 'proper cultivation purposes' for more than a decade."

"That's not how the law works."

"The Celestial Sword Sect *is* the law in this region. Or have you forgotten?"

Ren had not forgotten. He'd spent five years being reminded of that fact every single day.

---

The inspection team arrived three days later.

Ren had spent those three days preparing. He'd moved the spirit stones to a hidden vault beneath the meditation hall. He'd briefed his disciples on what to say and what not to say. He'd even hired a local calligrapher to create fake scrolls showing the temple as an "active cultivation site" with "continuous operation records" going back nine years.

It was a flimsy lie, but it was all he had.

The team consisted of five cultivators: three in the Qi Condensation stage, one at the peak of Foundation Establishment, and one who radiated an aura so oppressive that even Mei stopped complaining when he entered the room.

Elder Xu.

Ren knew him. Everyone in the Celestial Sword Sect knew him. He was the head of internal affairs, the man responsible for purging "undesirable elements" from the sect. He had the face of a kindly grandfather and the soul of a butcher.

"Ren Wei," Elder Xu said, his voice warm and welcoming. "It's been what, two years? You look well."

"I've been better, Elder Xu."

"I imagine so. Starting a new sect is never easy. Especially when you lack the resources and connections of the established orders." He walked past Ren, into the main hall, his eyes scanning everything. "This is... humble."

"We're still in our early stages."

"Indeed. Early stages." Elder Xu paused at the meditation cushions, which Mei had arranged in a perfect formation pattern. "These are new. The old ones were worn down to nothing."

"We replaced them."

"With what funds? I seem to recall that you left the sect with only your personal belongings and a small severance."

"The severance was quite generous."

Elder Xu's smile didn't waver. "Ren, we both know that's not true. The severance was three silver taels and a used sword. Hardly enough to buy new meditation cushions, let alone the jade walls I see in the back courtyard."

Ren's heart stopped.

The jade walls. The ones he'd bought with the first batch of spirit stones from Azure Pond. The ones that were supposed to be hidden behind a concealment formation.

"We've been renovating," Ren said, his voice steady despite the cold sweat running down his back.

"With what resources?"

"Investments."

"From whom?"

Ren met Elder Xu's eyes. "I'm not at liberty to say. The terms of our agreement include a confidentiality clause."

"A confidentiality clause." Elder Xu's smile finally faded. "Ren, I'm going to give you some advice, as someone who once thought highly of you. The Celestial Sword Sect has been the dominant power in this region for three thousand years. We've seen sects rise. We've seen sects fall. We've seen clever young cultivators think they could build something outside our orbit."

"I'm not trying to build outside your orbit."

"No? Then what are you trying to build?"

Ren took a breath. "Something new. Something that doesn't require crushing everyone else to succeed. A sect that competes through better service, better products, better cultivation methods. Not through political manipulation and violence."

Elder Xu stared at him for a long moment. Then he laughed.

It was not a kind laugh.

"You sound just like your master," he said. "Right before we expelled him."

Ren felt the words like a punch to the gut. "My master?"

"He never told you, did he? The great Ren Wei, founder of the New Dao Sect, doesn't even know his own history." Elder Xu shook his head. "Your master was a disciple of the Celestial Sword Sect. A talented one. He had the same ideas you do — that the old ways were inefficient, that there was a better path forward. We gave him the same chance we're giving you now."

"What chance?"

"The chance to walk away. To take your disciples and your dreams and go somewhere else. Somewhere far away from our territory."

"And if I refuse?"

Elder Xu's smile returned, but it was colder now. "Then we'll have to have a more serious conversation. About land rights. About unpaid taxes. About the illegal use of cultivation resources without proper registration."

"We have proper registration."

"Do you? Let me see your sect charter."

Ren's mouth went dry.

The sect charter. The document that officially recognized New Dao Sect as a legitimate cultivation organization. The document that required three signatures from established cultivators of the Foundation Establishment stage or higher.

The document that he'd been putting off getting because he didn't know three Foundation Establishment cultivators who would risk the Celestial Sword Sect's wrath to vouch for him.

"I thought so," Elder Xu said. "You have one month, Ren. One month to produce a valid sect charter, or this temple is seized and you're charged with unauthorized cultivation activities. The penalty is spiritual crippling and exile."

He turned and walked toward the door, his disciples falling into formation behind him.

"One month," he repeated, not looking back. "Use it wisely."

---

The silence after he left was deafening.

"We're dead," Mei said finally. "We're dead, we're dead, we're dead."

"We're not dead," Ren said, but even he didn't believe it.

"One month to get three Foundation Establishment signatures? Where are we supposed to find three cultivators willing to defy the Celestial Sword Sect?"

"The Azure Pond consortium—"

"Will not risk their entire operation for a forty percent stake in a failing startup. Huo might be willing to take a chance on us, but he's not stupid. If the Celestial Sword Sect is actively opposing us, he'll cut his losses and walk away."

"She's right," Li Chen said quietly. "I've seen this before. The Celestial Sword Sect doesn't make threats. They make promises. If they say they'll cripple you and take everything, they mean it."

Ren looked at his two disciples. At the crumbling temple around them. At the jade walls that had just exposed them.

He thought about giving up. About walking away. About finding some remote village where he could live out his days as a farmer or a merchant or anything other than a failed cultivator.

Then he thought about his master. The man Elder Xu had mentioned. The man who had tried the same thing and failed.

"I'm not going to walk away," Ren said.

"Ren—"

"I'm not going to walk away," he repeated, louder this time. "We have one month. That's thirty days to find three Foundation Establishment cultivators who will sign our charter."

"Where do we even start looking?"

Ren walked to the back of the hall, where a dusty bookshelf held the few possessions he'd brought from the Celestial Sword Sect. He pulled out a worn journal, its pages yellowed with age.

"My master left me this," he said. "I never opened it. I was too angry at him for abandoning me. But now..."

He opened the journal.

The first page was blank.

The second page had a single line of text: *"If you're reading this, Ren, it means they found you. Don't give up. Go to the place where the river meets the sky. Ask for the man who lost everything."*

"That's it?" Mei asked, reading over his shoulder. "That's your grand plan? A cryptic message from a dead man?"

"He's not dead," Ren said softly. "I don't think he ever was."

He closed the journal and looked at his disciples.

"We're going on a trip."

"Where?"

"The place where the river meets the sky. I think I know what it means."

"And if your master isn't there?"

Ren's expression hardened.

"Then I'll find someone else. There are always cultivators who hate the Celestial Sword Sect enough to help its enemies. We just have to find them before the month is up."

He walked toward the door, then stopped.

"And if I can't find them in time?"

He turned back, a grim smile on his face.

"Then I'll make them."

title: Chapter 12
content:
CHAPTER 12: The Spirit Stone Trap

The negotiation lasted three hours.

Ren emerged from the study with a parchment scroll clutched in his hands, his face carefully neutral. Mei and Li Chen fell into step beside him as they exited the Azure Pavilion, not speaking until they'd rounded three corners and entered a cramped tea house that smelled of burned leaves and desperation.

"Well?" Mei demanded, slamming her cup down.

Ren unrolled the scroll. "Read it yourself."

She scanned the contract, her eyes widening with each line. "Ten thousand spirit stones? Upfront? With only fifteen percent interest annually?" Her voice cracked. "Ren, this is—"

"A trap," Li Chen finished, not looking up from his tea. "I've seen these before. Consortium Master Huo doesn't give favorable terms. Ever. There's something he's not telling us."

"There's always something," Ren said, but his hands trembled as he rolled the scroll back up. "The question is whether we can exploit it before he exploits us."

They returned to the temple in silence. The crumbling pagoda seemed smaller now, more pathetic. Ren stood in the courtyard, staring at the broken bell that had once called disciples to meditation, and tried to convince himself he'd made the right choice.

The spirit stones arrived the next morning.

A procession of twelve guards in Azure Pond livery marched up the mountain path, each carrying a spirit stone chest on their shoulders. The chests were carved from jade, inlaid with gold, and sealed with formation locks that pulsed with protective energy. They set them in a neat row in the main hall and left without a word.

Mei cracked one open. The light that spilled out was almost blinding — pure spirit stones, each one the size of a fist, arranged in perfect crystalline rows. She picked one up, held it to the light, and whistled. "High grade. This is worth more than my family's entire business."

"My family would kill for this," Li Chen added, his voice hollow. "Actually, they *have* killed for this."

Ren touched one of the stones. The spiritual energy hummed against his skin, warm and alive. For a moment, he felt invincible.

Then the temple's front door exploded inward.

A figure stood in the doorway, backlit by the morning sun. Tall. Robes of crimson and gold. A sword at their hip that radiated killing intent like heat from a forge.

"Ren Wei," the figure said, stepping forward. The light revealed a woman in her forties, her face sharp as a blade, her eyes cold as winter steel. "I am Elder Feng of the Celestial Sword Sect. You have thirty seconds to explain why you're still breathing."

Ren's blood turned to ice.

"Elder Feng," he said, bowing deeply. "I didn't realize the Celestial Sword Sect monitored the activities of former disciples. I assure you, I'm not—"

"Not what? Not building a sect? Not accepting funding from criminal enterprises?" She gestured at the spirit stone chests. "Azure Pond Consortium is under investigation by the Heavenly Justice Bureau. Embezzlement. Money laundering. Possibly cultivation theft." Her smile was razor-thin. "And here you are, their newest partner."

Mei stepped forward, her voice sharp. "We didn't know. The contract was signed this morning—"

"Ignorance is not innocence in the eyes of the Heavenly Justice Bureau." Elder Feng's hand rested on her sword hilt. "You have two options. Surrender yourselves for questioning, and maybe — *maybe* — you'll survive the investigation. Or resist, and I'll report your sect as a criminal enterprise. The Bureau tends to burn those to the ground."

Ren's mind raced. *A trap. Huo knew. He sabotaged us from the start. But why?*

"Option three," he said slowly.

Elder Feng raised an eyebrow. "There is no option three."

"There's always an option three." Ren reached into his robe and pulled out a small jade slip — a recording talisman, one of the few things he'd stolen from the Celestial Sword Sect during his departure. "I have evidence. Conversations. Financial records. Everything that happened during my five years with your sect. Including the cover-up of the Seventh Peak disaster."

Elder Feng's face went pale.

"Blackmail," she breathed. "You're blackmailing your former sect."

"I'm negotiating." Ren held the talisman up. "You leave. You forget you ever saw us. And I forget I ever saw this." He patted his robe. "Deal?"

For a long moment, Elder Feng didn't move. Her hand trembled on her sword hilt. Her eyes burned with fury and something else — fear.

Then she laughed.

"You think that will work?" She stepped closer, her voice dropping to a whisper. "You think a recording talisman can protect you from the Celestial Sword Sect? From the Heavenly Justice Bureau?" She shook her head. "You're not a threat, Ren Wei. You're a corpse that hasn't learned to stop walking yet."

She turned and walked out, her robes billowing behind her.

The door hung crooked on its hinges, letting in the cold mountain wind.

Mei exhaled shakily. "That was—"

"Stupid," Li Chen finished. "That was stupid. She's going to report us anyway. She's going to bring the entire Bureau down on our heads."

"No," Ren said, but his voice wavered. "She won't. The Seventh Peak disaster would destroy the Celestial Sword Sect's reputation. She's bluffing."

"Are you willing to bet our lives on that?"

Ren looked at the spirit stone chests. At the broken door. At his two disciples, who had followed him into this madness without question.

"I'm willing to bet *my* life," he said. "But not yours. You can leave. Both of you. Go back to your families. Pretend this never happened."

Mei punched him in the arm.

"Idiot," she said, but her eyes were wet. "I'm not leaving. This is the most fun I've had since I was a child."

Li Chen said nothing. He simply drew his sword and began sharpening it, the rasp of metal against stone filling the silence.

Ren nodded, a lump forming in his throat. "Alright. Then we have work to do."

---

They spent the next three days fortifying.

Mei used her business connections to acquire defensive formations — cheap ones, but better than nothing. Li Chen trained until his hands bled, his sword forms becoming sharper, faster, deadlier. Ren pored over the Azure Pond contract, searching for loopholes, for exits, for anything that could save them.

On the third night, he found it.

Buried in a subsection titled "Force Majeure and Spiritual Catastrophes," there was a clause that read: *If either party determines, in good faith, that continued cooperation would result in irreparable harm to the cultivation ecosystem, the contract may be terminated without penalty, provided both parties agree in writing.*

"Good faith," Ren muttered. "Irreparable harm to the cultivation ecosystem."

He looked up at his disciples. "I have an idea. A terrible one. But it might work."

Mei raised an eyebrow. "Is it worse than blackmailing a sect elder?"

"Much worse."

"Excellent. I'm in."

Li Chen sighed. "I'll get the rope."

---

The plan was simple.

Ren would infiltrate the Azure Pavilion, find Consortium Master Huo, and force him to sign a termination agreement. How? By proving that the consortium's activities were causing "irreparable harm to the cultivation ecosystem."

How? By exposing their money laundering operation.

How? By breaking into their secure vault and stealing their ledgers.

"You're going to rob the richest consortium in the province," Mei said flatly. "With no combat cultivation, no stealth formations, and a drunk sword master as backup."

"It's a suicide mission," Li Chen agreed.

"Exactly." Ren smiled, and it was the smile of a man who had nothing left to lose. "That's why it's going to work."

They set out at midnight, creeping through the streets of the Jade District, avoiding patrols and automatic security formations. The Azure Pavilion loomed ahead, its jade walls glowing faintly in the moonlight.

Li Chen produced a small vial of liquid — formation solvent, the result of three days of brewing and luck. "This will disable the outer defenses for exactly thirty breaths. After that, we're dead."

"Thirty breaths," Ren repeated. "Plenty of time."

They moved.

The solvent hissed against the jade wall, eating through the formation lines with a soft sizzle. A gap opened, just wide enough for a man to squeeze through. Li Chen went first, his sword drawn, his footsteps silent. Ren followed, his heart pounding so loud he was sure it would wake the entire district.

The vault was in the basement, guarded by two Qi Condensation cultivators in matching armor. Li Chen dealt with them in three breaths — a flash of steel, two thuds, and silence.

The vault door was a masterpiece of formation engineering. Lock after lock, seal after seal, each one designed to keep out thieves and spies.

Ren pulled out his trump card.

A jade slip, identical to the one he'd shown Elder Feng. But this one didn't contain evidence.

It contained a recording of Consortium Master Huo's voice, extracted from their earlier negotiation.

"Open vault," the recording said, perfectly mimicked. "Authorization: Huo Wei."

The formation locks pulsed once, twice, three times.

Then they clicked open.

Mei had taught him that. "The most secure vaults have voice locks for emergency access. But they only respond to the consortium master's voice. If you can mimic it perfectly..."

Ren stepped into the vault.

The ledgers were there. Hundreds of them, stacked in neat rows, each one detailing a transaction, a bribe, a secret.

He grabbed the most recent one and ran.

The formation on the wall flared back to life as he squeezed through the gap, the solvent's effect fading just in time. He stumbled into the street, gasping, clutching the ledger to his chest.

"We got it," he whispered. "We actually got it."

Li Chen emerged behind him, his sword sheathed. "Guards are waking. We have five minutes before they sound the alarm."

They ran.

Back through the streets. Back through the market. Back up the mountain path, their lungs burning, their legs screaming.

They reached the temple just as the first alarm bells rang in the distance.

Ren collapsed in the courtyard, staring up at the stars, the ledger pressed against his heart.

"We did it," he breathed.

Then a shadow fell over him.

Consortium Master Huo stood at the temple gate, his face unreadable, his robes immaculate.

"I was wondering when you'd figure it out," he said. "You're smarter than I gave you credit for, Ren Wei."

Ren's blood ran cold.

"But not smart enough."

Huo raised his hand, and the night sky filled with flying swords.

title: The Due Diligence From Hell
content:
The meeting with Consortium Master Huo lasted three hours. Ren emerged with a parchment contract, a splitting headache, and the distinct feeling he'd just signed a deal with a demon who happened to wear scholar's robes.

"We need to talk," he said to Mei and Li Chen.

They found a tea house three streets from the Azure Pavilion. It was cheap, which meant the tea tasted like boiled weeds, but it had private rooms and the walls weren't enchanted for eavesdropping. Ren spread the contract across the table.

"He's investing," Ren said. "Three thousand low-grade spirit stones upfront. Another seven thousand upon completion of our first major project."

Li Chen's eyes went wide. "Ten thousand? That's enough to—"

"Read the terms."

Mei snatched the parchment. Her eyes moved rapidly, scanning line after line, her expression darkening with each passing second. By the time she reached the bottom, her face had gone pale.

"This is a death trap."

"It's a standard venture capital term sheet," Ren said. "With some... modifications."

"'Standard' doesn't include a clause that gives the investor the right to replace the sect master if he determines 'insufficient progress' has been made," Mei snapped. "What constitutes insufficient progress?"

"Unclear."

"'The investor retains the right to audit all cultivation techniques developed by the sect.' All of them. Forever."

"I noticed."

"'Any intellectual property created using investor funds becomes joint property of the investor and the sect, with the investor holding primary licensing rights.' Primary. That means he can sell our techniques to anyone he wants. We'd have no control."

Li Chen grabbed the contract, reading over Mei's shoulder. His finger traced条款 after条款. "This one says he gets a seat on the sect council. With veto power over all major decisions."

"Major decisions being defined as 'any decision involving expenditures over one hundred spirit stones, any new disciple admissions, any alliance agreements, and any changes to the sect's cultivation methods.'"

The drunk sword master set down his tea cup — which he'd somehow refilled with wine. "So basically, he owns us."

"Basically."

Mei slammed her hand on the table. "Then why did you sign it?!"

"Because the alternative was no funding. No temple repairs. No formation arrays. No future." Ren met her eyes. "And because I added a few clauses of my own."

He pulled out a second parchment from his robes. This one was handwritten, the ink still fresh.

"What's this?" Mei asked.

"An amendment. Hidden in the section about dispute resolution. It says that any disagreements about 'sufficient progress' will be settled by a third-party arbitrator selected by the sect. Not the investor."

"That's..."

"Unusual. But Huo was so focused on the control clauses that he skimmed the boilerplate. I made the font small. And I buried it between seventeen paragraphs about force majeure and governing law."

Li Chen stared at him. "You tricked a consortium master?"

"I didn't trick him. I just made sure the contract had a back door. Every good cultivator knows you never enter a formation without knowing the exit."

Mei's anger slowly transformed into grudging respect. "Fine. That covers the control issue. But what about the audit clause? The licensing rights?"

"We negotiate those later. Right now, we need the money. And we need to move fast."

"Move fast for what?"

Ren stood. "Huo wants to see our 'operations' tomorrow. He's sending a due diligence team. They'll inspect the temple, interview the disciples, review our cultivation methods. If they don't like what they see, the deal's off."

"So we need to make the temple look like a real sect."

"In twenty-four hours."

Li Chen drained his wine. "I know a guy who knows a guy who can forge ancient-looking scrolls. For a price."

"I know a formation master who owes me favors," Mei added. "She can set up some basic arrays that look impressive but cost almost nothing to maintain. Display arrays. Light shows. Nothing functional."

Ren nodded. "Good. I'll handle the disciples. We need to make them look like proper cultivators, not a drunk, a gambler, and a chef."

"Hey—"

"Present company included."

They split up, each taking a different direction through the city's twisting streets. Ren headed back toward the temple, his mind racing through everything that needed to be done.

The temple looked worse in daylight. Cracks in the foundation. Holes in the roof. Weeds growing through the courtyard stones. A statue of some forgotten deity that had lost its head centuries ago.

Ren stood in the courtyard, hands on his hips, and tried to see it through Huo's eyes.

It looked like a ruin. Because it was a ruin.

He had one day to make it look like a sect.

"Master!"

Ren turned. The chef — his name was Wang, a rotund man with flour permanently embedded in his apron — came running from the kitchen. "Master, the formation expert just used the last of the spirit stones on a gambling debt. He says he'll pay it back, but—"

"He what?"

"He said it was an 'investment opportunity.' His exact words."

Ren pinched the bridge of his nose. "Where is he now?"

"The East Market. The underground fighting pits."

Of course.

"Keep cooking," Ren said. "Make it smell good. We're going to have visitors tomorrow, and I need the place to feel... prosperous."

Wang nodded, though he clearly had no idea what "prosperous" meant in this context.

Ren found the formation expert at the fighting pits, exactly where Wang had said. The man — Old Zhang, a wiry cultivator with more debts than teeth — was watching two Qi Condensation fighters beat each other senseless while holding a betting slip.

"Zhang."

"Master! Perfect timing. I have a guaranteed winner—"

"You spent our last spirit stones on gambling."

"Investment. I told you. Investment."

Ren grabbed him by the collar. "Tomorrow, a consortium master is sending a due diligence team. They're going to inspect our sect. If they find out we're broke, the deal falls apart, and we all go back to eating wild grass and sleeping in the rain."

Old Zhang's face went pale. "What do you need?"

"Formations. Lots of them. Showy ones that don't cost much to maintain. Can you do it?"

"I can... but I need materials. Array flags, spirit ink, alignment tools—"

"How much?"

"At least two hundred spirit stones."

Ren closed his eyes. They had exactly zero spirit stones. The three thousand from Huo wouldn't arrive until the contract was fully executed, which required the due diligence team's approval.

"Find a way," Ren said. "Borrow. Steal. I don't care. Just get it done."

Old Zhang nodded, and Ren released him.

By evening, the temple had transformed — superficially. Old Zhang had somehow acquired enough materials to set up three display formations: one that made the walls glow with ethereal light, one that circulated spiritual energy in visible currents, and one that projected the image of a majestic mountain peak above the temple roof.

"The third one is a bit much," Ren admitted.

"The consortium master likes grandeur. Trust me."

Mei returned with a stack of forged scrolls. "Ancient cultivation techniques, supposedly from the Lost Dynasty period. They're actually just common breathing exercises with fancy names, but the calligraphy is convincing."

Li Chen stumbled in an hour later, smelling of wine and carrying a jade token. "Information. The due diligence team leader is a woman named Lin. She's known for being thorough. She once spent three weeks auditing a minor sect before approving a loan. Three. Weeks."

"We don't have three weeks. We have one day."

"Then we need to make sure she doesn't find anything worth auditing."

The morning came too fast.

Ren stood at the temple gates, wearing his best robes — freshly washed, though the patches were still visible if you looked closely. Behind him, the disciples stood in a line. Mei had drilled them on proper etiquette until they could recite the greetings in their sleep.

Wang had prepared a feast. Old Zhang had the formations running. Li Chen had hidden all evidence of his gambling addiction.

The due diligence team arrived at exactly the hour of the serpent.

There were five of them. Lin was in front — a middle-aged woman with sharp eyes and sharper cheekbones, wearing robes that cost more than Ren's entire sect. Behind her were two assistants carrying jade tablets for note-taking, a formation expert with a detection artifact, and a guard who looked like he could break bones with a glare.

"Sect Master Ren," Lin said, her voice neutral. "Thank you for hosting us."

"Thank you for coming. Please, allow me to show you our facilities."

The tour took two hours. Lin asked questions about everything. The foundation cracks ("Structural reinforcement is scheduled for next month"). The missing roof tiles ("A design choice — it allows better spiritual energy absorption during rainfall"). The headless statue ("A meditation aid. Empty vessels reflect the emptiness of the mind").

She didn't seem convinced.

The formation expert with the detection artifact kept frowning at Old Zhang's arrays. "These are... recent."

"We maintain them regularly," Ren said smoothly. "Formations degrade without proper care."

"They're still warm from activation."

"We activated them this morning. For your visit. To show respect."

Lin's eyes narrowed.

The feast was a disaster. Wang had prepared his best dishes, but Lin barely touched them. She spent the meal asking pointed questions about the sect's finances, cultivation methods, and disciple retention rates.

"How many disciples do you have?"

"Currently, four. But we're selective about admissions. Quality over quantity."

"Quality. Yes." She glanced at Old Zhang, who was nervously sweating. "Your formation expert seems... unwell."

"He's been working hard. Preparing for your visit."

By evening, Ren was exhausted. The team had found nothing incriminating, but they also hadn't found anything impressive. The temple was still a ruin with fancy lights.

Lin called him aside as the sun set.

"Sect Master Ren. I've reviewed your records. Your proposal. Your facilities."

"And?"

She handed him a parchment. "Here is my preliminary assessment."

Ren read it. His heart sank.

The assessment was thorough. It listed every flaw. The structural damage. The recent formations. The suspicious scrolls. The lack of qualified disciples.

It ended with a recommendation: "Further investigation required. Funding withheld pending complete audit."

"I'll be staying in the city for the next month," Lin said. "I look forward to working with you."

She walked away, leaving Ren standing alone in the courtyard, the parchment trembling in his hands.

The deal was dead.

Unless—

"You know," said a voice from behind him, "I could make her disappear. No questions asked."

Ren turned. Li Chen stood there, his hand resting on his sword.

"No," Ren said.

"Just saying. It's an option."

"It's not an option."

"Fine." Li Chen shrugged. "But we have another problem."

"What now?"

"The drunk sword master just challenged Lin's guard to a duel. Something about 'disrespectful posture.' He's currently in the middle of the street, sword drawn, shouting insults."

Ren closed his eyes.

Of course.

"I'll handle it," he said, already running.

But as he reached the street, he saw something that made him stop.

The guard was on the ground. Unconscious. The drunk sword master stood over him, swaying slightly, his sword pointed at Lin's throat.

"I said," the drunk repeated, "you need to show more respect."

Lin's face was pale. Her hands were raised.

And a crowd was gathering.

This was going to be a long month.

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: The Contract That Ate Everything

Ren sat across from Consortium Master Huo, the silence between them stretching like a blade.

Huo didn't speak. He simply watched, his gentle eyes missing nothing. The obsidian desk reflected the room's soft light, and Ren caught his own reflection—haggard, hopeful, terrified. He looked like a man who had bet everything on a single throw of the dice.

Because he had.

"I like your proposal," Huo finally said, and Ren's heart leaped. "It's innovative. Bold. Exactly the kind of thing Azure Pond looks for."

He pulled a scroll from his sleeve, unrolling it across the desk. The paper was pristine white, edged with gold. The characters were written in ink that shimmered faintly—spirit ink, the kind that bound cultivators to their word with more than just honor.

"I've taken the liberty of drafting a preliminary agreement," Huo continued. "Standard terms. You'll find them more than generous."

Ren picked up the scroll. His hands were steady. His stomach was not.

The first few clauses were exactly what he expected. Investment amount: five thousand spirit stones upfront, with another five thousand upon achieving specified milestones. Equity: twenty percent of New Dao Sect, non-dilutable. Board seat: one representative from Azure Pond to advise on strategic decisions.

Reasonable. Painful, but reasonable.

Then he kept reading.

Clause 7.3: "All intellectual property developed by New Dao Sect during the term of this agreement shall be jointly owned by both parties."

Ren's jaw tightened. Joint ownership of everything? Every technique, every formation, every recipe Mei's chef-disciples created? That was worse than standard. That was predatory.

He kept reading.

Clause 12.1: "In the event of default, Azure Pond retains first right of refusal on all sect assets, including but not limited to: real property, spiritual treasures, cultivation techniques, and disciple contracts."

Disciple contracts. They could buy his disciples.

Clause 18.4: "Azure Pond reserves the right to audit New Dao Sect's operations at any time, without prior notice, for the duration of the agreement."

They could walk into his temple whenever they wanted. Read his mail. Count his spirit stones. Interrogate his disciples.

Clause 22.7: "This agreement shall remain in effect for a period of ninety-nine years, renewable at Azure Pond's sole discretion."

Ninety-nine years. Before Ren was born, the current Celestial Emperor had been a Qi Condensation nobody. Ninety-nine years was an eternity in the cultivation world. It was *forever*.

He set the scroll down carefully, as if it might bite him.

"Consortium Master Huo," he said, his voice calm, "this agreement would make New Dao Sect a subsidiary of Azure Pond, not a partner."

Huo's smile didn't waver. "That's a harsh way to put it. I prefer 'strategic alignment.'"

"We'd be your vassals."

"You'd be our *investment*. There's a difference." Huo leaned back, steepling his fingers. "Ren Wei, let me be frank. You're a former outer disciple from a middling sect. Your 'sect' is a crumbling temple on a hill no one wants. Your disciples are a drunk, a gambler, and a cook. By any reasonable measure, you should have failed already."

Ren said nothing.

"The fact that you haven't—that you've actually attracted attention, built a following, started generating revenue—that's remarkable. It shows potential. But potential is not achievement." Huo's eyes hardened. "Azure Pond is offering you resources that would take you decades to acquire on your own. In exchange, we want security. Protection against your failure. And if you succeed, we want to share in that success."

"These terms would let you destroy us whenever you wanted."

"Why would we want to destroy our own investment?"

"Because if we become too successful, we'd threaten your other portfolio companies. Or your own operations. And clause 22.7 means you can keep us locked in for a century while you extract everything we build."

Huo's smile flickered. For just a moment, Ren saw something cold behind those gentle eyes.

"You're more perceptive than your file suggested."

"I had good teachers. Mostly people who tried to cheat me."

"Then you understand the position you're in." Huo gestured at the scroll. "This is the best offer you'll get. I've checked. Every other consortium in the city has already dismissed you. Azure Pond is your only lifeline."

Ren's mind raced. He had expected something like this. Li Chen's intelligence had warned him that Azure Pond was aggressive, but this was beyond aggressive. This was a takeover disguised as an investment.

He had two options.

Accept the terms and become a puppet. Build New Dao Sect under Azure Pond's thumb, always watching over his shoulder, always knowing that one wrong move would mean losing everything.

Or refuse. Walk away. Try to grow without investment, knowing that the major sects were already circling, that his disciples were getting stronger and hungrier, that the mortal world's attention was a double-edged sword.

He thought about Mei. About her dreams of building something real, something that would outlast them both. About the way her eyes lit up when she talked about expansion plans and market penetration.

He thought about Li Chen. About the intelligence network he was building, one drink and one bribe at a time. About the look on his face when he'd said, "This is the first time anyone's trusted me with something important."

He thought about the drunk sword master. About the way he'd started showing up to morning practice sober. About the grudging respect in his voice when he'd said, "You're not completely useless, sect leader."

He thought about the mortal disciples. The ones who had nowhere else to go. The ones who believed in him.

"Consortium Master Huo," Ren said, "I appreciate your offer. But I can't accept these terms."

Huo's smile vanished. "I see."

"They're not fair. They're not partnership. They're conquest by contract."

"You're making a mistake."

"Maybe. But it's my mistake to make." Ren stood. "Thank you for your time."

He was halfway to the door when Huo spoke again.

"Ren Wei."

He stopped. Didn't turn around.

"I admire your principles. Truly. But principles don't pay spirit stones. And they don't protect you from what's coming."

Ren turned. "What's coming?"

Huo's eyes were flat, emotionless. "The Celestial Sword Sect has filed a formal complaint with the Cultivation Alliance. They're accusing New Dao Sect of 'unauthorized cultivation techniques' and 'recruiting disciples through deceptive practices.'"

Ren's blood ran cold.

"The hearing is in three weeks. If you lose, your sect will be dissolved. Your techniques confiscated. Your disciples reassigned to approved sects."

Huo smiled, and this time there was nothing gentle about it.

"By all means, walk away from my offer. But if you do, you'll face the Cultivation Alliance alone. And I assure you, they are far less generous than I am."

Ren stood in the doorway, the weight of Huo's words crushing down on him. The Celestial Sword Sect. His old masters. The people who had thrown him out for being "insufficiently talented."

They weren't just content to let him fail. They wanted to *erase* him.

"I'll think about it," he said.

"Three weeks, Ren Wei. The offer expires when the hearing begins."

Ren walked out of the study, through the opulent lobby, past Mei and the drunk sword master. He didn't say a word until they were three blocks away, standing in an alley that smelled of fish and rot.

"Well?" Mei demanded. "How bad?"

Ren told her.

By the time he finished, Mei's face had gone pale. The drunk sword master was gripping his flask so hard his knuckles were white.

"Three weeks," Mei whispered. "We can't build a legal defense in three weeks. We can't even find a lawyer in three weeks."

"We have one option," Ren said. "We go to Huo. We accept his terms."

"No."

They both turned. The drunk sword master had spoken.

"It's a trap," he said. "I've seen contracts like that before. They don't just own your sect. They own your soul. Once you sign, you're never getting out."

"Then what do we do?" Mei's voice cracked. "We can't fight the Celestial Sword Sect alone. We can't fight the Cultivation Alliance alone. We're a startup with three disciples and a leaking roof!"

Ren closed his eyes.

He thought about the Celestial Sword Sect. About the thousands of years of tradition they represented. About the power they wielded.

He thought about Huo. About the consortium's endless resources, their connections, their ruthlessness.

He thought about his disciples. About the mortals who had believed in him. About the dream he had sold them.

And then he thought about something else.

Three weeks.

It wasn't much time.

But it was enough.

"Mei," he said, opening his eyes, "how much liquid capital do we have?"

She blinked. "What?"

"Spirit stones. Cash. Things we can spend immediately."

"Maybe... three hundred? Why?"

"Li Chen," Ren said, "how fast can you get me a meeting with a Celestial Sword Sect elder?"

The drunk sword master stared at him. "You want to negotiate with the people trying to destroy you?"

"No." Ren smiled, and it was not a nice smile. "I want to find out what they're afraid of. Every sect has secrets. Every elder has weaknesses. The Celestial Sword Sect isn't attacking us because we're a threat. They're attacking us because they're *scared*."

"Scared of what?"

"Of someone who doesn't play by their rules. Of a sect that proves you don't need ten thousand years of tradition to succeed. Of a future where they're not in control."

He started walking.

"Three weeks. That's how long we have to turn our weaknesses into weapons. To find out what the Celestial Sword Sect is hiding. To build a case so strong that the Cultivation Alliance can't ignore it."

Mei and the drunk sword master followed.

"And if that fails?"

Ren kept walking.

"Then we find a way to make failure look like a brilliant strategic retreat."

Behind them, in the window of a tea shop, a figure in blue robes watched them go. He raised a jade communication talisman to his lips.

"The sect leader has refused the offer. He's heading back to his temple."

A pause.

"Understood. I'll prepare the secondary protocol."

The figure vanished into the crowd, leaving no trace.

None at all.

title: The Rival Who Came Too Late
content:
Ren's hands remained steady on the obsidian desk, even as Consortium Master Huo's smile sharpened by a fraction of a degree.

"Bold words from a sect that currently operates out of a crumbling temple with three disciples and a debt to every spirit stone lender in the city," Huo said, leaning back in his chair.

"We prefer 'lean operations,'" Ren replied. "Keeps our burn rate low."

Huo laughed — a genuine sound, not the hollow courtesy of the powerful. "I like you, Ren Wei. You have the fire of a man who has nothing left to lose. Those are always the best investments. Or the most dangerous. Sometimes both."

He pulled a scroll from his desk, unrolled it with practiced ease. Ren recognized his own handwriting — the business plan he'd spent three sleepless nights perfecting, written in ink mixed with his own blood as a gesture of sincerity.

"I've reviewed your projections," Huo said. "You're asking for five thousand spirit stones. In exchange, you offer thirty percent equity in New Dao Sect, a seat on your advisory board, and first refusal on all future funding rounds."

"That's correct."

"The equity is too high. I want forty percent."

Ren's jaw tightened. "Thirty-five, with a vesting schedule. You don't get full ownership until certain milestones are met."

"Thirty-eight, and I want the right to appoint a financial officer to your sect."

"We don't have a financial officer position."

"Then create one."

The negotiation continued for another hour, the two of them dancing around numbers and terms like swordsmen circling for an opening. Ren gave ground where he had to, held firm where he couldn't afford to yield. By the end, they'd reached an agreement: thirty-five percent equity, a board seat, and the right to review major expenditures above five hundred spirit stones.

"Excellent," Huo said, sealing the contract with a stamp of his personal seal. "I'll have the funds transferred to your sect's account by sunset. Welcome to the Azure Pond portfolio, Ren Wei."

Ren shook his hand, the weight of the contract burning in his storage ring. Five thousand spirit stones. Enough to repair the temple, buy proper cultivation resources, and maybe — just maybe — start recruiting actual disciples.

He walked out of the study feeling like he was floating.

Mei and Li Chen were waiting in the reception area, their expressions carefully neutral. The guards didn't look at them. The receptionist didn't smile. Everything was exactly as it should be.

"How bad?" Mei whispered as they exited the pavilion.

"We got the funding," Ren said, unable to keep the grin off his face. "Thirty-five percent equity, but we're alive. We're actually alive."

Li Chen clapped him on the shoulder. "Drinks are on you tonight."

"Everything is on me tonight. We're a funded startup, gentlemen. The New Dao Sect is officially in business."

They celebrated at a tavern in the merchant district, a place that served decent wine and better food. Li Chen drank like a man who'd been stranded in a desert. Mei pulled out a piece of paper and started sketching expansion plans between sips of rice wine. Ren watched them both, feeling something he hadn't felt in years: hope.

The next morning, he woke to a headache and a jade slip glowing on his nightstand.

He picked it up, squinting at the message. It was from Huo's office, confirming the transfer of funds. Two thousand spirit stones. Half of what they'd agreed on.

Ren read it again. Then a third time.

"Mei!" he shouted, stumbling out of his room.

She was already awake, her face pale as she held her own jade slip. "I got the same message. Two thousand. With a note saying the rest will be delivered 'upon satisfactory performance of initial milestones.'"

"What milestones? We didn't agree to any milestones!"

"Apparently we did." She tossed him a scroll. "Look at the fine print."

Ren unrolled it, his blood running cold. There, buried in the third appendix of the contract, was a clause he'd missed. A performance review after thirty days. If Azure Pond deemed their progress insufficient, they could withhold the remaining funds.

"He moved the goalposts," Ren said, his voice flat. "He did it right in front of me, and I didn't see it."

"Because you were too busy celebrating," Mei said, not unkindly. "We both were. That's how they get you. The first check is always the hook. The rest is the line."

Ren sank onto a bench, his head in his hands. Two thousand spirit stones was still a fortune. It could repair the temple's roof and buy enough pills to get Li Chen to the next cultivation stage. But it wasn't enough to scale. It wasn't enough to recruit. It wasn't enough to do anything except survive.

And surviving wasn't the same as growing.

"We need to make this work," he said finally. "Two thousand stones. Thirty days. What can we do?"

Mei was already calculating. "The temple needs at least five hundred for structural repairs. Another three hundred for formation materials — I can set up basic defensive arrays and a spirit-gathering formation. That leaves twelve hundred for operations."

"Recruitment?"

"Expensive. We'd need to advertise, host a trial event, offer incentives. A proper recruitment drive would cost at least eight hundred stones minimum."

"Do it."

"We can't afford it and the temple repairs."

"Then we delay the repairs. The roof can leak for another month. What matters is getting disciples."

Li Chen stumbled in, still smelling of last night's wine. "I heard shouting. What'd I miss?"

"We got half the funding and a thirty-day deadline," Mei said.

Li Chen blinked. "So... more drinks?"

"No," Ren said, standing up. "We work. Li, I need you to find out everything you can about Azure Pond. Their other investments. Their reputation. Any skeletons in their closet."

"You think Huo is going to screw us?"

"I think Huo is a businessman. And businessmen protect their investments. If we fail the review, he takes control of the sect. If we succeed, he owns thirty-five percent. Either way, he wins. I want to find a way to make sure we win too."

Li Chen nodded, already heading for the door. "I know some people in the information trade. Give me three days."

"I'll start on the formations," Mei said. "We need the temple to look like a real sect, not a condemned building."

"Good. I'll handle recruitment prep." Ren pulled out a fresh scroll, his brush already moving. "We're going to make this work. We have to."

---

Three days passed in a blur of work and worry.

Mei managed to stabilize the temple's structural integrity with a combination of formation flags and creative engineering. The roof still leaked, but now it leaked in patterns that looked almost intentional. Li Chen returned with a pouch of information and a haunted look in his eyes.

"Azure Pond isn't just any investment consortium," he said, pouring himself a cup of tea with shaking hands. "They're connected to the Heavenly Trade Alliance. The same alliance that runs the spirit stone exchanges across the continent."

Ren felt his stomach drop. "The Heavenly Trade Alliance? As in, the organization that the Celestial Sword Sect has a partnership with?"

"The very same." Li Chen took a long drink. "Huo isn't just a random investor. He's a scout. He finds promising small sects, invests in them, and when they fail the review — which they always do — he absorbs their assets and talent into the alliance's network."

"How many sects has he done this to?"

"Seven in the last five years. All of them collapsed within six months of their funding review."

Mei's face was white. "We're not just dealing with a shady investor. We're dealing with a predatory acquisition firm disguised as a venture fund."

Ren stared at the contract on the table. The contract he'd signed. The contract that was now binding in the eyes of both mortal law and cultivation covenants.

"There's more," Li Chen said. "The Celestial Sword Sect knows about us. They've been monitoring our progress. And they've sent someone to 'observe' our recruitment drive."

"Who?"

Li Chen pulled out a wanted poster, creased and faded. The face on it was young, barely out of adolescence, with eyes that held a cold fury beyond their years.

"His name is Zhao Yun. Former disciple of the Celestial Sword Sect. Expelled for 'excessive violence' during a training exercise. He's been operating as a freelance enforcer for the past year. Word is, he's looking for a new sect to join. And he's been asking about you specifically."

Ren took the poster, studying the face. There was something familiar about those eyes. Something that made his skin crawl.

"Why me?"

"Because you're the one who got away," Mei said softly. "The Celestial Sword Sect doesn't like loose ends. They want to make sure you fail. And they're sending their sharpest blade to do it."

The door to the temple slammed open.

A young man stood in the doorway, silhouetted against the setting sun. He was tall, lean, and carried a sword that hummed with barely contained spiritual energy. His eyes found Ren immediately, and his lips curled into a smile that held no warmth.

"Ren Wei," Zhao Yun said, his voice carrying the weight of a blade's edge. "I've heard a lot about your little startup sect. Sounds like a place for misfits and failures."

Li Chen's hand moved to his sword. Mei's fingers twitched toward her formation flags. Ren stood perfectly still, meeting the young man's gaze.

"We're not a sect for misfits," Ren said. "We're a sect for people who don't fit into the old systems. There's a difference."

"Is there?" Zhao Yun stepped inside, his presence filling the room like a storm. "Show me. Prove that your way is better than the Celestial Sword Sect's. Or I'll walk out of here and tell everyone in the city that New Dao Sect is just another failed experiment."

The challenge hung in the air, heavy and undeniable.

Ren looked at his disciples — Mei with her cracked formation flags, Li Chen with his hangover and his haunted eyes, and himself with a contract that might as well be a noose.

"Alright," Ren said, surprising himself with the steadiness of his voice. "You want to see what New Dao Sect can do? Then join us. For one month. Train with us. Fight with us. And if you still think we're a failure after thirty days, I'll personally tear up our charter and dissolve the sect."

Mei gasped. Li Chen cursed.

Zhao Yun's smile widened. "Deal."

And just like that, the New Dao Sect had its fourth disciple — a killer sent by their enemies, watching their every move, waiting for them to fall.

title: The Consortium's True Price
content:
The silence stretched between them like a blade.

Consortium Master Huo studied Ren with the patience of a fisherman watching a river. His fingers traced the edge of his obsidian desk, leaving no marks, no evidence of movement at all. Ren had been in enough negotiations to recognize the tactic—whoever spoke first lost.

But Ren had also learned from Mei that sometimes the best move was to flip the board.

"I'll be direct, Consortium Master," Ren said, leaning forward. "You didn't bring me here to discuss my proposal. You brought me here because you've already decided. You just want to see if I'm worth the investment or if you should simply take what you want."

Huo's smile didn't waver, but something shifted in his eyes. Cold calculation, swiftly hidden. "That's quite an accusation."

"Is it wrong?"

Another pause. Then Huo laughed—a genuine sound, warm and surprised. "No. It's not wrong." He reached into his robe and pulled out a jade slip, placing it on the desk between them. "This is a summary of everything I know about you, Ren Wei. Your birth village. Your parents' deaths. Your five years at the Celestial Sword Sect. Your expulsion. The three misfits you've collected. The crumbling temple you call a sect. The mortal woman who handles your finances with the ruthlessness of a bandit queen."

Ren's blood ran cold, but he kept his face neutral. "Impressive research."

"I'm an investor. Research is my foundation." Huo tapped the jade slip. "What this doesn't tell me is why you think you can succeed where a thousand other 'innovators' have failed. The cultivation world has existed for ten thousand years. Hierarchies exist because they work. Sects exist because they concentrate power. What makes you think you can break that system?"

"Because I'm not trying to break it."

Huo raised an eyebrow.

Ren smiled. It was not a friendly smile. "I'm trying to replace it. From the inside. The old sects are dying, Consortium Master. They just don't know it yet. They've grown fat on tradition, bloated on inherited power. They've forgotten that cultivation is about growth, not hoarding. Every talented disciple they reject is a potential competitor. Every mortal they ignore is a potential customer. Every innovation they suppress is a potential revolution."

"And you plan to harvest those revolutions."

"I plan to *fund* them. That's where Azure Pond comes in."

Huo was quiet for a long moment. Then he picked up the jade slip and shattered it in his hand. The pieces fell to the floor like green tears.

"I'm going to tell you something I've never told another sect leader," Huo said softly. "I hate this world. I hate the way it's structured. I hate that a child born in a mortal village has no path to power except through the charity of ancient institutions. I hate that talent is wasted, potential is crushed, and the strong grow stronger by keeping the weak weak."

Ren stared at him.

"I founded Azure Pond with one goal," Huo continued. "To build an alternative. A way for those outside the system to gain resources, connections, power. But I've been careful. Patient. I've spent thirty years building relationships, gathering information, positioning myself. And in all that time, I've never met anyone who had the same vision." He looked at Ren. "Until you."

"Why me?"

"Because you're stupid enough to try."

Ren laughed despite himself. "That's... not the endorsement I was hoping for."

"It's the most valuable one I can give. Smart people see obstacles and stop. Stupid people see obstacles and wonder what's on the other side." Huo stood, walked to a window that overlooked the Jade District. "I'll fund your sect. Fifty thousand spirit stones. Initial investment. You'll have operational autonomy, but Azure Pond gets thirty percent equity and a seat on your 'board.'"

Ren's heart leaped, but he forced himself to stay calm. "Terms?"

"Quarterly reports. Right of first refusal on any major partnerships. And—" Huo turned, his eyes sharp "—I want access to your formation library."

The words hung in the air like poison.

Ren's mind raced. His formation library. Which currently consisted of exactly one formation—the one Master Chen had spent two months perfecting, the one that was their only competitive advantage. The one that could revolutionize spirit stone efficiency for mortal cultivators.

"That's not standard," Ren said carefully.

"Nothing about this arrangement is standard."

"Thirty percent equity is already aggressive. Access to proprietary formations—"

"Is the price of my trust." Huo's voice hardened. "I've invested in seventeen 'innovative' sects over the past decade. Fifteen of them failed within two years. One was destroyed by rivals. One simply vanished. I don't invest in dreams, Ren Wei. I invest in assets. Your formation is your only asset worth protecting."

"And if I refuse?"

Huo shrugged. "Then we're done. You walk out of here with nothing. Your sect collapses within six months. Your disciples scatter. And the cultivation world continues exactly as it has for ten thousand years."

Ren wanted to argue. Wanted to point out that the formation was Master Chen's work, that he had no right to give it away. Wanted to tell Huo that this wasn't a partnership, it was a takeover.

But he also knew that fifty thousand spirit stones could change everything. Could buy equipment. Could attract disciples. Could build a foundation that might, just might, survive the inevitable attacks from established sects.

"Twenty percent equity," Ren said. "And access to the formation is limited. You can view it, but you can't copy it or share it without my explicit permission."

Huo's smile returned. "Negotiation. Good. Twenty-five percent, and I can view the formation for one hour per month."

"Twenty-two percent, and you can view it for thirty minutes."

"Twenty-three, final offer."

Ren extended his hand. "Deal."

They shook. Huo's grip was firm, his palm warm. A cultivator's hand, but not a warrior's. A man who had never needed to fight because he had never needed to lose.

"I'll have the contract drawn up by tomorrow morning," Huo said. "We'll sign at noon. Bring your disciples. I want to meet the people I'm investing in."

Ren nodded, his mind already racing with plans. Fifty thousand spirit stones. He could buy the materials for three more formations. Could hire a proper guard for the temple. Could—

"Oh, and Ren?"

He paused at the door.

"One more thing." Huo's voice was casual, almost friendly. "I'm sure you've already considered this, but I want to make sure we're clear. If anything happens to my investment—if your sect fails, if you're destroyed by rivals, if you try to cut me out—I won't just lose money. I'll lose face. And in the cultivation world, losing face is worse than losing life."

Ren turned slowly. "What are you saying?"

"I'm saying that if this fails, I'll make sure you never cultivate again. I'll destroy your temple personally. I'll scatter your disciples to the four winds. I'll erase the name 'New Dao Sect' from every record, every memory, every stone." Huo's smile never wavered. "But that won't happen, will it? Because you're going to succeed. You're going to be the most successful sect leader in a thousand years. And I'm going to be right there beside you, collecting my dividends."

Ren held his gaze for a long moment. Then he nodded once and walked out.

The hallway felt narrower than before. The opulence of the Azure Pavilion felt like a cage, not a sanctuary. Every guard he passed seemed to watch him with new eyes—predatory eyes, calculating eyes.

Mei was waiting where he'd left her, her face carefully neutral. She saw his expression and her eyes widened slightly.

"Tell me you didn't sell our souls."

"Only seventy-seven percent of them."

She groaned. "That's worse than I expected."

"It's better than the alternative." He grabbed her arm and pulled her toward the exit. "We need to get back to the temple. We have a lot of work to do."

"What kind of work?"

"Preparing for the inevitable betrayal."

Mei stopped walking. "Ren. What did you agree to?"

He didn't answer. Couldn't answer. Because the truth was too complicated, too dangerous, too full of variables he couldn't control.

He had sold a piece of his dream to a man who collected dreams like trophies.

And now he had to figure out how to keep the rest of it alive.

They emerged from the Azure Pavilion into the afternoon sun. The Jade District sparkled around them, beautiful and treacherous. Somewhere in the distance, a bell tolled three times—the signal for a spirit beast auction at the central plaza.

"Ren." Mei's voice was quiet. "What aren't you telling me?"

He looked at her. At this woman who had abandoned her mortal business to follow a madman with a crumbling temple and three broken disciples. At the only person who believed in his vision enough to risk everything.

"I gave him access to Master Chen's formation."

Mei's face went pale. "You what?"

"It was the only way."

"There's always another way!"

"Not with fifty thousand spirit stones on the line!" Ren's voice cracked. "Mei, do you understand what that money means? We can buy equipment. We can hire help. We can actually *build* something that lasts. And yes, it comes with strings. Everything does. But if we don't take this chance, we have nothing. No money. No future. No hope."

Mei stared at him, her jaw tight. Then she did something unexpected.

She laughed.

"Gods above, you're an idiot," she said, shaking her head. "But you're my idiot. And if we're going to burn, we might as well burn bright."

She grabbed his hand and pulled him forward, into the crowd, into the chaos of the Jade District.

"Come on. We have money now. Let's spend it before Huo changes his mind."

Ren let himself be dragged, a smile tugging at his lips despite everything.

The New Dao Sect had its first investor.

Now they just had to survive the consequences.

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: The Offer You Can't Refuse

Consortium Master Huo smiled. It was the kind of smile that made Ren want to check his pockets.

"You're direct," Huo said, leaning back in his chair. "I appreciate that. Most entrepreneurs who come through that door spend the first hour explaining how their sect will change the cultivation world. They talk about destiny, about fate, about the Mandate of Heaven. You talked about market positioning and customer acquisition costs."

"Is that a problem?"

"It's refreshing." Huo opened a drawer and pulled out a jade slip, placing it on the desk between them. "I've done my own research on your New Dao Sect. Three disciples. A crumbling temple in the Outer Wilds. A debt of seventeen spirit stones to a local herb merchant. Monthly revenue of approximately twelve spirit stones, mostly from selling talismans and healing services to mortal villages."

Ren kept his face neutral. The fact that Huo knew all this meant he'd done more than read the proposal. He'd sent people. He'd investigated.

"You're thorough."

"I'm thorough because I'm interested." Huo tapped the jade slip. "Your business model is unsustainable. Your revenue is too low to cover your costs, your disciples are underqualified, and your temple is falling apart. By traditional metrics, you're already dead."

"By traditional metrics, every startup is already dead until it isn't."

Huo's smile widened. "Exactly. That's why I'm interested. You've built something with almost nothing. That's the kind of founder I invest in. Not the ones who inherit spirit stones from their ancestors, but the ones who create value from air."

He pushed the jade slip across the desk.

"This is my offer. Fifty thousand spirit stones. A dedicated formation master to upgrade your temple's defenses. Access to Azure Pond's merchant network across three provinces. And a seat on my advisory board."

Ren didn't touch the slip. "And what do you want in return?"

"Seventy percent equity in New Dao Sect."

The number hung in the air like a blade.

Ren's heart rate spiked, but his voice stayed calm. "Seventy percent. That's not an investment. That's a takeover."

"It's a valuation," Huo said. "I'm valuing your sect at roughly seventy-one thousand spirit stones. For a startup with your metrics, that's generous."

"And I'd be giving you control."

"You'd be giving me majority ownership. You'd still run day-to-day operations. You'd still make the decisions. But I'd have veto power over major strategic moves."

Ren stared at the jade slip. Seventy percent. He'd expected a tough negotiation, maybe forty or fifty percent. But seventy? That wasn't a partnership. That was indentured servitude with extra steps.

"I need time to think about this."

"Of course." Huo nodded. "Take the night. But I should mention—" he paused, his eyes sharpening— "there's another party interested in this space. A more established sect looking to expand into the mortal market. If you don't accept my offer, I'll have no choice but to invest in them instead."

"And they'd become my competition."

"Direct competition. With my resources behind them." Huo's smile never wavered. "I'd prefer to work with you, Ren. I think you have vision. But business is business. If you can't accept my terms, I'll find someone who can."

Ren picked up the jade slip. It was warm, pulsing with the spiritual energy embedded in the offer. Fifty thousand spirit stones. It was enough to rebuild the temple ten times over. Enough to hire real cultivators. Enough to make New Dao Sect a genuine threat to the old powers.

But at what cost?

"I'll give you my answer tomorrow morning."

"Excellent." Huo stood, extending his hand. "I look forward to working with you, Sect Leader Ren."

Ren shook his hand. The grip was firm, confident, the grip of a man who was used to getting what he wanted.

As Ren walked out of the study, Mei immediately grabbed his arm. "Well?"

"Seventy percent."

Her face went pale. "That's—"

"A robbery. I know."

"What are you going to do?"

Ren looked at the jade slip in his hand. Fifty thousand spirit stones. A dream come true wrapped in a nightmare.

"I don't know yet."

The three of them left the Azure Pavilion in silence, the weight of the offer pressing down on them like a mountain.

---

They found a cheap tea house near the city's edge, the kind of place where the tea was mostly water and the snacks were mostly air. But it was private, and the owner was too old to care about cultivators.

Li Chen drained his cup in one gulp. "So what's the play, boss?"

"I don't know." Ren stared at the tea leaves at the bottom of his cup. "Seventy percent. That's not just equity. That's control. He could overrule any decision I make. He could force us to pivot to whatever he wants. He could—"

"Fire you," Mei said quietly. "With majority ownership, he could remove you as sect leader and install someone else."

"Exactly."

"So we refuse." Li Chen slammed his cup down. "We survived this long without his money. We can survive longer."

"Can we?" Ren looked at him. "Our talisman sales are dropping because the local merchants are getting pressured by the Celestial Sword Sect. Our healing services are limited because I can't afford better medicines. And the temple is literally crumbling. We have six months, maybe less, before we have to shut down."

"There has to be another option," Mei said.

"There is." Ren took a deep breath. "We find another investor. One who wants a smaller stake."

"And where do we find one on short notice?" Li Chen asked. "Huo said there's another party interested. If we go to other investors, they'll hear about it. And if Huo's competition is backed by him..."

"Then we're screwed either way." Mei's voice was bitter. "He's cornered us. We either take his offer or watch him fund our replacement."

The three of them sat in silence, the tea growing cold.

Then a shadow fell over their table.

"Mind if I join you?"

Ren looked up. A woman stood there, her robes a deep crimson, her hair tied back in a practical bun. She carried a sword at her hip, but her posture was relaxed, almost casual.

"Do I know you?"

"Not yet." She pulled out a chair and sat down without waiting for an invitation. "But I know you. Ren Wei, founder of New Dao Sect. You just came from a meeting with Consortium Master Huo."

Ren tensed. "Who are you?"

"My name is Xue Feng. I represent an organization that's been watching your progress with great interest." She smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "And I'm here to make you a counter-offer."

Mei's eyes narrowed. "Another investor?"

"Something like that." Xue Feng reached into her sleeve and produced a black jade slip, sliding it across the table. "Huo is offering you seventy percent for fifty thousand stones. Generous, but ultimately a cage. You'd be trading one master for another."

"And your offer is different?"

Xue Feng's smile sharpened. "We're offering you thirty percent for one hundred thousand spirit stones. No controlling stake. No veto power. Just capital and resources, with complete operational autonomy."

Ren stared at the black jade slip. The terms were impossibly good. Too good.

"What's the catch?"

"There is no catch. We believe in your vision, Sect Leader Ren. We want to see you succeed."

"Bullshit." Li Chen leaned forward. "No one gives away that much money without wanting something in return."

"You're right." Xue Feng's eyes locked onto Ren's. "We do want something. But it's not control of your sect." She paused, her voice dropping to a whisper. "We want you to destroy the Celestial Sword Sect."

The tea house seemed to grow colder.

Ren's mind raced. The Celestial Sword Sect. The sect that had kicked him out. The sect that now saw New Dao Sect as a threat. The sect that was actively working to crush them.

"You want me to—"

"Not directly. Not yet. You're too weak for that." Xue Feng leaned back. "But we believe you have the potential to become a genuine rival. In time. With our resources." She gestured at the black jade slip. "That's an investment in your future. And in the future of your enemy."

"And who exactly is 'we'?"

Xue Feng's smile remained, but her eyes went hard. "That's not something I can share. Not yet. But I can tell you this: we have no love for the Celestial Sword Sect. And we believe you're the best weapon to use against them."

Weapon. The word stung.

"You want to use me."

"I want to help you. The motivation doesn't matter." Xue Feng stood up, smoothing her robes. "Think about it. Huo's offer gives you resources but takes your freedom. My offer gives you both. The only price is your continued opposition to the sect that wronged you."

She turned to leave, then paused.

"Oh, and one more thing. Huo's 'other party'? The sect he's planning to fund if you refuse? It's the Celestial Sword Sect's newest subsidiary. They're planning to enter the mortal market directly." She looked over her shoulder, her eyes gleaming. "You have until dawn. Choose wisely."

And then she was gone, disappearing into the evening crowd like a ghost.

The three of them sat frozen, the two jade slips sitting on the table like poisoned fruit.

"Boss," Li Chen finally said, his voice hoarse. "What the hell have we gotten into?"

Ren picked up the black jade slip. It was cold, almost unnaturally so, and it seemed to pulse with a dark energy.

"I don't know," he said quietly. "But I think we just became a pawn in a much bigger game."

Mei grabbed his wrist. "Ren, we can't trust her. We don't even know who she works for."

"And we can't trust Huo. He literally wants to own us."

"So what do we do?"

Ren looked at the two jade slips. Fifty thousand for his freedom. A hundred thousand for his soul.

"I have an idea."

He stood up, pocketing both slips.

"It's stupid. It's dangerous. And it's probably going to get us killed."

Li Chen sighed. "So it's Tuesday."

"Exactly." Ren headed for the door. "We're going to play them against each other. And we're going to come out of this with everything we want."

"Or nothing," Mei muttered.

"Or nothing." Ren smiled, the grin of a man who had nothing left to lose. "But if I'm going down, I'm taking them both with me."

As they stepped out of the tea house, the sun was setting, painting the sky in shades of blood and gold.

Dawn was coming.

And Ren had a decision to make.

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER_18: The Mortal Price

Ren watched Consortium Master Huo's face for any hint of reaction. The man's expression remained perfectly neutral, like a still lake hiding whatever lurked beneath.

"Bold words," Huo said, steepling his fingers. "You want my investment. But you're offering me something intangible — potential, vision, a dream. Dreams don't pay dividends, Ren Wei."

"Dreams build empires," Ren countered. "The Celestial Sword Sect started as a single swordsman with a broken blade. The Cloud Ascension Pavilion began as a cave with a leaking roof. Every great sect in history was once a 'minimum viable product.'"

Huo's smile widened, but it didn't reach his eyes. "You've done your research. I appreciate that. But let me ask you something more practical." He leaned forward. "Your sect has three disciples. One drunk, one gambler, one chef. Your temple is crumbling. Your total assets wouldn't buy me dinner at a decent establishment. Why should I trust you with my spirit stones?"

This was the moment. Ren had prepared for it.

"Because you're not just investing in a sect," Ren said, reaching into his robe. "You're investing in a *system*." He pulled out a scroll and unrolled it across Huo's desk. "This is our projection for the next three years. We'll start with pill distribution — we've already secured a partnership with a mortal apothecary who's been producing low-grade pills for years. No spiritual energy required, but effective enough for the early stages of cultivation."

Huo's eyes scanned the document. "Interesting. But mortal production has quality issues."

"We've solved that." Ren gestured to the next section. "My formation expert has developed a quality-control array that can be integrated into any production line. It filters impurities at the formation level, not the cultivation level. It's cheaper, faster, and more reliable than any spiritual refining method currently in use."

Huo's eyebrow rose a fraction. "And you're just showing me this?"

"I'm showing you what you get if you invest. If you don't..." Ren shrugged. "There are other consortiums."

The silence stretched. Ren could hear his own heartbeat.

Then Huo laughed. It was a warm sound, genuine, that transformed his scholarly face into something almost friendly. "You've got balls, kid. I'll give you that. Most people who walk through that door are begging. You're negotiating."

"That's because I have something worth negotiating for."
