"Maybe." Huo sat back, his smile fading. "But I have concerns. Your projection assumes you can scale your production without interference from established powers. The Celestial Sword Sect alone has a vested interest in seeing you fail. They don't tolerate competition."

"We're not competing with them. We're targeting a different market — the mortal cultivators, the ones who can't afford the elite sects. That's a market they've ignored for centuries."

"And when they notice you?"

Ren met his eyes. "Then we'll have grown enough to defend ourselves. Or we'll fail trying. That's the nature of startups."

Huo was silent for a long moment. Then he reached into his desk and pulled out a contract, already written, the seal already stamped.

"I had this prepared before you arrived," he said, sliding it across the desk. "As a formality. But now I'm actually considering it."

Ren took the contract. His hands were steady, even though his heart was pounding. The terms were fair — shockingly fair. Investment in exchange for twenty percent equity. No board seats. No control over operations. Just funding and connections.

"Twenty percent?" Ren asked. "That's generous."

"It's strategic. I want to see what you can build." Huo's eyes sharpened. "But I have one condition. A personal one."

Ren's stomach tightened. There it was. There was always a catch.

"Name it."

"I want you to take on a student. My daughter."

Ren blinked. "Your... daughter?"

"She's seventeen. Gifted in formation theory, but she refuses to join any traditional sect. Says they're 'soul-crushing bureaucracies.'" Huo's lips quirked. "I don't know where she gets that from. But she's interested in what you're doing. She wants to learn."

Ren's mind raced. Taking on a student from a powerful consortium could be a blessing or a curse. A spy? A liability? Or a genuine opportunity?

"May I meet her first?" Ren asked.

"Of course. She's waiting outside."

Huo clapped his hands. The door opened, and a young woman walked in.

She was nothing like Ren expected. Instead of silk robes and elegant jewelry, she wore simple traveling clothes — practical, unadorned. Her hair was pulled back in a messy bun, and she carried a leather satchel stuffed with scrolls. Her eyes were sharp, intelligent, and judging him.

"So you're the startup cultivator," she said, her voice flat. "You look poor."

"Hu—" Ren started, then caught himself. "I prefer 'frugal.'"

"Frugal means you choose not to spend. Your robes have patches. That's not a choice." She walked around him, inspecting him like a bug. "My father says you're building something new. Prove it."

"Prove it? I don't even know your—"

"Name's Huo Ling. I'm a formation specialist. Third-level, self-taught. I've memorized sixty-seven formation theories and designed twelve original arrays. None of them work yet, but that's because I'm missing practical experience." She stopped in front of him, arms crossed. "You have practical experience. I want it. In exchange, I'll give you the resources to make your little startup work."

Ren looked at Huo. The consortium master was watching with an amused expression, clearly enjoying the show.

"She's... direct," Ren said.

"She gets it from her mother."

"I get it from myself," Huo Ling said. "So. Do we have a deal, or do I go find some other failed cultivator to mentor me?"

Ren laughed despite himself. "You know, most people try to be charming when they ask for a favor."

"Charm is manipulation. I prefer honesty." She stuck out her hand. "Deal?"

Ren took her hand. Her grip was firm, confident. "Deal."

Huo nodded, satisfied. "Excellent. The contract will be finalized by tomorrow morning. I'll transfer the initial investment to your sect's account by noon. And Ling..." He turned to his daughter. "Behave yourself."

"I always behave myself. It's other people who can't handle it."

Ren left the study with a new investor, a new disciple, and a growing sense that he had just made a deal with forces he didn't fully understand.

---

The walk back to the temple was quiet. Mei and Li Chen flanked him, both suspiciously silent.

"So," Mei finally said, "you got the investment."

"Twenty thousand spirit stones. Upfront."

"And a new disciple."

"Huo Ling. Formation specialist."

Li Chen snorted. "She looked like she could stab a man with a formation needle and not even blink."

"She's passionate."

"She's terrifying."

Ren sighed. "She's an opportunity. Her father controls the Azure Pond's southern trade routes. That's access to markets we couldn't dream of reaching on our own."

"And if she's a spy?"

"Then we'll deal with it." Ren's voice hardened. "We've survived worse. Remember, we started with nothing. A few spies won't break us."

They reached the temple gates. The crumbling walls looked even worse in the fading light, but to Ren, they looked like home.

Then he saw the figure standing by the entrance.

It was a woman in white robes, her face obscured by a veil. She held a jade token that glowed with the unmistakable mark of the Celestial Sword Sect.

"Ren Wei," she said, her voice cold. "Elder Song requests your presence. Immediately."

Ren's blood ran cold. The Celestial Sword Sect never sent messengers. They sent enforcers.

"What does Elder Song want?"

"That is between you and the elder. You will come with me. Now."

Mei stepped forward. "He's not going anywhere—"

"It's fine." Ren put a hand on her arm. "If the Celestial Sword Sect wanted me dead, they wouldn't send a messenger. They'd send a sword."

The veiled woman said nothing.

"Take care of the temple," Ren said to Mei and Li Chen. "I'll be back."

He walked toward the veiled woman, his heart pounding.

Behind him, he heard Huo Ling's voice: "Well, this is already more interesting than I expected."

Ren didn't answer. He was too busy wondering if he had just signed his own death warrant.

The veiled woman led him through the city streets, past the mortal districts, past the Jade District, to a secluded pavilion that Ren had never seen before. It was small, unassuming, surrounded by a garden of black flowers.

Elder Song sat on a stone bench, his ancient face weathered by centuries of cultivation. He looked up as Ren approached, his eyes unreadable.

"Ren Wei," he said. "Sit."

Ren sat on the bench opposite him. The veiled woman disappeared into the shadows.

"I'm surprised you came," Elder Song said.

"You sent for me. I came."

"Bold. Foolish, but bold." Elder Song picked a black flower, examining it. "Do you know why I called you here?"

"To threaten me. To warn me. To remind me of my place."

Elder Song smiled — a thin, cold thing. "All of those. And none of those." He crushed the flower in his hand. "I called you here because I have a proposal."

Ren's eyebrows rose. "A proposal?"

"You're building something. Something that threatens the established order. The Celestial Sword Sect cannot allow that to continue."

"Then why aren't you killing me?"

"Because killing you would make you a martyr. And martyrs inspire movements." Elder Song's eyes sharpened. "Instead, I'm offering you a choice. Join us. Bring your sect under the Celestial Sword Sect's banner. You'll have resources, protection, legitimacy. Everything you've been fighting for."

Ren stared at him. "You're offering me a merger."

"I'm offering you survival."

"And if I refuse?"

Elder Song's smile didn't waver. "Then I'll destroy everything you've built. Your temple. Your disciples. Your reputation. I'll make sure the mortal world forgets you ever existed."

The silence stretched between them, broken only by the rustle of black flowers in the wind.

Ren stood up.

"I appreciate the offer," he said, his voice steady. "But I'm going to have to decline."

Elder Song's eyes flickered. "You're making a mistake."

"Maybe." Ren turned to leave. "But it's my mistake to make."

He walked away, feeling the elder's gaze on his back like a sword point.

Behind him, Elder Song's voice carried through the garden: "You have three days, Ren Wei. Three days to reconsider. After that, I'll assume your answer is final."

Ren didn't look back.

He walked through the city streets, past the mortal districts, back to his crumbling temple. Mei and Li Chen were waiting at the gates, their faces pale.

Huo Ling was sitting on the wall, her expression thoughtful.

"So," she said, "what did the big bad sect want?"

Ren looked at her, then at his disciples, then at the temple that was somehow still standing.

"We have three days," he said. "Three days to prepare for war."

Mei's face hardened. Li

title: The Offer That Breaks Everything
content:
Consortium Master Huo studied Ren for a long moment, his fingers steepled, his expression giving nothing away. The silence stretched, filled only by the soft hum of formations embedded in the walls.

"Your proposal is interesting," Huo finally said. "But I have a counter-offer."

Ren kept his face neutral. "I'm listening."

"Fifty thousand spirit stones. Direct investment. No interest for the first year."

Ren's heart stopped. That was more than he'd dreamed. Enough to rebuild the temple, buy proper cultivation resources, pay his disciples a salary that might actually keep them from leaving.

"What's the catch?"

Huo smiled. It wasn't a pleasant smile. "I want seventy percent equity in New Dao Sect."

The words hit Ren like a physical blow. Seventy percent. He'd be a figurehead in his own sect. Every major decision would require Huo's approval. Every innovation, every expansion, every disciple they accepted — all controlled by a man who saw cultivation sects as portfolio assets.

"That's not a partnership," Ren said, his voice cold. "That's a takeover."

"It's a fair price for a sect with no assets, no reputation, and three misfit disciples who couldn't get hired anywhere else." Huo leaned back, his smile widening. "Let me be frank, Ren Wei. I've done my research. Your 'sword master' is a drunk who was expelled from three sects for insubordination. Your formation expert is banned from every gambling house in the Eastern Province. And your chef? She cultivates by accident, which means she has no control over her advancement. You're not building a sect. You're running a charity for failures."

Ren's hands clenched under the desk. Every word was true. Every word made him want to punch the man in his perfect, condescending face.

"But here's the thing," Huo continued. "I see potential in chaos. Your disciples are misfits, yes, but misfits think differently. They solve problems in unconventional ways. That's valuable. Under proper management, New Dao Sect could become something interesting."

"Under *your* management."

"Under *our* management." Huo spread his hands. "Think of it as a partnership of equals. I provide resources, connections, protection from the major sects who will undoubtedly try to crush you. You provide the vision and the execution. Seventy-thirty is standard in the mortal business world."

"We're not mortals."

"No. You're cultivators who can't afford to pay your rent." Huo's eyes hardened. "This is my only offer. Take it, or leave this room and never return."

Ren stood. His legs felt weak, but he forced himself to stand straight. "I need to discuss this with my disciples."

"You have one hour."

---

The waiting area felt smaller than before. Mei and Li Chen looked up as Ren emerged, their expressions shifting from hope to concern when they saw his face.

"Well?" Mei asked.

"Not here." Ren grabbed both of them by the sleeves and pulled them out of the pavilion, past the armed guards, into a narrow alley between two merchant houses. "We need to talk."

He explained the offer. Mei's face went through twelve different expressions in three seconds, settling on fury. Li Chen just laughed, a bitter, hollow sound.

"He wants to own us," Mei said. "Like livestock."

"Seventy percent," Li Chen repeated. "That's not an investment. That's indentured servitude."

"I know." Ren ran his hands through his hair. "But fifty thousand spirit stones. Do you understand what we could do with that? We could actually *compete*. We could buy proper cultivation manuals, hire real guards, build a library, establish actual infrastructure—"

"And none of it would be ours," Mei interrupted. "Every brick, every scroll, every bowl of soup we serve — it would all belong to him. We'd be employees in our own sect."

"We're already barely making rent."

"Then we find another way."

"There is no other way!" Ren's voice cracked. He took a breath, forcing himself to calm down. "The Celestial Sword Sect has already started spreading rumors about us. Two potential disciples have withdrawn their applications because they heard we were 'a front for a demonic cultivation ring.' The Azure Pond Consortium is the only group willing to even talk to us."

Li Chen pulled a flask from his robes and took a long drink. "So we sell our souls, or we die."

"Pretty much."

Mei crossed her arms. "What does your gut say?"

Ren closed his eyes. His gut said this was a trap. His gut said Huo was the kind of man who smiled while he stabbed you in the back. His gut said taking this deal would destroy everything he was trying to build.

But his gut also said they were three desperate people with a crumbling temple and no future.

"I don't know," he admitted.

The alley fell silent. A cat wandered past, looked at them with disdain, and continued on its way.

Then Li Chen spoke, his voice unusually sober. "What if we took the deal, but we made sure he couldn't actually control us?"

Mei raised an eyebrow. "How?"

"Structure." Li Chen's eyes glittered with something that might have been intelligence or might have been alcohol. "He wants seventy percent equity, fine. But equity doesn't mean operational control. We structure the sect so that major decisions require a supermajority vote. We write the founding charter so that certain things — disciple admission, cultivation methods, sect principles — are non-negotiable. We give him money, not power."

Ren stared at him. "That's... actually brilliant."

"I have my moments. Usually right before I pass out."

Mei nodded slowly. "It could work. But it would require a lawyer. A good one. And we'd need to move fast — once he signs, the terms are locked."

"Do we know any lawyers?"

"I know a formation expert who used to forge contracts for the gambling dens," Li Chen said. "She's currently in prison, but that's actually more convenient — she can't run away before we finish negotiating."

Ren looked at his two disciples — his misfits, his failures, his family. "Let's go talk to a prisoner."

---

The city jail was less impressive than the Azure Pavilion. Stone walls, iron bars, the smell of unwashed bodies and despair. A bored guard led them to a cell in the back, where a woman with sharp features and sharper eyes sat cross-legged, tracing formations in the dirt floor.

"Li Chen," she said without looking up. "I was wondering when you'd show up. Come to collect on that debt?"

"Not today, Zhao. I need your brain."

Zhao finally looked up, her gaze sweeping over Ren and Mei. "Who are they?"

"Ren Wei, founder of New Dao Sect," Ren said. "We need a contract written. Something that protects our interests while making an investor think he's getting everything he wants."

Zhao's eyes lit up. "I like that. That's the kind of job that gets me out of this cell." She stood, brushing dirt from her robes. "What's the deal?"

Ren explained. Zhao listened, occasionally nodding, occasionally frowning. When he finished, she was silent for a long moment.

"It's doable," she finally said. "But it's dangerous. If Huo finds out you've structured the deal to limit his control, he'll have you killed. The Azure Pond Consortium doesn't like being outmaneuvered."

"Can you write it so he doesn't find out?"

Zhao smiled. It was not a reassuring smile. "I can write it so he thinks he's winning. But he'll figure it out eventually. The question is whether you'll be strong enough to survive when he does."

"We'll cross that bridge when we come to it."

"That's how people die, Ren Wei." Zhao held out her hand. "Get me out of here. I'll write your contract. But when Huo comes for you, don't say I didn't warn you."

---

Getting Zhao released required bribing three guards, forging a release order, and promising the warden a cut of the sect's future profits. By the time they returned to the Azure Pavilion, Ren had spent almost all of their remaining savings and was operating on pure desperation.

Huo was waiting in his study, patience personified.

"One hour and fifteen minutes," he said. "I was beginning to think you'd fled."

"Just needed to consult with my team." Ren sat down, his heart pounding. "We accept your offer. With conditions."

Huo's smile flickered. "Conditions."

"Seventy percent equity, yes. But the sect charter will be written to protect our core principles. Disciple admission, cultivation methods, and sect leadership succession require a supermajority vote. You get the money, you get the returns, but you don't get to change who we are."

Huo's eyes narrowed. "You've been talking to someone clever."

"I have no idea what you're talking about."

The silence stretched. Ren could feel the spiritual pressure building, Huo's cultivation base pressing against him like a physical weight. He didn't flinch.

Finally, Huo laughed.

"Fine," he said. "I'll accept your terms. But know this, Ren Wei — clever contracts only protect you as long as you have the power to enforce them. And right now, you have nothing."

He pulled a scroll from his desk, unrolled it, and began to write.

Ren watched, his hands steady, his heart racing.

*This is it,* he thought. *We're either building something real, or we're signing our own death warrant.*

The door to the study burst open.

A guard stumbled in, his face pale. "Consortium Master! There's been an incident."

Huo looked up, annoyed. "What kind of incident?"

"The Celestial Sword Sect. They've just announced a bounty. On all members of the New Dao Sect."

Ren's blood ran cold.

"Dead or alive."

title: Chapter 20
content:
CHAPTER 20: The Investor Who Already Owned Everything

Ren sat in Consortium Master Huo's study, the silence stretching between them like a blade.

Huo's smile never wavered. It was the kind of smile that made Ren want to check his pockets for missing belongings. The man leaned back in his chair, steepling his fingers, his gaze sharp despite those soft, scholarly features.

"Your proposal is ambitious," Huo said finally. "I'll give you that. But ambition without foundation is just expensive daydreaming. Tell me, Ren Wei — what makes you think your 'startup sect' can survive when the Celestial Sword Sect could crush you before breakfast?"

Ren had prepared for this question. He'd rehearsed answers in front of the mirror until his reflection looked bored.

"Scale," Ren said. "The Celestial Sword Sect is a mountain. Large, powerful, and impossible to move quickly. New Dao Sect is a river. We flow around obstacles, adapt to terrain, and carve our own path. They can't crush what they can't catch."

Huo chuckled. "Poetic. But rivers can be damned, diverted, or dried up. What's your plan when they decide to build a dam?"

"Then we become rain."

The consortium master's eyebrow twitched. It was the first genuine reaction Ren had seen from him. Good. That meant he was paying attention.

"I see you've thought about this," Huo said. "But let me ask you a more practical question. Your proposal mentions 'network effects' and 'scalable cultivation resources.' What are you actually *selling*?"

Ren reached into his robe and pulled out a jade slip, placing it on the obsidian desk. "This."

Huo picked it up, frowning. "A formation array?"

"Not just any formation. A spirit-gathering array optimized for low-quality spiritual veins. Most sects build their arrays to extract maximum qi from high-grade veins. But what about the sects that don't have high-grade veins? The minor families? The independent cultivators?"

Huo's frown deepened. "There's no profit in that market. They have no money."

"They have *potential*," Ren said. "There are thousands of minor cultivation families across the empire. Each one has at least one child with decent talent. But they can't afford the resources to train them. So those children either join major sects as outer disciples — where they're exploited — or they give up cultivation entirely."

"And your array changes this how?"

"It boosts spirit gathering efficiency by three hundred percent on low-grade veins. I've tested it. The conversion rate isn't as high as a premium array on a premium vein, but the *volume* of potential users is massive. Ten thousand mediocre cultivators are worth more than one hundred geniuses."

Huo set the jade slip down. His expression had shifted from polite skepticism to something sharper. Predatory.

"You've tested it? Where?"

"On our temple's vein. It was classified as 'negligible' by the Imperial Survey Office. After three months with my array, it produces enough qi to support five Qi Condensation cultivators simultaneously."

"Show me."

Ren blinked. "Excuse me?"

"Show me," Huo repeated. "I don't invest based on proposals. I invest based on results. Take me to your temple. Let me see this array in action. If it works as you claim, I'll fund your first expansion phase."

Ren's heart raced. This was happening faster than he'd anticipated. He'd expected negotiations, counter-offers, weeks of back-and-forth. Not an immediate field verification.

"That's... unusual for a first meeting," Ren said carefully.

"I'm an unusual investor." Huo stood, smoothing his robes. "I don't like wasting time. If you're legitimate, I want to see it. If you're a fraud, I want to know immediately so I can move on to the next opportunity."

*And if I'm a fraud, you'll probably have me killed*, Ren thought. But he didn't say that.

"Fine. Tomorrow at dawn."

"Excellent." Huo smiled that empty smile again. "I'll bring my evaluation team. Three experts in formations, cultivation, and financial auditing. If everything checks out, we'll sign the investment contract before sunset."

Ren extended his hand. "Deal."

Huo stared at his hand like it was a dead fish. "What are you doing?"

"Shaking hands. It's a mortal custom. Seals agreements."

"I prefer contracts written in blood on spirit parchment, but very well." Huo grasped Ren's hand briefly, his grip cold and firm. "I'll see you tomorrow, Sect Master Ren."

---

When Ren stepped out of the study, Mei was waiting with barely contained fury.

"What did you agree to?" she hissed, grabbing his arm and pulling him into a corner.

"Just a field verification. He wants to see the temple."

"Ren. Ren, look at me." Mei's eyes were wild. "Do you know who Consortium Master Huo actually is?"

"An investor with deep pockets and a suspicious nature?"

"No. He's the *former patriarch of the Azure Pond Consortium's rival faction*. He was overthrown in a coup ten years ago and supposedly went into hiding. And now he's running the entire operation again under a different name."

Ren's blood went cold. "How do you know this?"

"I have contacts. Li Chen's contacts, actually. He found out while drinking with some old information brokers. Huo isn't just an investor — he's a *fixer*. He takes over failing organizations, strips them for parts, and sells what's left. He's called 'The Butcher of Jade Market' because of what he did to a merchant family that refused his terms."

"And you're telling me this *now*?"

"You didn't give me time! You walked in there like a lamb to slaughter!"

Ren pressed his palms against his eyes. This changed everything. Huo wasn't looking for a partnership. He was looking for a target. The temple, the array, the startup — all of it would be evaluated not for investment, but for *acquisition*.

"I need to warn Li Chen and the others," Ren said.

"No time. We need a counter-strategy." Mei's mind was already racing, her fingers tapping against her thigh. "If Huo sees the temple as it is, he'll try to take it. But if we show him something he *wants* more than he wants to steal..."

"Like what?"

"A partnership with actual equity. Give him a percentage of future profits in exchange for protection from other predators. Make him see us as an asset to be nurtured, not harvested."

Ren shook his head. "That's just delaying the inevitable. Once he's inside our operation, he'll find every weakness and exploit it."

"Then we don't let him find weaknesses."

"How? We have a crumbling temple, three misfit disciples, and a business plan written in blood. That's basically an invitation to be exploited."

Mei grabbed his shoulders. "Then we build something he can't exploit. We show him the *vision*, not the reality. We sell him on the future, not the present."

Ren stared at her. "You want to lie to a man called 'The Butcher of Jade Market.'"

"I want to *strategically present information*," Mei said, her eyes burning with manic energy. "There's a difference."

---

They spent the rest of the day preparing. Li Chen was dispatched to gather more intelligence on Huo's past acquisitions. Xiao Bao was told to clean the temple until it sparkled — or at least until it didn't look like it was about to collapse. Old Man Zhang was given strict instructions to stay sober until the meeting was over.

"You're asking the impossible," Zhang muttered.

"I'm asking for four hours. Just four hours without alcohol."

"Fine. But I'm billing you for the headache."

By evening, the temple was as presentable as it was going to get. Ren stood on the main altar platform, staring at the spirit-gathering array he'd installed. It was crude, made from salvaged materials and cheap spirit stones, but it worked. That much was true.

The problem was everything *around* it.

The leaky roof. The cracked walls. The patches of moss growing in corners where qi had condensed into harmless but unsightly spiritual mold.

"This is a disaster," Mei said, appearing beside him. "He's going to walk in and laugh."

"Then we don't let him walk in."

"What?"

Ren turned to her, a plan forming in his mind. "We meet him halfway. There's that abandoned smithy on the main road into town. We'll set up a temporary reception there. Show him the array in a controlled environment. Keep him away from the temple entirely."

Mei's eyes widened. "That's... actually not stupid."

"Thank you."

"It's still stupid. But less stupid than the alternative."

They worked through the night, transporting a section of the array to the smithy, setting up demonstration materials, and rehearsing their pitch. By dawn, Ren was exhausted but ready.

Huo arrived with three experts as promised. The formation master was an old woman with sharp eyes and sharper criticism. The cultivation expert was a scarred man who looked like he'd rather be fighting. The financial auditor was a young woman with a ledger and a smile that promised pain.

"Impressive setup," Huo said, looking around the smithy. "Though I notice you chose a neutral location. Why not the temple?"

Ren smiled, forcing calm into his voice. "The temple is our operational headquarters. I prefer to keep some mysteries until the contract is signed."

Huo's eyes narrowed. "How many mysteries, I wonder."

"Only the pleasant ones."

The demonstration went smoothly. The array worked exactly as Ren claimed. The formation master was grudgingly impressed. The cultivation expert confirmed the qi output was three times higher than standard arrays on equivalent veins. The financial auditor took notes with the enthusiasm of a predator cataloging prey.

By noon, Huo was smiling. *Actually* smiling.

"Ren Wei," he said, "I think we can do business."

"Excellent."

"However..." Huo pulled a scroll from his robe, unrolling it on the smithy's workbench. "I've prepared a contract. I'd like you to sign it before we proceed."

Ren read the contract. His blood ran cold.

The document didn't offer investment. It offered *acquisition*. Huo's consortium would take seventy percent ownership of New Dao Sect. All intellectual property, including the array design, would transfer to Azure Pond. Ren would be retained as "operational manager" — a fancy title for a figurehead.

And buried in the fine print: if Ren refused to sign, the consortium would consider the proposal "a fraudulent misrepresentation of assets" and pursue legal action for damages.

"You planned this from the beginning," Ren said, his voice flat.

"I planned for *every* outcome," Huo replied, his smile widening. "Your array is valuable. Your 'startup' concept is novel. But you're a nobody from a broken sect with three disciples and a collapsing temple. You have no leverage. You have no protection. You have nothing I can't take."

Mei was shaking beside him. Li Chen's hand was inching toward his sword.

"Sign the contract, Ren Wei," Huo said, his voice soft as silk. "Or I'll make sure your 'startup' never gets another investor in the entire Jade Empire."

Ren looked at the contract. Then at his disciples. Then at the array he'd built with his own hands, from scraps and desperation and stubborn hope.

He picked up the brush.

And broke it in half.

"Huo, I reject your offer."

The consortium master's smile vanished. "You're making a mistake."

"No. I'm making a statement." Ren straightened his robes.
